summary: Bilberry extract is rich in anthocyanins and polyphenolic compounds with antioxidant properties, traditionally used to support eye health and vascular function. Evidence supports benefits for vision and microcirculation, though most studies use standardized extracts with defined anthocyanin content.
evidence_notes: Anthocyanin-rich eye support

dosage.special_populations: Night driving, eye strain

name: BioCell Collagen®
summary: BioCell Collagen® is a proprietary hydrolyzed collagen extract from chicken sternal cartilage containing collagen peptides, hyaluronic acid, and chondroitin. It is designed to support joint, skin, and connective tissue health.
mechanism_of_action: Provides bioavailable collagen peptides that may accumulate in skin and cartilage
Contains endogenous hyaluronic acid and chondroitin for joint lubrication
May stimulate collagen and glycosaminoglycan synthesis
evidence_notes: Multiple randomized controlled trials demonstrate improvements in joint comfort, skin hydration, and skin elasticity. Evidence is solid for the proprietary form, though effect sizes are moderate. More research on long-term outcomes is needed.

dosage.typical_range: 1,000–2,000 mg daily (10 mg hyaluronic acid, 100 mg chondroitin per serving in standard formulation)
dosage.timing: Can be taken anytime; morning dosing with breakfast is common
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with vitamin C-containing foods may enhance collagen synthesis
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically observed after 4–12 weeks of consistent use; longer duration (8–12 weeks) associated with more pronounced skin and joint improvements in studies
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy and lactation based on collagen safety profile, but consult healthcare provider. No age restrictions noted.

contraindications: Egg allergy or poultry sensitivity (derived from chicken cartilage)
Shellfish allergy concern if processing involves shellfish-derived ingredients (verify supplier)

supplements: Vitamin C: may enhance collagen synthesis
Hyaluronic acid: complementary effect on skin hydration

name: Bioenergetic Cellular Complex
summary: Proprietary complex containing CoQ10 and PQQ in phospholipid form supporting mitochondrial function and cellular energy production.
evidence_notes: Contains well-researched CoQ10 and PQQ

dosage.special_populations: Well tolerated across populations

name: BioPerine (Black Pepper Extract)
summary: Patented black pepper extract containing piperine. Enhances absorption of many supplements including curcumin, CoQ10, and vitamins.
evidence_notes: Well-established absorption enhancer for many compounds

dosage.special_populations: Anyone taking curcumin, CoQ10, etc.

medications: Increases absorption of MANY drugs - caution

name: BioPerine® (Black Pepper Extract)
summary: BioPerine® is a standardized black pepper fruit extract containing piperine, primarily used as a bioenhancer to increase the absorption and bioavailability of other nutrients and compounds. Clinical evidence supports its ability to enhance nutrient absorption, though its standalone health benefits are limited.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibition of intestinal glucuronidation and sulfation (Phase II metabolism)
Enhanced gastrointestinal absorption of co-administered compounds
evidence_notes: Good evidence for bioenhancement function in human studies; multiple RCTs demonstrate increased absorption of curcumin, beta-carotene, and other nutrients. Limited evidence for standalone health benefits; most research focuses on its role as an adjuvant.

dosage.timing: With meals, ideally with other supplements/nutrients
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to enhance absorption of co-ingredients
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; typically used continuously as part of supplement formulations
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe; pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider

common_side_effects: Rare: mild gastrointestinal upset at very high doses
contraindications: Those taking medications requiring careful dose timing (may enhance absorption)

medications: May enhance absorption of certain medications (consult healthcare provider)
Potential interaction with drugs metabolized via Phase II pathways
supplements: Enhances uptake of other fat-soluble and hydrophobic compounds

name: Biotin
summary: A water-soluble B vitamin essential for hair, skin, and nail health and serves as a coenzyme in amino acid and glucose metabolism. D-biotin is the naturally active form.
evidence_notes: Popular, but only helps if deficient

dosage.special_populations: Hair loss (if deficient), brittle nails

name: Birch Polypore
summary: A wood-decay fungus traditionally used in Northern European and Asian folk medicine, often included in mushroom blends for immune support. Modern research on bioactive compounds is limited and largely preliminary.
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical data. Traditional use in European herbalism but minimal rigorous RCT evidence. Mycelia on rice biomass form has not been specifically studied in humans.

dosage.typical_range: 500-1500 mg daily (as part of mushroom blends)
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in immunocompromised individuals without medical supervision; pregnancy/lactation data unavailable

contraindications: Possible cross-reactivity in individuals with mold allergies

name: Bitter Melon Extract
summary: Traditional anti-diabetic plant with multiple blood sugar-lowering compounds.
evidence_notes: Traditional blood sugar support

dosage.special_populations: Blood sugar support, traditional medicine preference

supplements: Works with other blood sugar support

name: Black Cherry Extract (Prunus serotina)
summary: Black cherry extract is rich in anthocyanins and polyphenols with preliminary evidence supporting benefits for exercise recovery, sleep quality, and inflammation management. Most clinical support exists for tart cherry varieties, though black cherry shares similar phytochemical profiles.
mechanism_of_action: Uric acid reduction potentially benefiting gout management
evidence_notes: Black cherry extract itself has limited direct RCT evidence. Related tart cherry research (Montmorency variety) shows moderate evidence for recovery and sleep, but black cherry (Prunus serotina) clinical data is sparse. Grade reflects preliminary support rather than strong clinical confirmation.

dosage.typical_range: 300–600 mg daily (equivalent to 6,000–12,000 mg whole fruit at 20:1 concentration)
dosage.timing: Evening for sleep support; post-exercise for recovery support
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with food may reduce potential stomach upset
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically require 2–4 weeks of consistent use; some sleep studies show acute benefit within 1–2 hours
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; those with cyanide sensitivity (rare) should avoid

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal upset possible at high doses
contraindications: Warfarin or anticoagulant use (high anthocyanin content may potentiate effect; medical supervision recommended)

medications: Warfarin and other anticoagulants (possible additive effect; monitor INR)
supplements: Other high-anthocyanin supplements may have cumulative antioxidant effects

name: Black Cohosh
summary: Black cohosh is a traditional herbal remedy primarily used for menopausal symptom relief, with moderate clinical evidence supporting its efficacy for hot flashes and night sweats. Its mechanisms likely involve phytoestrogen activity and serotonergic pathways, though the active constituents remain incompletely characterized.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for hot flashes and menopause symptoms

dosage.special_populations: Menopausal and perimenopausal women

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Monitor liver function, rare hepatotoxicity reported

supplements: Often combined with St. John's Wort

name: Black Cumin Seed Oil
summary: Black cumin seed oil is extracted from Nigella sativa seeds and contains bioactive compounds including thymoquinone, with preliminary evidence supporting immune and inflammatory support. Human clinical data remain limited, though traditional use is well-established in Middle Eastern and South Asian medicine.
mechanism_of_action: Thymoquinone and other constituents may modulate immune cytokines
evidence_notes: Moderate preliminary evidence from animal and in vitro studies; limited well-controlled human RCTs. Most human studies are small and lack robust design. Traditional use is extensive but does not substitute for clinical evidence.

dosage.with_food: Recommended to enhance absorption and reduce gastrointestinal upset
dosage.duration_notes: Studies typically span 8–12 weeks; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/nursing: insufficient safety data; use with caution or avoid. Pediatric use not well-established.

contraindications: Known allergy to Nigella sativa or related plants
Concurrent use with immunosuppressant medications (potential interaction)

medications: May potentiate blood pressure-lowering agents (caution advised)
Potential interaction with anticoagulants (limited evidence; monitor)
supplements: Additive effects with other immune-modulating herbs (e.g., echinacea, elderberry)

name: Black Garlic Extract
summary: Black garlic is raw garlic that has been aged through a heating and fermentation process, resulting in a darker color and purported concentration of antioxidant compounds including S-allylcysteine and polyphenols. Limited clinical evidence supports health claims, though in vitro and animal studies suggest potential antioxidant and cardiovascular benefits.
mechanism_of_action: Increased antioxidant compounds (S-allylcysteine, polyphenols) compared to raw garlic
evidence_notes: Most human evidence is limited or preliminary. Some small clinical studies show modest effects on cardiovascular markers, but the body of clinical evidence is not robust. In vitro and animal data suggest antioxidant potential, but human RCT data is scarce and inconsistent.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may cause less digestive upset than raw garlic when consumed with food
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used daily for chronic health support; optimal duration unknown
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; those on anticoagulants should monitor intake

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal upset (less common than raw garlic)
Body odor (less pronounced than raw garlic)
contraindications: Bleeding disorders (garlic has mild anticoagulant properties)
Concurrent use of anticoagulant medications (consult healthcare provider)

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel) - potential additive effects
Diabetes medications - may have mild effects on blood glucose
supplements: Other anticoagulant herbs (ginkgo, ginger, turmeric) - theoretical additive effects

name: Black Ginger Extract
summary: An Asian rhizome extract rich in methoxyflavones with emerging research suggesting potential benefits for athletic performance, blood flow, and energy metabolism. Human evidence remains limited and mostly from Asian studies.
evidence_notes: Preliminary human RCTs show modest benefits for endurance performance and blood flow parameters. Most studies small (n=20-40) and from Asian institutions. Limited long-term safety data. Effect sizes modest. Requires larger, independent replication.

dosage.timing: Morning or pre-workout; some evidence suggests timing with exercise
dosage.with_food: May be better absorbed with dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits in studies typically observed after 2-4 weeks; may require 4-8 weeks for maximal effect
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and children not established; caution in blood clotting disorders

contraindications: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulation therapy (theoretical risk)

medications: Potential interaction with blood thinners (anticoagulants/antiplatelets) — use with caution
May interact with antidiabetic medications (improved insulin sensitivity reported)
supplements: Caution combining with other NO-boosters or vasodilators at high doses

name: Black Maca Powder
summary: Black maca is a root vegetable from Peru traditionally used to support sexual function, fertility, and energy levels. Limited clinical evidence suggests potential benefits for sexual desire and sperm quality, though most studies are small and conducted in specific populations.
mechanism_of_action: Contains bioactive compounds (glucosinolates, alkaloids) that may influence hormonal signaling
Potential effects on nitric oxide production and vascular function
May support dopamine and serotonin pathways related to sexual function
evidence_notes: Limited RCT evidence, mostly from small studies in specific populations (infertile men, healthy volunteers). Black maca specifically has less research than generic maca. Results are mixed and effect sizes modest. Insufficient evidence for broad clinical claims.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with or without food; consistent daily dosing recommended
dosage.with_food: May be better tolerated with food; can be mixed into beverages or meals
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits may take 8-12 weeks of consistent use; most studies used durations of 8-24 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider before use

contraindications: History of thyroid disease (maca contains goitrogens in high amounts)
Hormone-sensitive conditions (breast cancer, prostate cancer) — use with caution
Pregnancy and lactation — insufficient safety data

medications: Blood pressure medications (maca may have mild BP effects)
supplements: Other adaptogenic herbs with hormonal effects

name: Black Pepper Extract (Piper nigrum) - Standardized to Piperine
summary: Piperine is the alkaloid compound in black pepper that enhances nutrient absorption by increasing intestinal permeability and hepatic metabolism. It is commonly used in supplements to boost the bioavailability of other ingredients.
mechanism_of_action: Increases intestinal permeability and tight junction modulation
Inhibits hepatic and intestinal metabolism (CYP3A4, P-glycoprotein)
Enhances nutrient transport across intestinal epithelium
evidence_notes: Well-documented bioavailability enhancement (20-30% increase in absorption of co-administered nutrients). Good safety profile and multiple human studies; most evidence is in food/nutrition context rather than disease treatment.

dosage.timing: With meals containing other nutrients to enhance absorption
dosage.with_food: Recommended — taken with fat-soluble nutrients and food
dosage.duration_notes: Effects on absorption are immediate; benefits accumulate with consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Reduce dosage or avoid in pregnancy/lactation; caution in individuals with gastric ulcers or GERD

common_side_effects: Rare: heartburn or stomach upset at high doses

medications: CYP3A4 substrates (e.g., certain statins, immunosuppressants) — may increase plasma levels
P-glycoprotein substrates (e.g., digoxin, talinolol) — may increase absorption and levels
Antidiabetic medications — may enhance glucose-lowering effects
NSAIDs — may increase GI irritation risk
supplements: Turmeric/curcumin (synergistic — piperine dramatically increases curcumin bioavailability)
CoQ10 (enhances absorption, as in this formulation)
Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K — enhanced absorption)

name: Black Rice Extract
summary: Black rice extract is rich in anthocyanins, particularly cyanidin 3-glucoside, which provide potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefits. It may support metabolic health and cardiovascular function.
evidence_notes: Black rice anthocyanins have shown promise in cell and animal studies for antioxidant activity. Human clinical data is limited. Cyanidin 3-glucoside is well-characterized but human efficacy data remain preliminary.

forms.form: Black rice seed extract (standardized to cyanidin 3-glucoside)

dosage.with_food: Take with fat-containing meals for enhanced anthocyanin absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Generally safe for long-term use; benefits optimized over weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; limited safety data in pregnancy

common_side_effects: Rare; may cause mild GI upset in sensitive individuals

name: Black Seed Oil (Nigella sativa)
summary: Traditional remedy with adaptogenic and cortisol-modulating properties.
evidence_notes: Traditional use with emerging research

dosage.special_populations: Immune support, stress

name: Black Walnut Hulls Extract
summary: Black walnut hulls are traditionally used in herbal medicine and contain juglone and polyphenols with antimicrobial and antioxidant properties. Limited clinical evidence supports most health claims, with most research conducted in vitro or in animal models.
mechanism_of_action: Juglone compound exhibits antimicrobial activity against bacteria and fungi
Polyphenol content may provide antioxidant effects
evidence_notes: Most evidence is from traditional use and in vitro studies. Limited human clinical trials; mechanisms plausible but not well-validated in humans. Safety profile unclear with heavy use.

dosage.typical_range: 200-500 mg daily (dried extract) or 1-2 ml tincture daily
dosage.with_food: Recommended to take with food to minimize GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests 2-4 week cycles; avoid prolonged continuous use without medical supervision
dosage.special_populations: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation; use cautiously in children; consult healthcare provider if taking medications

common_side_effects: Staining of teeth or tongue (dark discoloration)
contraindications: Concurrent use with iron supplements or iron-dependent medications (juglone may inhibit iron absorption)

medications: Iron supplements (may reduce absorption due to tannin and juglone content)

name: Blackcurrant Powder (Ribes nigrum)
summary: Blackcurrant fruit powder is rich in anthocyanins and polyphenols with preliminary evidence supporting benefits for vision, inflammatory markers, and antioxidant status. Most human studies are small or use concentrated extracts rather than whole fruit powder.
evidence_notes: Good phytochemical profile with human studies showing promise for vision and inflammation, but evidence is primarily from small RCTs using standardized extracts. Whole fruit powder forms have less robust clinical validation. Grade reflects moderate evidence for specific health claims but limited large-scale RCT data.

dosage.typical_range: 1-3 grams daily of whole fruit powder; concentrated extracts typically 300-500 mg daily
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; absorption may be enhanced with fat intake for anthocyanins
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent daily use recommended for several weeks to assess potential benefits
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; minimal data in pregnancy/lactation

common_side_effects: Mild allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Bleeding disorders (theoretical concern; high anthocyanin foods traditionally used; consult healthcare provider)

medications: Potential interaction with anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (theoretical; seek professional advice)
supplements: No significant established interactions with other supplements

name: Bladderwrack
summary: Iodine-rich seaweed traditionally used for thyroid support, but dose is highly variable.
evidence_notes: Variable iodine content makes dosing unreliable

dosage.typical_range: Varies widely - often 500-1000mg providing variable iodine
dosage.special_populations: Those seeking natural iodine source

serious_warnings: Variable iodine can cause thyroid dysfunction

avoid_combining_with: Other iodine sources without calculating total

name: Blood Sugar & Hormone Stack
summary: Insulin and hormone support: Berberine, Chromium, Alpha Lipoic Acid, Inositol, Cinnamon.
evidence_notes: Evidence-based blood sugar and metabolic support

dosage.typical_range: Berberine 500mg 2-3x + Chromium 200-400mcg + Alpha Lipoic Acid 300-600mg + Myo-Inositol 2g (if PCOS) + Ceylon Cinnamon 500-1000mg
dosage.special_populations: Insulin resistance, prediabetes, PCOS metabolic component

name: Blood Sugar Support Stack
summary: Comprehensive glucose management: berberine, chromium, alpha-lipoic acid, and cinnamon.
evidence_notes: Multi-pathway blood sugar support

dosage.special_populations: Prediabetes, metabolic syndrome, PCOS

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Monitor if combining with other blood sugar agents

name: Blueberry Extract (Vaccinium angustifolium)
summary: Blueberry extract is a concentrated source of anthocyanins and polyphenols with demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Research supports potential benefits for cognitive function, cardiovascular health, and eye health, though clinical evidence in humans remains moderate.
evidence_notes: Good evidence from multiple RCTs and observational studies supporting antioxidant efficacy and cognitive/cardiovascular benefits. Well-established anthocyanin content and bioavailability. Grade B rather than A due to some inconsistency in clinical outcomes and limited mechanistic certainty in humans.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; food may enhance anthocyanin absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent daily use recommended; benefits typically observed after 4–8 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for most adults; pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider

common_side_effects: Rare; generally well tolerated at recommended doses
contraindications: None established; use caution in individuals with bleeding disorders or on anticoagulants (theoretical risk due to polyphenol content)

supplements: Other antioxidant supplements (potential synergy or redundancy)

name: Bone Broth Collagen
summary: Traditional whole-food source of collagen with additional minerals and amino acids.
evidence_notes: Traditional use, less standardized

dosage.special_populations: Gut healing, whole food preference

name: Bone Density Stack
summary: Complete bone support: calcium, vitamin D3, K2, and magnesium. Essential for bone health.
evidence_notes: Essential bone support

dosage.timing: Split calcium doses, D3/K2 with fat
dosage.special_populations: Postmenopausal, osteoporosis risk, low dairy intake

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Kidney function needed for D3 activation

practical_notes: D3 and K2 are essential partners

citations.title: Calcium and vitamin D for bones
citations.finding: Combination more effective than either alone

citations.title: K2 and bone health
citations.finding: Vitamin K2 enhances calcium bone deposition

name: Boric Acid
summary: Boric acid is a boron-containing compound sometimes used in supplements for bone health and hormonal support, though clinical evidence in humans is limited. Most research on boron's effects comes from dietary studies rather than controlled trials of supplemental boric acid.
mechanism_of_action: May enhance calcium and magnesium absorption and retention
Potential modulation of estrogen and testosterone metabolism
evidence_notes: Limited human RCT data specific to boric acid supplementation. Most evidence is observational or from animal studies examining boron's role in mineral metabolism. Dietary boron deficiency effects are documented, but therapeutic supplementation benefits lack robust clinical support.

dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety data in humans is limited; short-term use (weeks) generally studied
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: avoid or consult healthcare provider; kidney disease: use with caution due to boron accumulation risk

medications: May interact with calcium or magnesium medications due to enhanced absorption effects
supplements: Calcium and magnesium supplements (may potentiate absorption)

name: Boron
summary: Trace mineral supporting bone health, cognitive function, and hormone metabolism. May support testosterone in deficient states.
evidence_notes: Supports bone and hormone health

dosage.special_populations: Postmenopausal women, athletes

name: Boron (as Boron Citrate)
summary: An essential trace mineral involved in bone metabolism, hormone regulation, and cognitive function. Boron citrate is a bioavailable form of elemental boron commonly used in supplements.
mechanism_of_action: Regulates calcium, magnesium, and phosphorus metabolism critical for bone mineralization
Modulates inflammatory signaling and antioxidant enzymes
evidence_notes: Boron has preliminary evidence for bone health and hormone support, particularly in post-menopausal women, but research is limited and inconsistent. Few high-quality RCTs; most evidence is observational or from small studies. Grade reflects moderate but incomplete evidence.

dosage.duration_notes: Safe for ongoing daily use; benefits accumulate over weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Particularly relevant for post-menopausal women; upper limit approximately 20 mg/day

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated at supplemental doses; GI upset rare
contraindications: Pregnancy and nursing: avoid high-dose boron supplementation; adequate dietary intake is safe
Individuals with kidney disease should consult healthcare provider

medications: May interact with hormone replacement therapy (HRT); consult provider if using concurrent estrogen/progesterone therapy
supplements: Synergistic with calcium, magnesium, and vitamin K for bone health
May enhance effects of other mineral supplements

name: Boron (From Boron Glycinate)
summary: A micromineral chelated to glycine for enhanced absorption, involved in bone metabolism, inflammation regulation, and testosterone/estrogen balance. Acts as a cofactor in multiple enzymatic pathways.
evidence_notes: Solid evidence for bone health; some evidence for testosterone support. More research needed on dosage and long-term safety. Glycine chelation improves bioavailability over inorganic forms.

dosage.duration_notes: Benefits to bone health develop over 8-12 weeks; effects on hormone balance may take similar timeframe
dosage.special_populations: Upper intake level ~20 mg/day; safety data limited above this range in humans

contraindications: Boron toxicity risk at doses >20 mg/day chronically
Pregnancy/nursing (developmental toxicity data at high doses)

supplements: Calcium/magnesium supplements may have cooperative effects on bone health

name: Boswellia (Frankincense)
summary: Boswellia serrata extract, standardized to boswellic acids, is traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine and has been studied for joint health and inflammatory support. Moderate clinical evidence supports its use for musculoskeletal comfort, though effect sizes are generally modest.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for OA pain/function

dosage.special_populations: Joint issues, arthritis

name: Boswellia Serrata
summary: Boswellia serrata resin extract contains boswellic acids, compounds with anti-inflammatory properties supported by moderate clinical evidence for joint health and mobility. Most studies show modest benefit for osteoarthritis and inflammatory conditions, though effect sizes are generally small to moderate.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibition of 5-lipoxygenase enzyme, reducing leukotriene production
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs demonstrate modest efficacy for osteoarthritis symptoms and joint function. Several well-designed studies support anti-inflammatory effects, but effect sizes are generally small to moderate and heterogeneity exists between studies. Grade B reflects consistent but modest clinical benefit.

dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to enhance absorption and reduce potential GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Studies typically assess outcomes at 8–12 weeks; benefits may continue with prolonged use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: limited safety data; use with caution. Pediatric: insufficient evidence.

contraindications: Known allergy to Boswellia or Burseraceae family plants

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (theoretical; limited evidence of interaction)
supplements: Other anti-inflammatory herbs (turmeric, ginger; additive effects)

name: Bovine Adrenal Cortex (Supranal)
summary: Bovine adrenal cortex is a glandular extract derived from cattle adrenal glands, traditionally used to support adrenal function and stress resilience. Evidence for efficacy is limited and primarily anecdotal; clinical research is sparse.
mechanism_of_action: Provides endogenous hormones and cofactors (cortisol, DHEA, chromaffin cell compounds) that may support adrenal axis function
Potential nutritional support via micronutrients present in adrenal tissue
evidence_notes: Very limited peer-reviewed clinical evidence. No high-quality RCTs demonstrating efficacy for adrenal fatigue or stress support. Historical use and practitioner anecdotes predominate; theoretical benefit based on tissue content rather than empirical validation.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with food to minimize digestive upset
dosage.duration_notes: No established optimal duration; typically used ongoing for chronic support
dosage.special_populations: Not established for pregnancy/lactation or pediatric use; autoimmune individuals should consult a practitioner

contraindications: Cushing's syndrome or excess cortisol states

medications: Thyroid medication (glandular products may contain thyroid tissue)
supplements: Other adrenal support or glandular products (cumulative hormone dose)

name: Blood Pressure Support Stack
summary: Natural BP support: magnesium, CoQ10, beetroot/citrulline, and potassium-rich diet.
evidence_notes: Natural BP support

dosage.typical_range: Magnesium 400-500mg + CoQ10 100-200mg + Beetroot/Citrulline + Potassium from diet
dosage.special_populations: Mild-moderate hypertension

practical_notes: May allow reduced medication with doctor

name: Broccoli
summary: Broccoli powder is a whole-food derived ingredient rich in sulforaphane, glucosinolates, and polyphenols that support antioxidant and detoxification pathways. Evidence supports potential benefits for cellular health and inflammatory response, though most human research uses fresh broccoli or concentrated extracts rather than powder forms.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for Nrf2 activation and detoxification support

dosage.special_populations: Detox support seekers

medications: May affect drug metabolism via detox enzymes

practical_notes: Myrosinase enzyme needed to convert glucoraphanin
Sprouts have 20-100x more than mature broccoli

name: Bromelain
summary: Pineapple-derived protease enzyme supporting protein digestion and having anti-inflammatory properties.
evidence_notes: Digestive and anti-inflammatory uses

dosage.timing: Between meals for inflammation; with meals for digestion
dosage.special_populations: Surgery recovery, sinusitis

name: Burdock Root (Arctium lappa)
summary: Traditional blood cleanser and liver support herb with prebiotic fiber and gentle detoxification properties.
evidence_notes: Traditional use, limited clinical research

dosage.special_populations: Those seeking skin and gentle detox support

serious_warnings: May be contaminated with belladonna (buy from reputable sources)

name: Butcher's Broom
summary: Traditional remedy for poor circulation and venous health. Supports leg circulation.
evidence_notes: Traditional circulation support

dosage.special_populations: Venous insufficiency, heavy legs, orthostatic hypotension

name: Butcher's Broom Extract
summary: Butcher's broom is a traditional European plant extract traditionally used to support venous circulation and vascular health, with preliminary evidence suggesting mild benefits for venous insufficiency symptoms. Most clinical research focuses on standardized extracts containing ruscogenins and flavonoids.
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence from European clinical studies on chronic venous insufficiency, primarily from older trials and smaller sample sizes. Standardization and quality vary. Most evidence focuses on standardized extracts rather than whole plant preparations. Lacks large modern RCT validation.

dosage.typical_range: 300-600 mg daily (of standardized extract equivalent to 4,500 mg dried whole plant)
dosage.with_food: Recommended to enhance absorption and minimize gastrointestinal irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Most clinical studies used 4-12 weeks of continuous use for symptom evaluation
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/nursing: avoid due to limited safety data. Use with caution if pregnant or breastfeeding.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to Ruscus aculeatus or Araliaceae family plants

name: Butterbur Extract (Petasites japonicus)
summary: Butterbur extract is a plant-derived supplement traditionally used for allergy and respiratory support, with petasins as the proposed active compounds. Clinical evidence for seasonal allergy relief is moderate, though long-term safety and efficacy data remain limited.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibition of leukotriene and histamine release
Anti-inflammatory activity via petasin and isopetasin compounds
Potential smooth muscle relaxation in airways
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence exists for butterbur in seasonal allergies from several small RCTs, though study quality is mixed and sample sizes limited. Evidence for other uses (migraines, respiratory) is more preliminary. Long-term safety data sparse, particularly regarding hepatotoxicity concerns with some plant preparations.

dosage.typical_range: 50-150 mg daily (standardized extract) or as directed on label
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; food may reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used seasonally for allergy support; 2-4 weeks may be needed for full benefit
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in pregnancy and lactation; limited safety data in children

contraindications: Hepatic disease (hepatotoxicity risk with certain preparations)

medications: Theoretical interactions with blood thinners (limited evidence)
supplements: Other herbal antihistamines may have additive effects

name: Butyrate (Tributyrin/Sodium Butyrate)
summary: Primary fuel for colonocytes, supporting colon health, barrier function, and reducing inflammation.
evidence_notes: Good mechanistic evidence, growing clinical research

dosage.special_populations: IBD, leaky gut, those with low fiber intake

name: C60 (Carbon 60 Fullerene)
summary: Spherical carbon molecule with potent antioxidant properties in olive oil. Controversial with limited human data despite promising rodent longevity studies.
evidence_notes: Limited human data, rodent studies controversial, experimental

dosage.special_populations: Experimental longevity enthusiasts

food_alcohol: Must be in oil (olive oil common)

practical_notes: Quality control critical - many poor products

name: C8 MCT Oil Powder
summary: C8 MCT oil powder is a specialized form containing primarily caprylic acid (8-carbon medium-chain triglyceride), the most rapidly metabolized MCT chain length. It is marketed as the most efficient MCT variant for ketone production and quick energy.
mechanism_of_action: fastest hepatic oxidation among MCTs due to C8 chain length
maximal ketone body production in low-carb states
evidence_notes: While MCTs generally show good evidence (Grade B), pure C8 products have more limited direct comparative research versus mixed-MCT oils. Some evidence suggests C8 may be more ketogenic than C10/C12, but clinical significance is modest. Most studies use mixed MCT blends.

dosage.timing: morning on empty stomach or pre-workout for maximal ketone production; can divide dose
dosage.with_food: best taken fasting or with minimal carbohydrates for ketone effect; fat with meals reduces relative advantage
dosage.duration_notes: continuous use is safe; GI tolerance typically improves with consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Start with 5g due to potency; those new to MCTs prone to GI distress should begin lower; individuals with liver disease, uncontrolled diabetes, or metabolic disorders should consult provider

medications: may enhance absorption of fat-soluble medications
supplements: may enhance bioavailability of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)

name: Caffeine
summary: Most widely used performance enhancer. Improves endurance, strength, and cognitive function through adenosine antagonism.
evidence_notes: Proven ergogenic aid

dosage.special_populations: Athletes, shift workers

name: Caffeine (Camellia sinensis leaf)
summary: Caffeine from Camellia sinensis (green tea) is a central nervous system stimulant that enhances alertness, focus, and endurance. This source also provides catechin antioxidants including EGCG.
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence confirms caffeine's efficacy for cognitive performance, alertness, and endurance across numerous studies. Green tea source provides additional polyphenol benefits.

dosage.typical_range: 50–200 mg caffeine daily for cognitive effects; up to 400 mg daily is generally safe for most adults
dosage.timing: morning or early afternoon to avoid sleep disruption
dosage.with_food: either, though food may delay absorption
dosage.duration_notes: tolerance develops; cycling recommended to maintain efficacy; effects typically last 3–5 hours
dosage.special_populations: pregnant women limit to <200 mg/day; sensitive individuals or anxiety disorders use lower doses or avoid

common_side_effects: elevated heart rate at high doses

supplements: avoid combining with other stimulants (e.g., synephrine, yohimbine)

name: Caffeine + Theanine Stack
summary: The most popular beginner nootropic combination. Smooth, focused energy.
evidence_notes: Most popular basic stack

dosage.special_populations: Beginners, productivity

citations.title: Caffeine + L-theanine synergy
citations.finding: Combination improves attention and task switching vs either alone

citations.title: Caffeine-theanine acute effects
citations.finding: Improved speed and accuracy of attention tasks

name: Calcium
summary: Essential mineral for bone health, muscle function, nerve signaling, and blood clotting.
evidence_notes: Essential mineral with strong evidence for bone health

dosage.special_populations: Post-menopausal women

moderate_dose.label: 500-1,000 mg from supplements
moderate_dose.notes: Combine with diet to reach RDA

name: Calcium (as Calcium AAC)
summary: Calcium is an essential mineral critical for bone health, muscle function, and nerve transmission. Calcium AAC is a chelated form designed for enhanced absorption.
mechanism_of_action: Structural component of bone and teeth
evidence_notes: Calcium supplementation has strong RCT evidence for bone health and fracture prevention, particularly in postmenopausal women and older adults. Chelated forms show improved bioavailability compared to some traditional forms.

dosage.timing: Divided doses with meals for optimal absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term use recommended for bone health maintenance
dosage.special_populations: Postmenopausal women and older adults may benefit from higher intake; adolescents need 1300 mg daily

name: Calcium (Calcium Carbonate)
summary: An essential mineral required for bone health, muscle contraction, nerve transmission, and hormonal regulation. Calcium carbonate is an inexpensive, well-absorbed form commonly used in supplements.
mechanism_of_action: Structural component of bone and teeth
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence for bone health, fracture prevention, and cardiovascular effects. Strong evidence for supplementation efficacy, particularly in women over 50 and those at risk for osteoporosis.

dosage.typical_range: 1000-1200 mg daily for adults (adequate intake); up to 2000 mg daily tolerable upper limit
dosage.timing: Divided doses improve absorption (max ~500 mg per dose)
dosage.with_food: Calcium carbonate should be taken with food; calcium citrate does not require food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; lifelong intake critical for bone health
dosage.special_populations: Women over 50 require 1200 mg daily; men 51+ require 1000-1200 mg daily; postmenopausal women benefit from adequate intake combined with vitamin D

medications: Fluoroquinolone and tetracycline antibiotics require separation
ACE inhibitors may increase calcium retention
supplements: Separate from vitamin D supplements by several hours for optimal absorption

name: Calcium Carbonate
summary: Common, economical calcium form with highest elemental calcium percentage. Requires stomach acid for absorption.
evidence_notes: Economical calcium source

dosage.special_populations: Take with food for absorption

citations.title: Calcium and bone health
citations.finding: Calcium supplementation modestly improves bone density

citations.title: Calcium forms comparison
citations.finding: Carbonate less well absorbed on empty stomach than citrate

name: Calcium Citrate
summary: Well-absorbed calcium form that doesn't require stomach acid. Better for those on acid-reducing medications.
evidence_notes: Well-absorbed calcium form

dosage.special_populations: Those on PPIs, older adults

practical_notes: Better absorbed than carbonate in low acid

citations.title: Calcium supplementation and bone health
citations.finding: Calcium supplementation modestly improves bone density

citations.title: Calcium citrate vs carbonate absorption
citations.finding: Citrate absorbed equally well with or without food; better for low stomach acid

name: Calcium D-Glucarate
summary: Supports phase II liver detox by inhibiting beta-glucuronidase, preventing toxin and hormone reabsorption.
evidence_notes: Good mechanism understanding, some clinical evidence

dosage.special_populations: Those with hormone concerns, detox support

serious_warnings: May increase clearance of some drugs

medications: May increase clearance of drugs metabolized by glucuronidation

avoid_combining_with: May affect some drug levels - monitor

name: Calcium + D3 (Menopause Bone)
summary: Essential combination for bone health as estrogen declines during menopause.
evidence_notes: Essential for postmenopausal bone health

forms.notes: Best absorbed, can take without food

dosage.timing: Divided doses (500-600mg calcium max per dose)
dosage.with_food: Citrate either way, carbonate with food
dosage.special_populations: Perimenopausal and postmenopausal women

food_alcohol: Citrate either way, carbonate with food

practical_notes: Add K2 to direct calcium to bones

name: Calcium (as Dicalcium Malate)
summary: Calcium is an essential mineral critical for bone health, muscle contraction, and nerve transmission. Dicalcium malate is a chelated form combining calcium with malic acid for enhanced absorption.
mechanism_of_action: structural component of bone and teeth
evidence_notes: Calcium has extensive RCT evidence for bone health and fracture prevention, particularly in older adults and postmenopausal women. The malate form may enhance absorption compared to some other forms.

dosage.special_populations: postmenopausal women may benefit from higher intakes; kidney disease patients should consult healthcare provider

contraindications: history of kidney stones (in susceptible individuals)

name: Calcium Gluconate
summary: Calcium gluconate is an organic calcium salt with high solubility and bioavailability, used to support bone health, muscle function, and cellular signaling. It is well-established that adequate calcium intake is essential for skeletal integrity and cardiovascular function.
mechanism_of_action: Provides bioavailable calcium for bone matrix formation and maintenance
Supports excitation-contraction coupling in muscle tissue
evidence_notes: Calcium's role in bone health and general physiology is extensively documented through large RCTs and meta-analyses. Gluconate is a well-absorbed organic form with established bioavailability compared to inorganic salts.

dosage.typical_range: 500–1200 mg elemental calcium daily (divided doses preferred)
dosage.timing: With meals to enhance absorption; divide into 2–3 doses for optimal uptake
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term daily use appropriate for bone health maintenance; consistent intake recommended
dosage.special_populations: Postmenopausal women, older adults, and those with low dietary calcium intake benefit most; requirements increase with age

medications: ACE inhibitors and thiazide diuretics may increase calcium retention
supplements: Zinc—may reduce zinc absorption; separate dosing advised
Vitamin K and vitamin D—synergistic for bone health

name: Calcium β-hydroxy β-methylbutyrate (HMB)
summary: HMB is a metabolite of the branched-chain amino acid leucine that plays a role in muscle protein synthesis and breakdown prevention. It is investigated for its potential to support muscle mass, strength, and recovery in resistance training and aging populations.
mechanism_of_action: Activates mTOR signaling pathway to promote muscle protein synthesis
Inhibits proteasome and calpain-mediated muscle protein degradation
Reduces inflammation and oxidative stress markers in muscle tissue
May increase satellite cell activation for muscle fiber repair
evidence_notes: Good-quality RCT evidence in resistance-trained and aging populations supports modest improvements in lean mass and strength. Effects are most pronounced in untrained individuals and older adults; benefits in trained athletes are smaller but consistent. Standard dose is 3 g/day.

dosage.typical_range: 3,000–3,600 mg daily (divided into 2–3 doses, typically 1,000–1,200 mg per dose)
dosage.timing: Distribute across meals; post-workout timing may offer marginal additional benefit when combined with resistance training
dosage.with_food: With meals; may improve absorption and reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Effects typically emerge after 2–4 weeks of consistent supplementation. Long-term safety data support use for 12+ weeks. Discontinuation leads to gradual return to baseline within 2–4 weeks.
dosage.special_populations: Greatest benefit in older adults (65+), untrained individuals, and those recovering from illness or immobility. Benefits are additive to resistance training; minimal effect without exercise stimulus.

common_side_effects: Mild GI upset (nausea, diarrhea) at high single doses; mitigated by dividing doses
contraindications: No absolute contraindications at recommended doses
Individuals with severe renal impairment should monitor metabolite levels (consult provider)

medications: Theoretical interaction with immunosuppressants (HMB may modulate immune function; clinical relevance unclear)
supplements: Creatine: synergistic for muscle gains when combined with resistance training
Whey protein/leucine: complementary leucine source; coordinate dosing to avoid excessive branched-chain amino acid intake
Vitamin D and calcium: supportive for muscle and bone health in aging populations

name: Calcium Lactate
summary: Calcium lactate is a bioavailable form of calcium that provides essential mineral support for bone health, muscle function, and nerve transmission. It is well-absorbed and commonly used as a dietary calcium supplement.
mechanism_of_action: Provides elemental calcium for bone mineralization and structural integrity
Enables proper muscle contraction and nervous system signaling
evidence_notes: Calcium supplementation has strong RCT evidence for bone health and deficiency prevention. Lactate is a well-established, bioavailable calcium salt with established safety and efficacy profiles.

dosage.typical_range: 500–1,000 mg elemental calcium daily (approximately 2,000–4,000 mg calcium lactate)
dosage.timing: Divided doses with meals preferred for optimal absorption
dosage.with_food: With food enhances absorption; taken with meals is recommended
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term supplementation (ongoing) for chronic deficiency prevention or bone support
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant and lactating women: 1,000–1,300 mg elemental calcium daily; adolescents and older adults: individualized per clinical guidelines

contraindications: History of kidney stones (consult healthcare provider)

supplements: Iron supplements (may reduce iron absorption if taken together)
Zinc supplements (may reduce zinc absorption if taken concurrently)

name: Calcium Orotate
summary: Calcium orotate is a calcium salt of orotic acid, proposed to enhance calcium absorption and bioavailability compared to standard calcium forms. Limited human evidence exists specifically for this form; efficacy relies primarily on its calcium content and theoretical advantages of the orotate carrier.
mechanism_of_action: Provides elemental calcium for bone mineralization and cellular function
Orotic acid may enhance mineral absorption and cellular energy metabolism
evidence_notes: Calcium supplementation in general is well-established (Grade A), but calcium orotate specifically lacks robust human RCT data. The orotate carrier has theoretical benefits in animal models but minimal human clinical validation for superior absorption or outcomes versus standard calcium forms.

dosage.typical_range: 500–1000 mg elemental calcium daily (divided doses preferred)
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term use appropriate for bone health maintenance; typical supplementation ongoing
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant and lactating women have higher requirements; elderly may benefit from divided dosing

contraindications: History of kidney stones or hypercalcemia

medications: Tetracycline antibiotics (reduce absorption; separate by 2+ hours)
supplements: Zinc (high doses may compete for absorption)

name: Calcium (for Relaxation)
summary: Essential for muscle contraction AND relaxation. Works with magnesium.
evidence_notes: Essential mineral

dosage.special_populations: Dairy-free, bone health

citations.title: Calcium and muscle function
citations.finding: Calcium essential for muscle contraction and relaxation

citations.title: Calcium nerve function
citations.finding: Adequate calcium supports neuromuscular function

name: Calebin-A (CurCousin®)
summary: A proprietary curcumin analog (98% Calebin-A) derived from turmeric with enhanced bioavailability and potential metabolic properties distinct from standard curcumin. An emerging derivative researched for inflammation and cellular health.
evidence_notes: Preliminary and emerging data on Calebin-A; in vitro and animal studies show promise; very limited human clinical trial data compared to standard curcumin; bioavailability advantage over curcumin is proposed but human evidence is sparse.

dosage.with_food: Essential; fat-soluble and requires dietary lipids for absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Continuous use recommended; effects typically develop over 4-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/nursing: avoid; gallbladder disease: consult provider; anticoagulant users: monitor

contraindications: Biliary obstruction or severe gallbladder disease

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelets (monitor; curcuminoids may have mild antiplatelet activity)
CYP3A4 substrates (curcumin derivatives are CYP3A4 inhibitors)

name: California Poppy
summary: Mild sedative and anxiolytic herb in the poppy family. Distinct from opium poppy — no opioid alkaloids.
evidence_notes: Limited human studies. Traditional use for sleep and mild anxiety; mechanism inferred from in vitro work.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibits enzymatic degradation of catecholamines and serotonin

forms.notes: Standardized to californidine in some products

dosage.typical_range: 200-600 mg dried herb, or 0.5-2 ml tincture before bed
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for short-term ongoing use; long-term safety not well studied
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in pregnancy

serious_warnings: Despite the name, contains no opioid alkaloids
safety.liver_kidney_notes: No significant concerns at typical doses

practical_notes: Often used in sleep blends rather than alone
Effect is subtle compared to valerian or kava
Not the same plant as opium poppy (Papaver somniferum)

name: Camellia sinensis Extract (Green Tea Leaf)
summary: Camellia sinensis leaf extract is a polyphenol-rich botanical standardized for EGCG, the primary catechin compound associated with green tea's antioxidant and metabolic benefits. Substantial human evidence supports its use for cognitive support, metabolic health, and antioxidant protection.
mechanism_of_action: EGCG-mediated antioxidant activity via free radical scavenging
Modulation of cellular signaling pathways (AMPK, MAPK)
Potential enhancement of fat oxidation and thermogenesis
evidence_notes: Well-established human trial data on green tea catechins; multiple RCTs demonstrate cognitive and metabolic effects. EGCG standardization (45%) is a recognized quality marker. Grade B rather than A due to modest effect sizes in some domains and heterogeneous study designs.

dosage.typical_range: 200–600 mg daily (EGCG content typically 90–270 mg at this dosage)
dosage.timing: With meals or as part of daily routine; can be divided into 2–3 doses
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; taking with meals may improve tolerability and absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term daily use is safe; benefits typically observed over 4–8 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy and lactation at typical dietary doses; pediatric and geriatric populations tolerate standard doses well

common_side_effects: Mild nausea or stomach upset (especially on empty stomach)
Jitteriness or sleep disruption in sensitive individuals (due to residual caffeine ~2–4% in extract)
contraindications: Iron deficiency anemia (high polyphenol content may reduce iron absorption; separate dosing by 2+ hours)
Severe caffeine sensitivity (use decaffeinated forms if available)
Concurrent anticoagulation therapy (theoretical interaction; use cautiously and monitor)

medications: Iron supplements (may reduce absorption; space dosing by 2+ hours)
Warfarin and other anticoagulants (theoretical; monitor if high doses used)
CYP2C9 substrates (EGCG may modulate enzyme activity at high doses)
supplements: Other high-dose polyphenol sources (e.g., resveratrol, curcumin) — additive antioxidant effects generally beneficial but monitor in sensitive individuals

name: Camu-Camu (Myrciaria dubia)
summary: Amazonian superfruit containing exceptionally high vitamin C levels (2-3% by weight), along with polyphenols and carotenoids. Provides natural-source vitamin C with enhanced bioavailability from co-occurring phytonutrients.
evidence_notes: Strong antioxidant evidence, emerging research on anti-inflammatory and metabolic benefits
mechanism_of_action: Provides vitamin C for collagen synthesis and immune function
Polyphenols (ellagic acid, flavonoids) provide additional antioxidant activity
Carotenoids support eye and skin health
Synergistic phytonutrient complex enhances vitamin C absorption

dosage.special_populations: Reduce dose if sensitive to vitamin C

medications: Theoretical interaction with blood thinners at very high doses

practical_notes: Contains ~60x more vitamin C than oranges by weight
Typical extract provides 20-30mg vitamin C per 100-150mg

name: Capsaicin / Cayenne
summary: Hot pepper compound increasing thermogenesis and reducing appetite.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for acute metabolic effects

dosage.special_populations: Weight management

name: Caralluma Fimbriata Extract
summary: Caralluma fimbriata is a succulent plant traditionally used in Indian cuisine with preliminary evidence suggesting potential appetite-suppressing properties. Limited human clinical trials support claims of weight management benefits, with most studies being small and short-term.
mechanism_of_action: Potential appetite regulation via CNS signaling
evidence_notes: Only a few small human clinical trials exist (mostly 8-12 weeks). Most positive studies are industry-sponsored. Mechanism remains unclear. Insufficient evidence for strong recommendation; more rigorous long-term RCTs needed for higher grading.

dosage.timing: Typically divided into 2 doses (morning and evening)
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; some sources recommend before meals
dosage.duration_notes: Human studies examined 8-12 week periods; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Insufficient safety data in pregnancy/lactation; use caution in diabetics (may affect blood sugar)

contraindications: Concurrent use with SGLT2 inhibitors (theoretical risk for euglycemic DKA)

medications: SGLT2 inhibitors (diabetes medications) — theoretical concern; monitor blood glucose
Other antidiabetic agents — possible additive effect; monitor glucose
supplements: Other appetite suppressants or weight loss supplements — additive effects unknown

name: Carbohydrate Powder (Dextrose/Maltodextrin/Cyclic Dextrin)
summary: Fast-digesting carbs for intra/post-workout energy and glycogen replenishment.
evidence_notes: Essential for endurance and glycogen recovery

dosage.special_populations: Endurance athletes, high-volume training

practical_notes: Cyclic dextrin is gentlest on stomach

name: L-Carnitine
summary: Shuttles fatty acids into mitochondria for energy. Supports fat burning and exercise performance.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for recovery and fat oxidation

dosage.timing: With carbs and insulin for uptake
dosage.special_populations: Endurance athletes, fat loss, elderly

practical_notes: Take with carbs for muscle uptake
ALCAR for brain, tartrate for exercise

name: Carrot Powder (Daucus carota)
summary: Carrot powder is a dehydrated whole-food ingredient derived from carrot roots, providing beta-carotene (vitamin A precursor), fiber, and various phytonutrients. Limited clinical evidence supports specific health claims beyond general nutritional contribution.
mechanism_of_action: Beta-carotene (provitamin A) conversion and antioxidant activity
Dietary fiber contribution to digestive health
evidence_notes: Carrot powder is a whole-food ingredient with known nutritional composition (beta-carotene, fiber, minerals), but lacks robust clinical trials demonstrating isolated health benefits beyond basic nutritional support. Evidence for raw carrots is stronger than for concentrated powder form.

dosage.timing: Can be taken anytime; often mixed into beverages, smoothies, or foods
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; may be better tolerated with food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use as a whole-food ingredient
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe; individuals with beta-carotene conversion issues (genetic variations) may experience less benefit

common_side_effects: Possible carotenemia (skin discoloration) with very high intakes of beta-carotene over time
contraindications: Smokers should avoid excessive beta-carotene supplementation (mixed evidence on increased lung cancer risk)
Caution in individuals with specific genetic disorders affecting carotenoid metabolism

medications: Potential minor interaction with blood thinners (vitamin K content is minimal but present)
supplements: No significant known interactions with other supplements

name: Cascara Sagrada Extract 10:1
summary: Cascara sagrada is a traditional herbal laxative derived from the bark of Rhamnus purshiana, historically used to promote bowel regularity and relieve occasional constipation. Modern evidence supports its cathartic properties, though long-term use carries safety concerns and it is regulated as an over-the-counter drug in many jurisdictions.
mechanism_of_action: Anthraquinone glycosides stimulate colonic muscle contractions
Increases fluid secretion in the colon
evidence_notes: Cascara sagrada has long historical use and established pharmacological activity as a stimulant laxative. Evidence is solid for short-term constipation relief, but B rather than A due to limited modern RCTs and significant safety concerns limiting research. FDA recognized it as an active laxative ingredient, though it has faced regulatory restrictions.

dosage.typical_range: 300-900 mg daily (cascara sagrada equivalent based on 10:1 extract ratio)
dosage.timing: Evening or before bedtime for overnight effect
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; effects typically occur 6-12 hours after ingestion
dosage.duration_notes: Intended for short-term, occasional use only; not recommended for continuous use beyond 1-2 weeks without medical supervision
dosage.special_populations: Avoid during pregnancy, lactation, and in children under 12 years; not suitable for individuals with bowel obstruction, inflammatory bowel disease, or acute abdominal pain

contraindications: Chronic use increases risk of laxative dependency and potassium depletion

name: Casein Protein
summary: Slow-digesting milk protein. Provides sustained amino acid release, ideal before bed.
evidence_notes: Well-established slow-release protein

dosage.special_populations: Overnight muscle preservation

citations.title: ISSN protein position stand
citations.finding: Casein provides sustained amino acid release

citations.title: Casein before sleep
citations.finding: Pre-sleep casein improves overnight muscle protein synthesis

name: Cat's Claw
summary: Amazon rainforest herb with anti-inflammatory and immune-modulating properties.
evidence_notes: Amazonian joint remedy

dosage.special_populations: OA, RA (adjunct), immune support

practical_notes: Two species - tomentosa is studied one

name: Cat's Claw Extract (Uncaria tomentosa)
summary: Cat's Claw is a Peruvian vine traditionally used to support immune function and joint health, containing alkaloids and polyphenols with preliminary anti-inflammatory properties. Evidence remains moderate and primarily from in vitro and animal studies, with limited high-quality human clinical trials.
mechanism_of_action: Contains pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids that may modulate immune response
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence from small human trials and traditional use; most mechanistic evidence is in vitro or animal-derived. Limited large-scale RCTs in humans. 10:1 extract represents concentrated form but lacks standardized clinical evidence specific to this ratio.

dosage.typical_range: 400-1000 mg daily (or 20-60 mg of 10:1 extract equivalent)
dosage.duration_notes: Some studies used 12-24 week protocols; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Avoid during pregnancy/lactation; caution in autoimmune conditions

contraindications: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant use (theoretical risk)

name: Catuaba Bark Extract
summary: Catuaba bark is a traditional Amazonian herbal used in folk medicine for energy and sexual function, but clinical evidence in humans is very limited. Most claims rest on traditional use and preliminary in vitro studies rather than robust human trials.
mechanism_of_action: Potential alkaloid and phenolic compound activity on CNS signaling
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical trial data. Primary evidence comes from traditional use in Amazonian medicine and in vitro studies. No large, well-controlled RCTs in humans support efficacy claims. Classification reflects preliminary status and lack of robust human evidence.

dosage.timing: Typically taken once or twice daily with meals
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; food may improve tolerance
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests weeks to months for effects; optimal duration unclear
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and children not established; use with caution

common_side_effects: Rare: insomnia or overstimulation (if taken in high doses or late in day)
contraindications: MAOi use (potential interaction with alkaloids—use with caution)

medications: Potential interaction with MAOi antidepressants (alkaloid content)
Possible mild stimulant effects; caution with stimulant medications
supplements: May have additive effects with other stimulating or adaptogenic herbs

name: Cayenne Pepper (Fruit)
summary: Cayenne pepper is a pungent fruit rich in capsaicin, an alkaloid compound with demonstrated thermogenic and anti-inflammatory properties. It is widely used to support circulation, metabolism, and digestive function.
mechanism_of_action: Capsaicin activates TRPV1 receptors, increasing heat production and metabolic rate
Promotes peripheral blood flow and vasodilation
Modulates inflammatory cytokines and reduces prostaglandin production
Stimulates digestive secretions and may improve nutrient absorption
evidence_notes: Moderate clinical evidence supporting thermogenesis, pain relief (especially topical), and modest metabolic effects. Human RCTs demonstrate small but consistent improvements in energy expenditure and satiety. Heat units (HU) standardization helps ensure consistency.

dosage.typical_range: 30-120 mg capsaicinoids (or 40,000 HU cayenne powder) daily
dosage.timing: With meals (preferably with fat-containing foods to improve absorption)
dosage.with_food: Strongly recommended with food to minimize GI upset and enhance bioavailability
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used long-term; tolerance may develop with continuous use, suggesting periodic breaks
dosage.special_populations: Caution in GERD, IBS, or inflammatory bowel conditions; avoid if pregnant/nursing without medical guidance

common_side_effects: Stomach upset, burning sensation, or abdominal discomfort
contraindications: Severe GERD or peptic ulcer disease

medications: Aspirin or NSAIDs (may increase GI irritation)
Anticoagulants (capsaicin may have mild antiplatelet effects)
ACE inhibitors (may potentiate cough side effect)
Thermoregulatory medications (may interact with temperature control)
supplements: Other thermogenic agents (synergistic, but monitor for overstimulation)
Black pepper/piperine (both activate TRPV1; potential synergy)

name: CBD Oil
summary: Non-psychoactive cannabis compound for anxiety, pain, and sleep. Legal status varies.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for anxiety, emerging for other uses

dosage.special_populations: Anxiety, chronic pain, sleep

safety.liver_kidney_notes: May affect liver enzymes high doses

name: CDP-Choline (Citicoline)
summary: Choline and cytidine donor supporting brain cell membranes and neurotransmitters. Well-studied for cognitive support.
evidence_notes: Well-researched cognitive support

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive support, stroke recovery

citations.title: CDP-choline cognitive effects
citations.finding: CDP-choline supports memory and attention

citations.title: Citicoline meta-analysis
citations.finding: Improves cognitive function in cerebrovascular disease

name: Celastrus paniculatus
summary: Celastrus paniculatus is a traditional Ayurvedic herb used for cognitive enhancement, with preliminary evidence suggesting potential benefits for memory and learning. Limited human clinical data exists; most evidence is traditional use and in vitro/animal studies.
evidence_notes: Traditional Ayurvedic nootropic

dosage.typical_range: 10-15 drops oil or as extract directs
dosage.special_populations: Memory, learning

name: Celery Seed Extract
summary: Traditional remedy for blood pressure containing 3-n-butylphthalide (3nB).
evidence_notes: Traditional BP remedy

dosage.special_populations: Blood pressure, gout

name: Cellular Renewal Complex
summary: Proprietary blend targeting cellular regeneration and longevity pathways through compounds that support autophagy, mitochondrial function, and healthy aging processes.
evidence_notes: Proprietary blend with established longevity-focused components
mechanism_of_action: Provides NAD+ precursors for cellular energy
Contains senolytic compounds targeting aged cells

dosage.timing: Morning, may be taken fasted for autophagy benefits
dosage.special_populations: Start with lower dose for sensitive individuals

serious_warnings: Consult healthcare provider if on medications

supplements: Works well with other longevity compounds

practical_notes: May enhance benefits of intermittent fasting
Effects build over weeks to months

name: Centella asiatica Extract (Gotu Kola)
summary: Centella asiatica is a traditional Asian herb standardized for triterpenes and madecassosides, studied for cognitive support, skin health, and venous function. Evidence supports modest benefits for microcirculation and wound healing, with emerging data on cognitive effects.
mechanism_of_action: Triterpenes and madecassosides enhance collagen synthesis and angiogenesis
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects on vascular endothelium
May improve cerebral blood flow and neuroprotection
Modulation of connective tissue remodeling via fibroblast activation
evidence_notes: Solid clinical evidence for venous insufficiency and wound healing; moderate evidence for cognitive effects. Multiple RCTs support microcirculation benefits, though cognitive outcomes are less consistently demonstrated. Well-researched traditional herb with established safety profile.

dosage.with_food: Recommended to enhance absorption and reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically observed after 4-8 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy at standard doses; consult healthcare provider. Caution in sedative users or those with upcoming surgery.

contraindications: Concurrent sedative medications (additive CNS effects possible)
Scheduled surgery (may affect bleeding/clotting; discontinue 2 weeks prior)
Known allergy to Apiaceae family plants

medications: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, sedatives): potential additive effects
Anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents: theoretical increased bleeding risk
supplements: Other herbal anticoagulants (ginkgo, garlic, bromelain): monitor for bleeding risk
Sedative herbs (valerian, passionflower): additive CNS effects possible

name: Centrophenoxine
summary: Choline compound and anti-aging nootropic. Reduces lipofuscin and supports acetylcholine.
evidence_notes: Anti-aging nootropic

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive aging

name: Ceramides (Phytoceramides)
summary: Lipid molecules that support skin barrier function and hydration from within.
evidence_notes: Oral skin barrier support

dosage.special_populations: Dry skin, aging skin, eczema-prone

practical_notes: Works on skin barrier from inside

name: Cetyl Myristoleate (CMO)
summary: Fatty acid ester studied for joint lubrication and inflammation reduction.
evidence_notes: Limited but promising research

dosage.special_populations: OA, joint stiffness

name: Ceylon Cinnamon Bark Powder
summary: Ceylon cinnamon is the bark powder of Cinnamomum verum, containing cinnamaldehyde and polyphenols with traditionally documented effects on glucose metabolism and inflammatory markers. It differs from cassia cinnamon (Cinnamomum cassia) by having lower coumarin content.
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs show modest improvements in fasting glucose and HbA1c in prediabetic and diabetic populations. Effect sizes range from 5-25% improvement. Meta-analyses support benefit at 1-6g daily. Evidence stronger for metabolic effects than for other traditionally claimed uses.

dosage.timing: With meals, particularly those high in carbohydrates
dosage.with_food: Take with food; provides better glycemic benefit when consumed with meals
dosage.duration_notes: Minimal effects observed before 2-4 weeks. Most studies range 8-16 weeks; benefit sustained with continued use.
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/nursing: generally recognized as safe in culinary amounts; supplement use should be discussed with healthcare provider. Coumarin content much lower in Ceylon than Cassia cinnamon, making it safer for long-term supplementation.

contraindications: Severe hepatic impairment (caution with higher doses due to coumarin)

name: Chaga (Inonotus obliquus)
summary: Antioxidant-rich fungus from birch trees with immune-modulating and anti-inflammatory properties.
evidence_notes: Traditional use with emerging research

dosage.special_populations: Those seeking antioxidant and immune support

serious_warnings: High oxalate content - kidney stone risk with very high doses
safety.liver_kidney_notes: High oxalates - kidney concern with excessive use

name: Chaga Mushroom (Inonotus obliquus)
summary: Chaga is a fungal growth on birch trees traditionally used in Scandinavian and Russian medicine, containing bioactive compounds like polysaccharides and betulinic acid. Limited human clinical evidence exists for most claimed benefits, with most research conducted in vitro or in animal models.
mechanism_of_action: Antioxidant activity via melanin and phenolic compounds
evidence_notes: Most evidence is in vitro or animal-based; human RCTs are limited and small. Traditional use is well-established but does not substitute for clinical evidence. Grade reflects preliminary scientific support for immune and antioxidant claims.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; often mixed into beverages
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; some evidence suggests taking with food may improve absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Traditionally used long-term; optimal duration for modern use unclear due to limited human studies
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; may interact with immunosuppressant medications

common_side_effects: Rare: allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Immunosuppressant therapy (potential immune stimulation may interfere)
Known allergy to fungi or birch pollen

name: Chamomile
summary: Chamomile is a traditional herbal remedy with mild sedative and anxiolytic properties, supported by moderate clinical evidence for sleep quality and relaxation. Multiple studies suggest benefits for anxiety and digestive comfort, though effects are generally modest.
evidence_notes: Traditional use, moderate evidence

dosage.special_populations: Gentle option

name: Chanca Piedra Extract (Phyllanthus niruri)
summary: Chanca piedra (Phyllanthus niruri) is a traditional Amazonian herb with preliminary evidence suggesting potential benefits for kidney stone management and urinary tract health. Most human studies are small and of moderate quality, with stronger evidence needed for definitive efficacy claims.
mechanism_of_action: Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activity via polyphenols
Possible antispasmodic effects on urinary tract
May affect calcium oxalate crystal formation
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials with modest sample sizes. Some in vitro and animal studies support traditional uses, but controlled RCT evidence in humans is sparse. Traditional use in Amazonian medicine is well-documented but does not establish clinical efficacy by modern standards.

dosage.duration_notes: Traditionally used for extended periods; typical supplementation ranges from weeks to months for kidney stone support
dosage.special_populations: Not established in pregnancy/lactation; avoid in young children without medical guidance

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to Phyllanthus or Euphorbiaceae family plants
Concurrent use with immunosuppressive drugs (theoretical concern)

medications: May interact with medications metabolized by liver (CYP450 enzymes)
Caution with anticoagulants or antiplatelets (theoretical risk)
Possible interaction with blood pressure medications
supplements: High-dose vitamin C (may increase oxalate levels)

name: Chaste Berry Extract
summary: Chaste berry (Vitex agnus castus) is a traditional herbal extract from the fruit used to support hormonal balance and menstrual cycle regulation in women. Clinical evidence supports benefit for premenstrual symptoms (PMS) and breast tenderness.
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs demonstrate efficacy for PMS symptoms, particularly breast pain and mood changes. Evidence is good for short-term use (3-6 months); long-term safety data is more limited. Considered one of the better-evidenced herbal options for women's hormonal support.

dosage.typical_range: 20-40 mg daily (standardized extract) or 400-500 mg fruit powder daily
dosage.timing: Typically once daily in the morning; consistent daily use is key
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; morning dosing preferred
dosage.duration_notes: Effects typically observed after 3-6 cycles of menstruation; requires consistent use over weeks/months
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in pregnancy; controversial in nursing; avoid with estrogen-dependent conditions

contraindications: Nursing (effects on milk supply unclear; use cautiously)

medications: Dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, metoclopramide) — potential opposing effects
Hormone replacement therapy — possible reduced efficacy or additive effects
Antipsychotics — theoretical interaction via dopamine pathways
supplements: Other hormone-modulating herbs (black cohosh, dong quai)

name: Chasteberry Extract
summary: A traditional herbal extract from the chasteberry fruit used primarily for hormonal balance and menstrual cycle support. Moderate clinical evidence suggests potential benefit for menstrual symptoms, though high-quality RCTs remain limited.
mechanism_of_action: Believed to influence dopamine and prolactin regulation
Mechanisms not fully elucidated in humans
evidence_notes: Several moderate-quality trials and systematic reviews suggest benefit for PMS and menstrual symptoms, but evidence quality is limited by small sample sizes, heterogeneous outcomes, and lack of large RCTs. Mechanism of action remains incompletely understood.

dosage.typical_range: 400-500 mg daily (equivalent to 500 mg chasteberry; 1:1 extract as noted)
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Typically requires 3-6 months of consistent use for full benefit; effects may persist after discontinuation
dosage.special_populations: Not recommended during pregnancy or lactation; avoid if taking dopamine agonists or antipsychotics

contraindications: Pregnancy and lactation (traditional precaution; limited safety data)

medications: Dopamine antagonists (antipsychotics, metoclopramide) — may reduce efficacy or cause antagonistic effects
Hormone replacement therapy or oral contraceptives (limited data; theoretical interaction)
supplements: Other hormonal support herbs (limited evidence of additive effects)

name: Chicory
summary: Chicory root extract is a natural source of inulin, a prebiotic fiber that may support digestive health and beneficial gut bacteria. Evidence for its efficacy is moderate, with some studies showing improvements in bowel regularity and microbiota composition.
mechanism_of_action: Inulin acts as a prebiotic, selectively promoting growth of beneficial Bifidobacteria and Lactobacillus
Increases fecal bulk and stool frequency
Modulates gut pH and short-chain fatty acid production
evidence_notes: Chicory root/inulin has good clinical evidence from multiple RCTs demonstrating prebiotic effects and improvements in gut microbiota composition. Well-established mechanism and consistent positive outcomes in digestive health studies.

dosage.typical_range: 2–15 grams inulin daily (from chicory root extract or whole root)
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; with food may reduce GI discomfort
dosage.duration_notes: Ongoing daily use; effects on microbiota typically observed after 2–4 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Start with lower doses (2–5 g) and titrate up gradually to minimize initial bloating or gas; not recommended for patients with fructose malabsorption or FODMAP sensitivity
