Hyaluronic acid — complementary for skin hydration support

name: Hydrolyzed Collagen (Porcine)
summary: Hydrolyzed porcine collagen is a bioavailable source of collagen peptides rich in glycine, proline, and hydroxyproline that may support joint, skin, and gut health. Moderate evidence supports benefits for skin elasticity and joint comfort, particularly when combined with vitamin C.
mechanism_of_action: Provides bioavailable amino acids (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline) that serve as building blocks for collagen synthesis
May stimulate endogenous collagen production via fibroblast activity
Supports extracellular matrix integrity in skin and joints
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs and systematic reviews show moderate evidence for skin hydration, elasticity, and joint pain reduction. Benefits are most consistent when combined with vitamin C and sustained over 8-12 weeks. Evidence quality varies by outcome; skin benefits better-established than bone outcomes.

dosage.typical_range: 10-20 g daily (equivalent to 2-3 scoops of typical powders)
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; consistency more important than timing
dosage.with_food: No requirement; can be mixed into beverages or taken with meals
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically appear after 4-8 weeks of consistent use; optimal results at 8-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe for most adults; not well-studied in pregnancy/lactation

contraindications: Strict vegetarians/vegans should avoid porcine-derived sources
Those with pork allergies should use alternative collagen sources

supplements: Vitamin C: enhances collagen synthesis when combined
Hyaluronic acid: complementary for skin hydration

name: Hydrolyzed Collagen Type II
summary: Hydrolyzed Type II collagen is a processed form of collagen derived primarily from chicken cartilage, broken down into smaller peptides for improved bioavailability. It is formulated to support joint cartilage integrity and resilience.
mechanism_of_action: Provides bioavailable collagen peptides that accumulate in articular cartilage
May trigger immune tolerance to type II collagen, reducing cartilage-degrading immune responses
Supplies amino acids (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline) needed for collagen synthesis
evidence_notes: Solid evidence from RCTs showing improvements in joint comfort and cartilage markers. Some evidence suggests oral tolerance induction (particularly for undenatured UC-II form). Effect sizes are moderate; benefits depend on consistent use and adequate duration.

dosage.typical_range: 1,000–3,000 mg daily (10 mg undenatured type II collagen per capsule in UC-II formulations; 1,500–2,000 mg hydrolyzed form more common)
dosage.timing: Morning dosing with breakfast is standard; can be taken anytime
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with vitamin C enhances collagen synthesis
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically appear after 8–12 weeks of consistent use; some studies show cumulative benefit up to 16+ weeks
dosage.special_populations: Safe in most populations; generally recognized as safe in pregnancy based on collagen safety data. Avoid if poultry allergy present. Consult provider if immunocompromised (some evidence suggests immune modulation).

contraindications: Known shellfish allergy if processing environment involves cross-contamination

medications: Immunosuppressants: theoretical concern if UC-II form used (oral tolerance mechanism); monitor with healthcare provider

name: Hydrolyzed Marine Collagen Peptides
summary: Enzymatically broken-down collagen from fish sources with smaller molecular weight for enhanced absorption and bioavailability. Provides amino acids (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline) that support connective tissue synthesis.
mechanism_of_action: Provides collagen amino acids for tissue repair
Supplies glycine and proline for collagen cross-linking
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs show improvements in skin elasticity, hydration, and joint comfort. Marine source has high bioavailability due to smaller peptide size. Meta-analyses support efficacy for skin and joint outcomes.

dosage.timing: Any time; consistent daily intake recommended
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; mixes easily in beverages
dosage.duration_notes: Best results with consistent use over 8-12 weeks; benefits continue with ongoing use
dosage.special_populations: Safe for most populations; those with fish allergies should avoid or verify processing

supplements: Synergistic with Vitamin C (required for collagen synthesis)

name: Hydroxocobalamin Acetate
summary: Hydroxocobalamin is a naturally occurring form of vitamin B12 that plays essential roles in energy metabolism, nervous system function, and red blood cell formation. The acetate salt improves stability and bioavailability compared to standard cyanocobalamin.
primary_uses: Red blood cell formation and oxygen transport
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor in methylation reactions and DNA synthesis
Essential for myelin formation and nerve function
Supports conversion of homocysteine to methionine
Participates in energy metabolism via citric acid cycle
evidence_notes: Vitamin B12 has extensive RCT evidence for preventing deficiency and supporting energy, cognition, and neurological health. Hydroxocobalamin is the preferred form in clinical settings due to superior tissue retention and conversion efficiency compared to cyanocobalamin.

dosage.timing: With breakfast or first meal of the day for optimal absorption
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; sublingual forms bypass digestive absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Vitamin B12 stores in the liver, so daily supplementation for maintenance or 6-12 weeks for deficiency repletion
dosage.special_populations: Vegans and vegetarians have higher requirements; older adults may have reduced intrinsic factor and benefit from supplemental forms; pernicious anemia patients require medical supervision

common_side_effects: Occasional mild headache or dizziness at high doses
Acne-like skin reactions in sensitive individuals (very rare)
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to cobalamin or acetate
Leber's disease (hereditary optic neuropathy) — avoid due to risk of worsening vision

medications: Proton pump inhibitors and H2 blockers — may impair intrinsic factor-mediated absorption
Certain antibiotics (rifampin, aminoglycosides) — may reduce B12 levels
supplements: Calcium and B12 absorption — ensure adequate calcium for optimal B12 uptake
Potassium — may compete for absorption if taken simultaneously in large doses

contraindications: Iron deficiency (IP6 may reduce iron absorption)

medications: No major known interactions, but may theoretically affect mineral-dependent medications
supplements: Iron supplements (separate by 2+ hours; IP6 may reduce absorption)
Zinc supplements (separate by 2+ hours; IP6 may reduce absorption)
Calcium/magnesium supplements (IP6 naturally complexed with these minerals; monitor levels)

name: Ipriflavone
summary: Synthetic isoflavone studied for bone density. May support bone formation and reduce breakdown.
evidence_notes: Synthetic isoflavone for bones

dosage.special_populations: Postmenopausal, osteoporosis risk

serious_warnings: Monitor lymphocyte counts with long-term use

name: Iron
summary: Essential for oxygen transport and immune cell function, but excess can feed pathogens. Important to test before supplementing.
evidence_notes: Essential mineral critical for oxygen transport

dosage.timing: Empty stomach or with vitamin C
dosage.special_populations: Women, vegetarians

therapeutic_dose.label: 60-200 mg elemental
therapeutic_dose.notes: Iron-deficiency anemia; alt-day dosing better absorbed

upper_limit.label: 45 mg
upper_limit.notes: Above this risks GI distress; do not supplement without testing ferritin

name: Iron (Bisglycinate)
summary: Highly absorbable iron form that's gentle on the stomach. Best choice for those who experience GI upset with other iron forms.
evidence_notes: Well-absorbed with minimal GI effects
mechanism_of_action: Doesn't cause constipation like other forms

dosage.timing: Empty stomach or with vitamin C
dosage.with_food: Can take with food if GI upset
dosage.special_populations: Menstruating women, vegetarians

citations.title: Iron bisglycinate bioavailability comparison
citations.finding: Iron bisglycinate shows superior absorption with fewer GI side effects

citations.title: Iron supplementation for anemia
citations.finding: Iron supplementation effectively treats iron-deficiency anemia

name: Iron (as Fermented Iron Bisglycinate)
summary: Iron is an essential mineral critical for oxygen transport and fetal development during pregnancy. The bisglycinate chelate form offers enhanced absorption and bioavailability with reduced GI irritation compared to ferrous salts.
primary_uses: Fetal development and birth weight support
mechanism_of_action: Reduced ionic irritation to GI tract
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence for iron supplementation in pregnancy; strong evidence that adequate iron prevents anemia and poor birth outcomes; chelated forms reduce side effects while maintaining efficacy.

dosage.timing: morning on empty stomach preferred for absorption, but can take with food if GI upset occurs
dosage.with_food: separate from calcium, zinc, and polyphenols by 2 hours when possible; vitamin C enhances absorption
dosage.duration_notes: throughout pregnancy and postpartum; continue if breastfeeding
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant women 27 mg/day; lactating women 9–10 mg/day; dosage may increase if anemia confirmed

contraindications: sickle cell disease without supplementation indication

medications: ACE inhibitors, some antibiotics may be affected by timing
supplements: Calcium, zinc, copper (spacing recommended); vitamin C enhances absorption

name: Iron (as Ferrous Fumarate)
summary: Ferrous fumarate is a highly bioavailable form of iron used to prevent and treat iron deficiency anemia by supporting oxygen transport and energy metabolism. It is one of the most absorbable iron salts available.
mechanism_of_action: component of hemoglobin for oxygen transport
component of myoglobin for muscle oxygen storage
evidence_notes: Iron deficiency anemia treatment is supported by extensive clinical evidence; ferrous fumarate specifically has strong bioavailability data.

dosage.typical_range: 18-27 mg elemental iron daily (adult women); 8-11 mg daily (adult men)
dosage.timing: take on empty stomach for optimal absorption, or with small amount of food if GI upset occurs
dosage.with_food: preferably without food, but avoid dairy, calcium, and coffee which reduce absorption
dosage.duration_notes: treatment duration depends on deficiency severity; typically several months for repletion
dosage.special_populations: pregnant women: 27 mg daily; children require age-adjusted dosing; avoid in hereditary hemochromatosis

name: Iron (Ferrous Sulfate)
summary: An essential mineral critical for oxygen transport, energy production, and immune function. Ferrous sulfate is a highly bioavailable form commonly used in supplemental iron products.
primary_uses: Iron deficiency anemia prevention and treatment
mechanism_of_action: Core component of hemoglobin and myoglobin
Cofactor for cytochrome oxidase and other enzymes
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence for iron deficiency anemia treatment and prevention. Strong evidence for efficacy of ferrous salts. Well-established hematologic and biochemical benefits.

dosage.typical_range: 8-18 mg daily (recommended dietary allowance); 25-325 mg daily for anemia treatment
dosage.timing: Take on empty stomach 1 hour before or 2 hours after meals for optimal absorption; with food if GI upset occurs
dosage.with_food: Food reduces absorption by ~40-50%; vitamin C enhances absorption; avoid calcium, tea, coffee which inhibit absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Supplement duration depends on severity of deficiency; treatment typically 3-6 months; maintenance dose for prevention
dosage.special_populations: Women 19-50 require 18 mg daily; postmenopausal women and men require 8 mg daily; pregnant women require 27 mg daily; children require 7-11 mg daily based on age

medications: Tetracycline and fluoroquinolone antibiotics (reduce antibiotic absorption)
supplements: Calcium, zinc, magnesium reduce iron absorption
Vitamin C increases ferrous iron absorption
Black tea, green tea, coffee inhibit absorption
Separate dosing by 2+ hours from other minerals

name: Jerusalem Artichoke Fiber
summary: A prebiotic fiber extract from the Jerusalem artichoke tuber, composed primarily of inulin and oligofructose. Strongly stimulates beneficial bacteria and supports digestive health and glycemic control.
mechanism_of_action: Inulin and oligofructose are selectively fermented by Bifidobacterium and Lactobacillus
Resistant to small intestinal digestion; fermented in colon
SCFA production improves colonic pH and microbial balance
Enhanced satiety and reduced postprandial glucose response
evidence_notes: Strong evidence from multiple RCTs demonstrating prebiotic efficacy, improved insulin sensitivity, and lipid markers. Inulin is one of the best-characterized prebiotic fibers in clinical literature.

dosage.timing: Any time of day; divide dose if > 10 g
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; mix with liquid or food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; gradual titration (start 2–3 g, increase over 1–2 weeks) reduces digestive upset
dosage.special_populations: FODMAP-sensitive individuals may experience bloating; start with very low dose. May be better tolerated in IBS if introduced slowly.

common_side_effects: Bloating and distention (common with inulin supplementation)
contraindications: Severe FODMAP intolerance or IBS (use cautiously)
Ragweed allergy (cross-reactivity risk; Jerusalem artichoke is Helianthus family)

medications: May reduce absorption of oral medications if taken simultaneously (separate by 1-2 hours)
supplements: Other inulin or high-FODMAP prebiotic sources (increased fermentation and gas)

name: Joint Foundation Stack
summary: Essential joint support combination: glucosamine, chondroitin, and MSM. The classic combo.
evidence_notes: Most popular joint stack

dosage.special_populations: OA, joint maintenance, active adults

citations.title: Joint supplement combinations
citations.finding: Glucosamine + chondroitin provide modest joint benefits

citations.title: Multimodal joint support
citations.finding: Combination approaches may enhance individual effects

name: Full Spectrum Kanna Extract (Sceletium tortuosum)
summary: Kanna is a South African succulent plant containing alkaloids (primarily mesembrine) traditionally used for mood support and anxiety relief. Modern research suggests potential serotonergic activity, though human clinical evidence remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Potential SSRI-like activity via serotonin reuptake inhibition
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials; most evidence is in vitro or animal studies. A few small human studies suggest mood and anxiety benefits, but larger RCTs are needed for stronger evidence grading. Full spectrum extracts with defined alkaloid content are better characterized than crude forms.

dosage.typical_range: 100-500 mg daily of extract standardized to 5% alkaloids
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Effects may develop over days to weeks with consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should avoid; use caution if taking serotonergic medications

contraindications: Concurrent SSRI/SNRI use (potential serotonin syndrome risk)

supplements: Other mood-supporting herbs with serotonergic effects

name: Kava
summary: A Pacific island plant traditionally used for relaxation and stress relief, standardized to kavalactones which may modulate GABA and other neurotransmitter systems. Evidence supports anxiolytic effects, though long-term safety concerns regarding liver health warrant caution.
evidence_notes: Effective anxiolytic - liver monitoring advised

dosage.special_populations: Anxiety, social situations

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Monitor liver - use noble kava only

name: Kelp
summary: Kelp is a natural source of iodine and trace minerals, supporting thyroid function and providing marine-derived nutrients.
evidence_notes: Well-established iodine source, thyroid support

dosage.special_populations: Iodine-deficient populations

practical_notes: Variable iodine content in unstandardized forms
Test thyroid before and during use

name: Keratin (Solubilized)
summary: Hair and nail protein in absorbable form. Provides building blocks for keratin structures.
evidence_notes: Emerging, less evidence than biotin

dosage.special_populations: Weak hair/nails

name: Konjac Root Extract (Amorphophallus konjac)
summary: Konjac root extract is a soluble dietary fiber derived from the konjac plant, primarily composed of glucomannan, which absorbs water and forms a viscous gel in the digestive tract. Evidence supports its use for promoting digestive health, blood sugar management, and modest weight loss support.
mechanism_of_action: High-viscosity soluble fiber that absorbs water and slows gastric emptying
Increases fecal bulk and promotes bowel regularity
Reduces glycemic response by slowing carbohydrate absorption
May lower cholesterol by binding bile acids
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs and systematic reviews support efficacy for bowel regularity, modest glycemic control, and cholesterol reduction. Evidence for weight loss is moderate but present. Well-established safety profile with decades of use in food applications.

dosage.typical_range: 3-15 g daily (1.5-5 g per serving, 2-3 times daily)
dosage.timing: Take with meals or separate doses throughout the day
dosage.with_food: Must be taken with at least 8 oz (240 ml) of water or liquid; can be mixed into beverages
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; effects on bowel regularity typically observed within 3-7 days
dosage.special_populations: Start with lower doses (3-5 g daily) and increase gradually to minimize gas and bloating; persons with difficulty swallowing should avoid or use with caution; inadequate fluid intake increases choking and obstruction risk

contraindications: Severe dehydration or inadequate fluid intake

medications: May slow absorption of oral medications if taken simultaneously; separate by 1-2 hours
Potential interaction with antidiabetic medications (additive blood sugar lowering effect)
supplements: May reduce absorption of fat-soluble vitamins if taken simultaneously; separate dosing by 1-2 hours recommended

name: Krill Oil
summary: Omega-3s from Antarctic krill in phospholipid form with astaxanthin. Potentially enhanced absorption.
evidence_notes: Phospholipid form may enhance bioavailability

dosage.special_populations: Shellfish allergy contraindicated

name: Krill Oil (NKO®)
summary: Krill oil is a marine source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) bound to phospholipids, which may enhance bioavailability compared to fish oil triglycerides. NKO® is a patented, sustainably harvested krill oil extract standardized for phospholipid content.
mechanism_of_action: EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids reduce inflammatory mediators
Phospholipid form enhances cellular membrane incorporation
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs support omega-3 benefits for cardiovascular and cognitive health. Krill oil specifically shows good evidence for lipid profiles and joint comfort. Phospholipid delivery form has emerging evidence for superior absorption vs. triglyceride fish oil.

dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble and better absorbed with dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent daily use recommended; benefits typically observed after 4–12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult provider; those on anticoagulants should discuss with healthcare provider

contraindications: High-dose anticoagulant therapy without medical consultation

medications: Warfarin and other anticoagulants (additive antiplatelet effects)
supplements: Ginkgo biloba, garlic extract, turmeric (additive antiplatelet properties)

name: Kudzu Root Extract
summary: Kudzu root extract contains isoflavones (particularly daidzein and puerarin) that have been traditionally used in Asian medicine for various purposes. Modern research shows preliminary evidence for cardiovascular and metabolic applications, though human clinical data remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Isoflavone content (puerarin, daidzein) may support vascular function and endothelial health
May influence glucose and lipid metabolism pathways
evidence_notes: Limited high-quality human RCT data. Most evidence comes from in vitro and animal studies, with some small human trials showing potential for cardiovascular and metabolic markers. Insufficient evidence for strong clinical recommendations.

dosage.timing: With meals for potential absorption benefit
dosage.with_food: Recommended to reduce potential GI irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety profile not extensively studied in humans; typical supplementation periods range 4–12 weeks in available trials
dosage.special_populations: Not established for pregnancy, breastfeeding, or pediatric use; use caution in individuals with hormone-sensitive conditions

contraindications: Hormone-sensitive cancers or conditions (due to phytoestrogen content)
Individuals taking hormone replacement therapy (potential interaction)

medications: Hormone therapies (estrogen, tamoxifen) — potential additive estrogenic effects
Antidiabetic medications — monitor for additive glucose-lowering effects
supplements: Other phytoestrogen-rich supplements (red clover, black cohosh) — additive hormonal effects possible

name: L-Alanine
summary: L-alanine is a non-essential amino acid involved in protein synthesis and glucose metabolism via the alanine cycle. Evidence for performance or cognitive benefits is limited and primarily theoretical.
mechanism_of_action: Serves as a building block for protein synthesis
Participates in the alanine cycle for glucose production during exercise
evidence_notes: L-alanine is an endogenous amino acid with established biochemical roles, but direct supplementation lacks robust clinical evidence for performance or health benefits in healthy populations. Most studies focus on beta-alanine (a different compound) rather than L-alanine itself.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; no critical timing window
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: No established optimal duration; safe for long-term use as part of normal diet
dosage.special_populations: No specific contraindications in healthy adults

name: L-Arginine
summary: A conditionally essential amino acid that serves as a precursor to nitric oxide, supporting vasodilation and blood flow. Most evidence supports benefits for endothelial function and exercise performance, though clinical results are mixed.
mechanism_of_action: Converted to nitric oxide by NOS
evidence_notes: Nitric oxide precursor

dosage.timing: Before exercise or split through day
dosage.special_populations: Cardiovascular, athletes, ED

name: L-Arginine (Blood Pressure)
summary: Nitric oxide precursor for vasodilation and blood pressure support.
evidence_notes: NO precursor for vasodilation

dosage.special_populations: Mild hypertension, circulation

name: L-Arginine (Sexual Function)
summary: Amino acid that boosts nitric oxide for blood flow, supporting erectile function.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for mild erectile dysfunction

dosage.with_food: Away from food for better absorption
dosage.special_populations: Mild ED, cardiovascular support

name: L-Aspartic Acid
summary: L-aspartic acid is a non-essential amino acid involved in protein synthesis, neurotransmitter function, and energy metabolism. Limited direct evidence supports supplementation for athletic performance or testosterone enhancement in healthy individuals.
mechanism_of_action: Component of purines and pyrimidines for nucleotide synthesis
evidence_notes: L-aspartic acid is naturally occurring and essential for normal physiology, but direct supplementation evidence in healthy humans is limited. Most research conflates it with D-aspartic acid (DAA), which has been studied more heavily with mixed results for testosterone. Insufficient RCT data to support specific performance claims.

dosage.timing: Any time; often taken with meals or as part of amino acid blends
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use as part of normal protein intake; no established protocol for supplemental dosing
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for most populations; pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare providers

common_side_effects: Minimal side effects reported in literature
High doses may cause gastrointestinal distress
contraindications: Individuals with kidney disease should consult healthcare provider before supplementing

medications: No significant known interactions at typical supplemental doses
supplements: No major interactions with other amino acids or common supplements at standard dosages

name: L Carnitine
summary: L-carnitine is a naturally occurring compound synthesized from amino acids that plays a crucial role in transporting long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for energy production. Evidence supports modest benefits for exercise performance and recovery in carnitine-deficient individuals, though benefits in healthy populations are limited.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for male fertility, particularly sperm motility

dosage.special_populations: Male fertility, sperm motility issues, oligospermia

upper_limit.label: No established UL
upper_limit.notes: Consider TMAO production with high doses

name: L-Carnitine Fumarate
summary: L-carnitine is a compound synthesized from amino acids that facilitates the transport of long-chain fatty acids into mitochondria for energy production. L-carnitine fumarate is a salt form that may have improved bioavailability compared to base L-carnitine.
mechanism_of_action: Transports long-chain fatty acids across the inner mitochondrial membrane (CPT I system) for β-oxidation
Enhances cellular energy production from fat metabolism
May improve exercise capacity and reduce fatigue
evidence_notes: L-carnitine has moderate-to-good evidence for improving endurance performance and fatty acid oxidation in trained athletes and certain clinical populations. The fumarate form offers no additional advantage over standard L-carnitine based on current evidence, but the salt may improve absorption.

dosage.timing: Split into 2–3 doses; pre-workout timing may be beneficial for performance studies
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may improve absorption with carbohydrates and protein
dosage.duration_notes: Studies typically assess benefits after 2–4 weeks of consistent use; effects may increase with longer duration
dosage.special_populations: Vegetarians and vegans may benefit most as carnitine is primarily dietary in origin. Dose adjustments may be needed in renal disease; consult healthcare provider.

common_side_effects: Fishy body odor (due to TMAO production)
contraindications: Seizure disorders (historical concern; current evidence is inconclusive)
Advanced kidney disease (risk of carnitine accumulation)
Hypersensitivity to carnitine or fumaric acid components

medications: Anticonvulsants (phenytoin, valproic acid) may reduce carnitine levels
Dialysis may deplete carnitine in patients with renal failure

name: L-Carnosine
summary: L-carnosine is a dipeptide composed of alanine and histidine found naturally in muscle and brain tissue, with evidence suggesting roles in antioxidant defense and metal chelation. Research supports potential benefits for muscle function and age-related decline, though human clinical data remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Intracellular buffering of hydrogen ions and lactic acid
evidence_notes: L-carnosine has established biochemical roles in muscle and brain, with in vitro and animal evidence supporting antioxidant and buffering effects. Human clinical trials are limited and mixed; most evidence is preliminary or mechanistic rather than from large RCTs demonstrating clinical benefit in humans.

dosage.timing: Divided doses with meals optimal for absorption
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; consistent timing recommended
dosage.duration_notes: Typical research protocols span 4–12 weeks; long-term safety profile requires further study
dosage.special_populations: Not established in children or pregnant/nursing women; may have limited bioavailability in vegetarians/vegans due to dietary scarcity

common_side_effects: Rare reports of mild gastrointestinal upset
contraindications: Caution in individuals with histamine sensitivity or mast cell disorders (carnosine may trigger release)

name: L-Citrulline
summary: More effective nitric oxide booster than arginine. Converts to arginine while bypassing liver metabolism.
evidence_notes: Superior to arginine for NO production

dosage.typical_range: 3-6 g citrulline or 6-8 g citrulline malate
dosage.special_populations: Athletes, blood flow support

name: L-Citrulline Malate (2:1)
summary: A non-protein amino acid bound to malic acid (2:1 ratio) that enhances nitric oxide production and ATP regeneration in muscle tissue. Emerging evidence suggests superior bioavailability and endurance benefits compared to L-arginine.
mechanism_of_action: L-citrulline converts to L-arginine in kidneys, bypassing hepatic degradation for superior NO production
Malic acid supports ATP synthesis in mitochondria (TCA cycle intermediate)
Enhanced ammonia clearance, reducing fatigue metabolites
Improved vascular endothelial function and blood flow
Reduced protein degradation in muscle tissue
evidence_notes: Good RCT evidence for endurance performance, fatigue reduction, and DOMS. Multiple studies show citrulline malate 2:1 improves exercise output and recovery better than placebo. Better evidence base than arginine for athletic performance.

dosage.typical_range: 6-8 grams daily (providing 4-5.3 g citrulline and 2-2.7 g malate)
dosage.timing: 60-90 minutes before exercise for endurance; can divide daily dose
dosage.with_food: Better absorbed on empty stomach; take with water or beverage
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits may appear within 7-14 days of consistent use; full effects by 2-4 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally very well-tolerated; safe for most populations including athletes; minimal contraindications

common_side_effects: Mild nausea (more common at doses >8 g daily)
contraindications: Theoretical caution with severe renal dysfunction (as with all amino acids)

medications: Phosphodiesterase inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) — additive vasodilation; monitor BP
Antihypertensive medications — may enhance blood pressure reduction
Anticoagulants — minimal interaction; theoretical increased bleeding risk
supplements: L-Arginine or Arginine AKG — complementary NO pathway; may have additive effect
Creatine monohydrate — synergistic for strength and endurance
Pomegranate extract — complementary antioxidant and NO support

name: L-Citrulline (Erectile Support)
summary: Amino acid that converts to arginine more efficiently, supporting nitric oxide for erections.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for erectile support, may be better than arginine
mechanism_of_action: May be more effective than arginine

dosage.typical_range: 1.5-3g L-Citrulline daily for erectile (higher for exercise)
dosage.special_populations: Erectile support, exercise performance

serious_warnings: Less likely to trigger herpes than arginine

practical_notes: May be more effective than arginine

name: L-Cysteine HCl Anhydrous
summary: L-Cysteine HCl is a semi-essential amino acid that serves as a precursor to glutathione, a major cellular antioxidant. It plays roles in protein synthesis, detoxification, and hair/skin/nail health.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for cysteine's role in glutathione synthesis and antioxidant support from mechanistic and clinical studies. RCT evidence exists for specific conditions (acetaminophen toxicity, respiratory health) though most general wellness uses are supported by moderate evidence.

dosage.with_food: Can take with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; effects on antioxidant status build over weeks
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with homocystinuria should avoid or use under medical supervision

contraindications: Pregnancy and lactation (limited safety data; use only if medically indicated)

medications: Acetaminophen (cysteine depletes with high-dose acetaminophen use; may protect against toxicity at therapeutic doses)
Cisplatin and other chemotherapy agents (enhanced nephrotoxicity risk reported in some studies)
supplements: N-Acetyl-L-Cysteine (NAC) — avoid concurrent use; redundant glutathione pathway activity

name: L-Cystine
summary: Oxidized dimer form of cysteine, important for keratin production, hair/nail/skin health, and antioxidant support via glutathione synthesis.
evidence_notes: Established role in keratin synthesis and sulfur metabolism
mechanism_of_action: Reduced to cysteine in the body
Structural component of hair and nails

dosage.special_populations: Hair/nail support

practical_notes: Often combined with methionine for hair formulas
NAC is more bioavailable for glutathione

name: L-Glutamic Acid
summary: L-glutamic acid is a non-essential amino acid and excitatory neurotransmitter that plays roles in protein synthesis, energy metabolism, and nervous system function. Evidence for isolated supplementation in healthy populations is limited, though glutamate is naturally abundant in dietary protein sources.
mechanism_of_action: Serves as an excitatory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system
Involved in amino acid metabolism and nitrogen transport
evidence_notes: L-glutamic acid is an established amino acid with clear biochemical roles, but direct RCT evidence for isolated supplementation benefits in healthy populations is limited. Most research focuses on glutamate's role in pathological conditions or as part of protein/food matrices rather than supplemental form.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; often taken with meals
dosage.with_food: May be better absorbed with food containing carbohydrates
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use as it is a dietary amino acid
dosage.special_populations: No specific adjustments needed; naturally present in high-protein foods

common_side_effects: Excessive amounts may cause headaches or gastrointestinal discomfort
contraindications: No major contraindications at nutritional doses
Those with severe kidney disease should consult healthcare provider before high-dose supplementation

supplements: May interact with other amino acids for absorption if taken in large excess

name: L-Glutamine
summary: Most abundant amino acid in the body. Critical for gut lining integrity, immune function, and muscle recovery.
evidence_notes: Important for gut and muscle

dosage.special_populations: Gut issues, athletes, critical illness

supplements: Works with zinc carnosine for gut

name: L-Glutamine (Gut Repair)
summary: Primary fuel for intestinal cells, essential for gut barrier repair and reducing intestinal permeability.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for gut barrier support

dosage.timing: Away from protein meals or divided throughout day
dosage.duration_notes: High dose 2-3 months for healing, then maintenance
dosage.special_populations: Leaky gut, IBD, post-antibiotic, athletes

name: L-Glutathione Reduced
summary: L-Glutathione is a tripeptide antioxidant naturally produced in cells that helps neutralize free radicals and support detoxification processes. Oral bioavailability is limited due to rapid degradation in the digestive tract, though some studies suggest reduced forms may be better absorbed than oxidized forms.
mechanism_of_action: Direct antioxidant activity via conjugation with free radicals and oxidative species
Cofactor for glutathione peroxidase and glutathione S-transferase enzymes
Regeneration of other antioxidants (vitamins C and E)
Protein synthesis and DNA repair support
evidence_notes: Oral glutathione has limited bioavailability; most human studies show modest systemic absorption. Some evidence supports IV forms in clinical settings, but oral supplementation evidence is preliminary. Well-established physiological importance does not translate to strong oral supplement efficacy data.

dosage.timing: Any time of day; with or without food
dosage.with_food: No strong preference; some sources suggest taking with vitamin C to enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safety data supports continuous use; optimal duration for therapeutic benefit unclear
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult practitioners; limited safety data in pediatric populations

common_side_effects: Generally well tolerated at typical doses
contraindications: Avoid in individuals taking cisplatin or other chemotherapies (may reduce efficacy)
Caution in patients with cystinuria or homocystinuria
Limited data in pregnancy—consult healthcare provider

medications: Chemotherapy agents (may reduce drug efficacy—consult oncologist)
Antiretroviral medications (potential interaction; use with medical supervision)
supplements: Vitamin C: may enhance glutathione absorption and recycling
Milk thistle/silymarin: synergistic hepatoprotective effects possible
N-Acetylcysteine (NAC): complementary precursor; combining may be redundant

name: L-Glycine
summary: A simple, non-essential amino acid with documented sleep-promoting properties through thermoregulation and GABA receptor modulation. Evidence supports its use for improving sleep latency and quality.
mechanism_of_action: Promotes vasodilation and heat dissipation (core temperature reduction)
evidence_notes: Good RCT evidence (Japanese trials) showing 3g glycine reduces sleep latency and improves subjective sleep quality; mechanism well-characterized.

dosage.with_food: Can take with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; benefits may increase with consistent use over 2+ weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated across populations

common_side_effects: Well-tolerated; minimal side effects at 3-10g doses
Mild GI effects possible at very high doses

medications: Clozapine (rare case reports of seizure-like activity)

name: L-Histidine
summary: L-Histidine is an essential amino acid precursor to histamine and carnosine, involved in immune function, wound healing, and neurotransmission. Evidence for isolated supplementation in healthy adults is limited, though histidine is necessary for normal physiological function.
mechanism_of_action: Combines with beta-alanine to form carnosine
evidence_notes: Established physiological roles in multiple systems

dosage.special_populations: Those with histamine issues use caution

serious_warnings: May increase histamine in sensitive individuals

name: L-Isoleucine
summary: Essential branched-chain amino acid (BCAA) important for muscle protein synthesis, energy production during exercise, and immune function.
evidence_notes: Well-studied as part of BCAAs for exercise performance
mechanism_of_action: Activates mTOR pathway for muscle protein synthesis
Oxidized in muscle for energy during exercise

dosage.special_populations: Athletes, those in caloric deficit

supplements: Works with leucine and valine as BCAAs

practical_notes: Usually taken as part of BCAA blend

name: L-Lysine
summary: Essential amino acid important for collagen synthesis, calcium absorption, and herpes virus suppression.
evidence_notes: Established for herpes prevention
mechanism_of_action: Competes with arginine for herpes virus

dosage.special_populations: Herpes sufferers, vegetarians

name: L-Lysine
summary: Essential amino acid important for protein synthesis, collagen formation, calcium absorption, and immune function, particularly noted for antiviral properties.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for cold sore prevention, protein synthesis
mechanism_of_action: Competes with arginine for viral replication

dosage.special_populations: Those prone to cold sores

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Caution with kidney disease at high doses

practical_notes: Higher doses during cold sore outbreaks

name: L-Ornithine α-Ketoglutarate
summary: L-Ornithine α-Ketoglutarate is a salt combining the amino acid L-ornithine with α-ketoglutarate, theoretically supporting protein synthesis and muscle recovery. Limited clinical evidence exists for its efficacy in healthy populations, with most data from small or older studies.
mechanism_of_action: Provides ornithine precursor for polyamine synthesis and collagen formation
Supplies α-ketoglutarate to support amino acid metabolism and TCA cycle function
May enhance arginine synthesis through the urea cycle
evidence_notes: Some clinical studies show modest benefits for wound healing and post-surgery recovery in stressed populations, but evidence in healthy athletes is limited and inconsistent. Most studies are small, older, or conducted in surgical/hospitalized patients rather than healthy supplement users.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with carbohydrate or protein may enhance amino acid utilization
dosage.duration_notes: Typically taken for 4–8 weeks during training phases; longer-term safety data is limited
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnant/nursing women, children, and those with urea cycle disorders not well established

common_side_effects: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses
contraindications: Urea cycle disorders or arginine/ornithine metabolism conditions
Use with caution in individuals with liver dysfunction

medications: No major interactions documented; however, arginine-modulating compounds may have additive effects
supplements: May have complementary effects with other amino acids (leucine, arginine) and creatine

name: L-Ornithine Hydrochloride
summary: L-Ornithine is a non-essential amino acid involved in the urea cycle and polyamine synthesis, with some evidence suggesting potential benefits for athletic performance and muscle recovery. Research on ornithine supplementation is limited and mixed, with most studies examining small populations.
mechanism_of_action: Substrate for urea cycle and nitrogen metabolism
Potential stimulator of growth hormone release
Support for creatine and arginine metabolism
evidence_notes: Limited clinical research with mostly small trials. Some studies suggest benefits for ammonia clearance and endurance performance, but evidence is preliminary and inconsistent. Not established as a primary performance supplement.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may be better tolerated with light food if GI sensitivity occurs
dosage.duration_notes: Typically studied in 4–8 week protocols; longer-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider; individuals with kidney disease or on dialysis should avoid without medical supervision due to nitrogen metabolism

common_side_effects: Gastrointestinal discomfort (nausea, diarrhea at higher doses)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment or end-stage kidney disease
Arginine-restricted diets (ornithine can compete with arginine metabolism)

medications: May interact with medications affecting ammonia metabolism
Potential interaction with growth hormone-altering drugs (theoretical)
supplements: Lysine (both compete for absorption; separate dosing recommended)

name: L-Ornithine L-Aspartate
summary: L-Ornithine L-Aspartate is a combined amino acid supplement studied primarily for its potential role in ammonia metabolism and liver health support. Evidence exists mainly in clinical settings for hepatic encephalopathy management, with limited data in healthy populations.
mechanism_of_action: Substrate for hepatic urea cycle enzymes to increase ammonia clearance
Supports nitrogen metabolism through ornithine and aspartate donation
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence exists in clinical hepatology for ammonia reduction in hepatic encephalopathy. Very limited evidence in healthy individuals or athletic populations. Few rigorous RCTs in consumer health applications.

dosage.with_food: Recommended to reduce potential gastrointestinal discomfort
dosage.duration_notes: Clinical studies typically span 4-12 weeks; optimal duration for healthy populations unclear
dosage.special_populations: Not recommended for children without medical supervision; consult healthcare provider if pregnant or nursing

contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to ornithine or aspartate

medications: May interact with medications affecting ammonia metabolism
Caution with lactulose and other hepatic encephalopathy treatments
supplements: Other ammonia-reducing amino acid combinations (additive effect)

name: L-Proline
summary: L-Proline is a non-essential amino acid that serves as a structural component of collagen and elastin, and is involved in protein synthesis and tissue repair. It has limited direct clinical evidence as an isolated supplement, though adequate proline intake supports connective tissue health.
mechanism_of_action: Acts as a structural amino acid in collagen (comprises ~10% of collagen)
Substrate for hydroxylation to form hydroxyproline, critical for collagen cross-linking
evidence_notes: L-Proline is biochemically essential for collagen formation with strong mechanistic support, but isolated supplementation lacks robust RCT evidence demonstrating superior outcomes versus dietary protein intake. Most evidence is derived from collagen peptide studies that include multiple amino acids. Direct proline supplementation studies are limited.

dosage.timing: Anytime; often combined with other collagen-supporting amino acids (glycine, hydroxyproline)
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Ongoing use typical for structural support; effects on skin and joint health may require 8–12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Adequate dosing may support recovery in athletes; individual needs vary based on dietary protein intake

common_side_effects: Minimal; occasional mild GI upset at high doses

supplements: Synergistic with glycine and hydroxyproline for collagen support
Complementary to vitamin C, copper, and iron (cofactors for collagen synthesis)

name: L-Serine
summary: L-serine is a non-essential amino acid that serves as a precursor for phosphatidylserine and neurotransmitter synthesis. Limited clinical evidence supports its use for cognitive or neurological support, with most research occurring in specialized medical contexts.
mechanism_of_action: Precursor for phosphatidylserine (PS) and sphingolipid synthesis
evidence_notes: L-serine is a well-characterized amino acid with established biochemical roles, but clinical evidence for supplementation benefits in healthy populations is limited and preliminary. Most human studies involve specialized populations (e.g., neurological disorders) rather than general wellness.

dosage.timing: Any time of day; can be taken with meals

summary: Magnesium bisglycinate is a highly absorbable form of magnesium bound to the amino acid glycine, which enhances intestinal absorption and reduces GI side effects compared to inorganic forms. evidence_notes: Magnesium has strong evidence for muscle function, migraine prevention, and cardiovascular support. The malate form is theoretical but plausible for energy-related applications.

dosage.timing: divided doses, evening dose may support sleep
dosage.with_food: can be taken with or without food
dosage.special_populations: kidney disease patients should consult provider; may help with pregnancy-related leg cramps

medications: fluoroquinolone and tetracycline antibiotics (separate by 2+ hours)

name: Magnesium (Elemental)
summary: Magnesium is an essential mineral involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions, including energy metabolism, protein synthesis, and nervous system function. Strong evidence supports magnesium supplementation for supporting sleep quality, muscle relaxation, and overall mineral status.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor for ATP synthesis and cellular energy production
Regulates neurotransmitter release and nerve transmission
Supports sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium regulation (muscle relaxation)
evidence_notes: Magnesium is one of the most extensively researched minerals with robust RCT evidence for sleep, muscle function, and stress management. Bisglycinate chelate is a highly bioavailable form with good gastrointestinal tolerance.

forms.form: Magnesium bisglycinate chelate monohydrate (chelated form with glycine, highly absorbable)

dosage.timing: Evening dose preferred for sleep support; can split across meals
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; bisglycinate form is gentle on stomach
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; effects on sleep may be noticeable within 2-4 weeks
dosage.special_populations: RDA: 400-420 mg (adult men), 310-320 mg (adult women); higher needs during pregnancy, stress, or intense exercise

common_side_effects: Loose stools or diarrhea (dose-dependent; rare with bisglycinate form)
Mild GI upset at high doses
contraindications: High-dose magnesium should be avoided within 2-4 hours of certain medications

medications: Bisphosphonates (osteoporosis drugs): separate intake by 2+ hours
Tetracycline and fluoroquinolone antibiotics: separate intake by 2+ hours
ACE inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics: monitor due to additive effects
supplements: Calcium: may compete for absorption; space out doses
Zinc: may compete for absorption in high doses

name: Magnesium Gluconate
summary: Magnesium gluconate is an organic salt form of magnesium with good bioavailability and mild gastrointestinal tolerability. Magnesium is an essential mineral involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions, including energy production, muscle function, and nervous system regulation.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor in ATP synthesis and energy metabolism
Regulation of muscle contraction and relaxation
Modulation of NMDA receptors and neurotransmitter function
Support of normal blood pressure regulation
Involvement in bone mineralization and remodeling
evidence_notes: Magnesium is one of the most well-researched minerals with extensive RCT evidence supporting its role in muscle function, sleep, cardiovascular health, and metabolic processes. Gluconate is a highly bioavailable, chelated form with good absorption and minimal laxative effect compared to other forms.

dosage.timing: Can be taken anytime; evening dosing may support sleep

dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; consistent intake supports maintenance of magnesium status
dosage.special_populations: RDA: 310-320 mg for adult women, 400-420 mg for adult men; higher needs in athletes and during stress

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal effects (loose stools) at high doses
Nausea or stomach discomfort (less common with gluconate form)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment or kidney disease
Heart block or severe cardiac arrhythmias

ACE inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics (monitor with high-dose magnesium)
supplements: Calcium (may compete for absorption; timing separation recommended)

name: Magnesium Hydroxide

Supports muscle relaxation and neuromuscular signaling
Increases stool water content via osmotic effect
evidence_notes: Magnesium is an extensively researched essential mineral with strong evidence for its physiological role. Magnesium hydroxide specifically has well-established bioavailability and safety as a supplement form, though evidence is primarily observational rather than from large RCTs focused on this particular salt.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; often taken with meals to reduce GI upset
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food, though food can reduce laxative effect if constipation relief is not desired

Fluoroquinolone antibiotics (reduced absorption if taken within 2 hours)

name: Magnesium Lysinate Glycinate
summary: Magnesium lysinate glycinate is a chelated form of magnesium designed for enhanced absorption, combining magnesium with the amino acids glycine and lysine. Magnesium is essential for over 300 enzymatic reactions and supports muscle function, energy production, and nervous system health.
mechanism_of_action: Acts as a cofactor for ATP production and energy metabolism
Regulates neurotransmitter release and nerve impulse transmission
Enhances GABA receptor activity for calming effects
Chelation with glycine and lysine may improve intestinal absorption relative to inorganic forms
evidence_notes: Magnesium itself has strong RCT evidence for multiple health outcomes (sleep, migraine, muscle function). Chelated forms like lysinate glycinate show improved bioavailability in absorption studies compared to inorganic salts, though clinical outcome data is less extensive than for magnesium in general. Grade reflects robust evidence for magnesium with additional support for chelated formulations.

dosage.timing: Divided doses or as directed; often taken in evening for sleep support
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; with food may improve tolerance
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term supplementation; effects on sleep and relaxation may develop over 2-4 weeks
dosage.special_populations: RDA: 310-320 mg (adult women), 400-420 mg (adult men). Higher needs in athletes and during stress. Those with kidney disease should consult healthcare provider.

common_side_effects: Gastrointestinal upset (less common with chelated forms than salt forms)
contraindications: Severe renal impairment or kidney disease (risk of magnesium accumulation)
Heart block or other serious cardiac conduction disorders

medications: Fluoroquinolone and tetracycline antibiotics — separate by 2+ hours
Calcium channel blockers — possible additive effects on muscle relaxation
supplements: Calcium (high amounts may compete for absorption; ideally 2:1 or 3:1 calcium:magnesium ratio)
Zinc (may compete for absorption at very high doses)

name: Magnesium Malate
summary: Magnesium with malic acid for energy production. Favored for fibromyalgia, chronic fatigue, and muscle pain.

mechanism_of_action: Malic acid is Krebs cycle intermediate

dosage.special_populations: Fatigue, fibromyalgia

name: Magnesium N-Acetyl Taurinate
summary: Magnesium N-Acetyl Taurinate is a chelated form of magnesium bound to N-acetyl taurine, designed to enhance absorption and cellular uptake compared to standard magnesium salts. Magnesium is an essential mineral supporting muscle function, nervous system health, energy production, and cardiac function.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor for ATP synthesis and cellular energy production
Regulates neurotransmitter activity and nervous system excitability
Supports muscle relaxation via calcium regulation
N-acetyl taurine enhances intestinal absorption and cellular transport
Taurine provides additional cardioprotective and neuromodulatory benefits
evidence_notes: Magnesium itself has strong RCT evidence (grade A) for multiple health outcomes. The N-acetyl taurinate chelation form has preliminary evidence (grade C) for enhanced bioavailability, but the overall ingredient warrants grade B due to solid magnesium evidence combined with emerging support for the chelated formulation.

dosage.typical_range: 200-500 mg elemental magnesium daily (from 800 mg magnesium acetyl taurinate)
dosage.timing: Evening or split doses; can be taken anytime
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; food may slightly enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; effects on sleep and relaxation may develop within 1-2 weeks

common_side_effects: Loose stools or mild digestive upset at high doses
Drowsiness (may be desired for sleep support)
contraindications: Severe kidney disease or renal failure

medications: Fluoroquinolone and tetracycline antibiotics (separate dosing by 2+ hours)
ACE inhibitors and potassium-sparing diuretics (risk of hypermagnesemia)
supplements: Calcium (very high doses may compete for absorption)
Zinc (high doses may reduce magnesium absorption)

name: Magnesium Orotate
summary: Magnesium orotate is a chelated form of magnesium bound to orotic acid, proposed to enhance absorption and support cardiovascular and cellular energy metabolism. Evidence for this specific form is limited compared to well-studied magnesium salts, though magnesium itself is essential for numerous physiological functions.
mechanism_of_action: Magnesium cofactor in ATP synthesis and cellular energy metabolism
Support for cardiac muscle contractility and electrical function
Potential enhanced bioavailability via orotic acid chelation
evidence_notes: While magnesium itself is well-established (Grade A), magnesium orotate specifically has limited human clinical trials. Some European clinical data exists for cardiac applications, but evidence is preliminary and not widely replicated in rigorous RCTs. The orotate form lacks strong differentiation evidence over other bioavailable magnesium salts.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; evening dosing may support relaxation

contraindications: Severe renal impairment or kidney disease
Heart block or serious cardiac conduction disorders (consult healthcare provider)
