dosage.special_populations: Metabolic syndrome, prediabetes

name: L-Methionine
summary: Essential sulfur amino acid. Precursor to cysteine, taurine, and SAM-e.
evidence_notes: Essential amino acid

dosage.special_populations: Liver support, sulfur support

supplements: Take with B6, B12, folate to prevent homocysteine rise

practical_notes: Must take B vitamins to prevent homocysteine rise

name: Methyliberine
summary: Methyliberine is a purine alkaloid structurally similar to caffeine that is proposed to enhance energy, focus, and mood through dopaminergic and adenosinergic mechanisms. Clinical evidence in humans is very limited, with most data derived from preliminary in vitro and animal studies.
evidence_notes: Methyliberine has virtually no published human clinical trials. Available evidence is limited to proprietary research, in vitro studies, and unpublished manufacturer data. Grade D reflects the absence of peer-reviewed RCT evidence in human populations.

dosage.timing: Morning or early afternoon to avoid sleep disruption
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: No established long-term safety data; use as directed on label
dosage.special_populations: Not studied in pregnant/nursing women, children, or those with cardiovascular conditions; avoid if sensitive to stimulants

name: Microactive® Melatonin
summary: Microactive® is a branded, micronized form of melatonin designed for enhanced absorption and faster onset. Melatonin is a well-researched natural hormone that regulates circadian rhythm and sleep-wake cycles.
mechanism_of_action: MT1 and MT2 melatonin receptor agonism
Increased sleep pressure via neurological pathways
evidence_notes: Melatonin has extensive RCT evidence for sleep onset and jet lag. Microactive® specifically claims improved bioavailability via micronization; standard melatonin is already well-established. The branded form is A-grade as melatonin is a foundational sleep ingredient with strong evidence.

dosage.with_food: can be taken with or without food; food may slow absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; discontinue if effectiveness diminishes (rare tolerance)
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in children and elderly; pregnancy/nursing: consult physician; diabetics monitor glucose (melatonin may affect insulin sensitivity)

contraindications: severe depression or bipolar disorder (may worsen symptoms)

medications: blood thinners (melatonin may have mild antiplatelet effects)

name: Microbiome-Modulating Prebiotics (MMP)
summary: Proprietary prebiotic blend containing Japanese wasabi and Sophora extracts that selectively modulate beneficial gut bacteria.
evidence_notes: Contains researched prebiotic compounds including quercetin
mechanism_of_action: Contains quercetin for gut barrier support

dosage.special_populations: Well tolerated across populations

name: Milk Thistle (Silymarin)
summary: Milk thistle extract contains silymarin, a flavonoid complex traditionally used to support liver health and function. Clinical evidence suggests modest benefits for liver support, though results remain mixed across studies.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for liver protection

dosage.special_populations: Those with liver concerns, alcohol users, medication users

name: Mitidol® (Ginger & Acmella Phytosome®)
summary: Mitidol® combines standardized ginger rhizome extract (2.4% gingerols and shogaols) with Acmella oleracea (0.3% spilanthol) in a phytosome delivery system designed to enhance bioavailability. The combination targets inflammatory response and oral sensory stimulation, though evidence is primarily derived from individual component research rather than the proprietary blend itself.
mechanism_of_action: Gingerols and shogaols inhibit prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis
Spilanthol provides localized oral stimulation and potential immune modulation
Phytosome delivery enhances absorption and cellular uptake
evidence_notes: Ginger alone has B-grade evidence for anti-inflammatory effects, but the proprietary Mitidol® blend combining ginger phytosome with Acmella lacks dedicated human clinical trials. Acmella oleracea has limited human data. The specific combination and phytosome formulation represent a novel product with preliminary evidence at best.

dosage.typical_range: As directed by manufacturer; typically 1-2 capsules daily
dosage.timing: With meals for optimal absorption and reduced gastrointestinal upset
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to enhance phytosome absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; effects typically emerge after 2-4 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; may interact with blood thinners or medications metabolized via CYP3A4

contraindications: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy (ginger may potentiate effects)
Gallstones or bile duct obstruction (ginger contraindication)
Pregnancy and lactation (insufficient safety data for proprietary blend)
Allergy to Zingiberaceae or Asteraceae families

medications: Warfarin and anticoagulants (ginger may increase bleeding risk)
Diabetes medications (ginger may enhance hypoglycemic effects)
CYP3A4 substrates (phytosome delivery may alter bioavailability)
supplements: High-dose garlic or other anticoagulant herbs
Other ginger products (risk of excessive intake)

name: Caffeine + L-Theanine
summary: Classic synergistic stack for focused energy. L-theanine smooths caffeine's rough edges.
evidence_notes: Well-studied synergistic combination

dosage.special_populations: Productivity, focus needs

name: Modafinil (Informational)
summary: Prescription wakefulness agent used off-label for cognitive enhancement. Requires prescription in most countries.
evidence_notes: Prescription medication - well-studied
mechanism_of_action: Promotes wakefulness without typical stimulant effects

dosage.special_populations: Narcolepsy, shift work (FDA approved)

name: Modified Citrus Pectin (MCP)
summary: Modified fiber that binds heavy metals in bloodstream (not just gut) and may have immune and cancer benefits.
evidence_notes: Growing research for heavy metals and galectin-3

dosage.timing: On empty stomach or between meals
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term use appropriate for heavy metals
dosage.special_populations: Those with heavy metal burden, cancer support

serious_warnings: May enhance effects of some cancer treatments

medications: May affect some drug absorption - separate by 1 hour
food_alcohol: Take away from food for metal binding

name: Molybdenum
summary: Essential trace mineral for sulfite detoxification and enzyme function. Most people get adequate amounts from food.
evidence_notes: Essential trace mineral

dosage.special_populations: Sulfite sensitivity

name: Molybdenum (as Molybdenum Glycinate Chelate)
summary: Molybdenum is an essential trace mineral and cofactor for sulfite oxidase and other molybdenum-dependent enzymes involved in amino acid and nucleotide metabolism. The glycinate chelate form aims to improve bioavailability.
evidence_notes: Molybdenum is established as essential with defined biochemical roles, but clinical supplementation evidence is sparse. The glycinate chelate form lacks robust human bioavailability studies.

dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; deficiency is rare in adequate diets
dosage.special_populations: Liver disease: use with caution; genetic molybdenum cofactor deficiency requires specialist management

common_side_effects: Minimal; very well tolerated at physiological doses
contraindications: Gout or uric acid elevation (may increase xanthine oxidase activity)

medications: May interact with allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor)
supplements: Copper interaction possible; separate dosing if possible

name: Molybdenum (as Sodium Molybdate)
summary: Molybdenum is an essential trace mineral required for the function of molybdenum-dependent enzymes including sulfite oxidase, xanthine oxidase, and aldehyde oxidase. It plays important roles in detoxification and purine metabolism.
primary_uses: Cofactor for sulfite oxidase, xanthine oxidase, and aldehyde oxidase
Uric acid metabolism and purine catabolism
mechanism_of_action: Essential cofactor in molybdenum hydroxylases (MoCo-dependent enzymes)
Facilitates oxidation of sulfite, xanthine, and other substrates
Critical in Phase I and Phase III detoxification
evidence_notes: Molybdenum is an established essential mineral with well-characterized enzymatic roles. Deficiency is extremely rare and has documented clinical consequences; adequacy is supported by biochemical research.

dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term supplementation at recommended levels; upper limit is 2 mg/day
dosage.special_populations: Individuals consuming high amounts of sulfites or exposed to occupational molybdenum dust may have altered requirements

common_side_effects: Virtually no side effects at recommended intake levels
Very high doses may cause gout-like symptoms (due to elevated uric acid) or copper deficiency
contraindications: History of gout or hyperuricemia (molybdenum increases xanthine oxidase activity)
Copper deficiency (high molybdenum may further impair copper status)

medications: Allopurinol (both inhibit xanthine oxidase; combination may reduce uric acid excessively)
supplements: Copper (high molybdenum may impair copper absorption and status)
Sulfur-containing amino acids or sulfite-rich foods (molybdenum facilitates their metabolism)

name: Monk
summary: Monk fruit powder is a natural sweetener derived from Siraitia grosvenorii that contains mogrosides, compounds estimated to be 150–250 times sweeter than sucrose with negligible calories. Limited clinical evidence exists on potential metabolic or health benefits beyond its use as a sugar substitute.
primary_uses: Sugar substitute for blood sugar management
mechanism_of_action: Mogrosides bind to taste receptors providing sweetness without glucose metabolism
Potential antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects from polyphenol content (limited evidence)
evidence_notes: Monk fruit is recognized as safe by regulatory agencies and has traditional use history. However, human clinical trials demonstrating specific health benefits are sparse. Most evidence is limited to in vitro studies or animal models; human efficacy data for metabolic claims is preliminary.

dosage.typical_range: 1-2 grams daily (varies by product concentration and mogroside content)
dosage.timing: As needed for sweetening beverages or foods
dosage.with_food: Not applicable; typically used as food/beverage ingredient
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use as a sweetener replacement
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe in pregnancy and lactation; appropriate for pediatric use as a sweetener

common_side_effects: Rare reports of mild gastrointestinal effects in sensitive individuals

name: Monolaurin (Lauric Acid)
summary: Derived from coconut, this antimicrobial fatty acid disrupts lipid-coated viruses and bacteria.
evidence_notes: Promising in vitro data, limited clinical trials
mechanism_of_action: Disrupts lipid membranes of enveloped viruses
Does not harm beneficial bacteria as much

dosage.duration_notes: Short to medium term, or ongoing at lower doses
dosage.special_populations: Those with viral or bacterial concerns

serious_warnings: Start low - die-off can be significant

medications: Generally safe, monitor if on antivirals

name: Monounsaturated Fat
summary: A macronutrient with a single double bond in the carbon chain, commonly associated with cardiovascular health through dietary patterns like the Mediterranean diet. Naturally present in olive oil and some fish oils alongside polyunsaturated fats.
evidence_notes: Strong epidemiological and RCT evidence from Mediterranean diet studies demonstrates cardiovascular protective effects. Consistent benefits on lipid profiles and endothelial function.

dosage.typical_range: 15-20% of total daily calories (approximately 15-30 grams for 2000 calorie diet)
dosage.with_food: consumed as part of normal dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: ongoing dietary intake recommended for sustained benefit
dosage.special_populations: beneficial for all populations; particularly emphasized in Mediterranean and DASH diet recommendations

name: Moringa (Drumstick Tree)
summary: Moringa oleifera leaf is a nutrient-dense plant source containing vitamins, minerals, and polyphenols traditionally used in Ayurvedic and African medicine. Limited clinical evidence exists for specific health claims, though the whole plant contains bioactive compounds with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in vitro.
evidence_notes: Traditional use with modern research on nutrients and blood sugar

dosage.special_populations: Nutrient supplementation, blood sugar support

serious_warnings: Root bark is toxic - leaf only

name: Moringa
summary: Moringa is a nutrient-dense plant with antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and blood sugar supporting properties, often called a superfood.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for antioxidant and nutritional benefits

dosage.special_populations: Nutritional support seekers

common_side_effects: Mild laxative effect at high doses

medications: May enhance blood thinner and diabetes medication effects

practical_notes: Leaf is the safe, beneficial part

name: Mr. Happy Stack (Uridine)
summary: Popular stack combining uridine, DHA, and choline for dopamine upregulation and brain health.
evidence_notes: Synergistic dopamine and membrane support

dosage.special_populations: Low mood, dopamine support

practical_notes: Take with fat for DHA absorption

name: MSM (Methylsulfonylmethane)
summary: Organic sulfur compound supporting joint tissue, connective tissue, and reducing inflammation.
evidence_notes: Good safety profile, moderate evidence

dosage.special_populations: Joint issues, athletes

name: MSM (Sulfur Source)
summary: Organic sulfur for connective tissue, detoxification, and anti-inflammatory effects.
evidence_notes: Organic sulfur compound

dosage.special_populations: Joint issues, detox support

name: Mucilaginous Fiber Blend
summary: Soothing fiber blend (slippery elm, marshmallow) for gut lining support and hydration.
evidence_notes: Traditional soothing herbs

dosage.special_populations: GERD, sore throat, gut sensitivity

name: Mucuna pruriens
summary: Mucuna pruriens is a tropical plant containing L-DOPA (levodopa), a precursor to dopamine that may support mood, motivation, and motor function. Evidence for cognitive and physical performance benefits exists but is moderate and primarily derived from animal studies and small human trials.
evidence_notes: Natural L-DOPA source

dosage.special_populations: Low dopamine, motivation

supplements: Don't combine with other dopamine precursors

name: Mucuna pruriens Extract (Seed) - 15% L-DOPA
summary: A legume extract standardized to contain 15% L-DOPA, a direct precursor to dopamine. Traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine for mood, motivation, and motor function support.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for L-DOPA in Parkinson's disease; emerging evidence for mood and motivation in healthy individuals. 15% standardization is meaningful; however, bioavailability can vary.

dosage.timing: Morning to early afternoon (avoid evening due to stimulating effects)
dosage.with_food: Take with light food to minimize nausea; avoid large meals which can interfere with absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Effects typically noticed within 2-4 weeks; tolerance may develop with long-term daily use
dosage.special_populations: Caution in individuals with cardiovascular disease; may interact with psychiatric medications

contraindications: Recent heart attack or unstable angina

supplements: High-dose vitamin B6 may reduce L-DOPA effectiveness

name: Muira Puama Extract
summary: Muira puama is a traditional Amazonian plant historically used for sexual function and vitality, though clinical evidence in humans remains limited and mostly preliminary. Most research consists of small or animal studies, with few rigorous controlled trials in humans.
mechanism_of_action: Potential vasodilation via increased nitric oxide
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials; most evidence from traditional use, animal studies, or very small uncontrolled human studies. A few small RCTs show modest benefits for sexual function, but sample sizes are typically <100 subjects and methodological rigor is variable. Insufficient evidence for strong recommendation.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with or without meals; often split into divided doses
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; may be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests 4–12 weeks for potential effects; limited data on long-term safety
dosage.special_populations: Avoid during pregnancy/lactation; limited safety data in children

common_side_effects: Rare reports of insomnia or nervousness at high doses
contraindications: Cardiovascular disease (use with caution; no direct contraindication but limited safety data)

medications: Possible interactions with antihypertensive agents (theoretical; limited evidence)
Possible interaction with erectile dysfunction medications (additive vasodilation effect) — use caution
supplements: Other vasodilatory herbs or nitric oxide boosters (e.g., L-arginine, beetroot) — additive effect possible

name: Mullein Leaf Extract
summary: Mullein leaf has been used traditionally in herbal medicine for respiratory support, but clinical evidence in humans remains limited and primarily consists of traditional use reports rather than robust controlled trials. Most research focuses on in vitro activity and animal models.
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trial data. Traditional use is well-documented in herbal medicine systems, but controlled RCTs in humans are scarce. Some in vitro and animal studies suggest potential respiratory benefits, but quality and quantity of evidence do not support A or B grading.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with meals or between meals; often used as needed for respiratory symptoms
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; may be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests short-term support during seasonal challenges or acute symptoms; long-term safety in humans not extensively studied
dosage.special_populations: Avoid during pregnancy and lactation due to limited safety data

common_side_effects: Rare: skin irritation with topical application
contraindications: Individuals with glycemic control concerns (contains small amounts of saponins)

medications: No major known interactions, but use caution with medications affecting blood sugar due to saponin content

name: Multi-Peak Energy Complex
summary: Proprietary energy-enhancing blend combining sustained-release caffeine with adaptogenic compounds designed to provide steady energy without crashes.
evidence_notes: Proprietary blend with established components
mechanism_of_action: Multi-phase caffeine release for sustained energy

dosage.special_populations: Caffeine-sensitive individuals start with half dose

serious_warnings: Contains caffeine - monitor total daily intake
safety.liver_kidney_notes: No specific concerns at normal doses

food_alcohol: Coffee, tea add to caffeine load

practical_notes: Take early in day to avoid sleep issues
Start with lower dose to assess tolerance
Cycle use to prevent tolerance buildup

name: Multi-Strain Probiotic
summary: Blend of multiple probiotic strains for broad-spectrum gut support. Diversity may enhance benefits.
evidence_notes: Diversity may enhance benefits

dosage.special_populations: General gut health

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Likely safe at normal doses - consult provider

practical_notes: Look for strains, not just species

name: Multivitamin (Athletic Formula)
summary: Broad-spectrum insurance for micronutrient gaps. Athletes may need more than RDA.
evidence_notes: Insurance against deficiencies, not performance enhancer

dosage.special_populations: Athletes, restricted diets

serious_warnings: Iron can be problematic for men

medications: Many interactions possible - check specific nutrients

name: Muscadine Grape Seed and Skin Powder
summary: Muscadine grape (Vitis rotundifolia) seed and skin powder is rich in proanthocyanidins, resveratrol, and other polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Muscadine varieties are native to the southeastern United States and contain higher antioxidant concentrations than European grape varieties.
evidence_notes: Grape seed/skin extracts have moderate evidence for cardiovascular and antioxidant benefits. Muscadine-specific human clinical data is more limited than for European grape extracts, warranting a more conservative grade.

dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble polyphenols benefit from dietary fat context
dosage.special_populations: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant use: caution with high doses; pregnancy/nursing: use food-level amounts

common_side_effects: Mild headache or nausea possible at high doses

medications: Potential additive anticoagulant effects at very high doses with warfarin—monitor
supplements: No major interactions; may have additive antioxidant effects with other polyphenol sources

name: Mushroom Immune Stack
summary: Medicinal mushroom combination: Turkey Tail, Reishi, and Chaga for comprehensive immune modulation.
evidence_notes: Synergistic mushroom benefits

dosage.timing: Any time, Reishi evening if preferred for calming
dosage.special_populations: Those seeking immune modulation and adaptogenic support

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Monitor liver with high-dose reishi; chaga oxalates concern for kidneys

name: Myricetin
summary: Flavonoid with potent antioxidant and anti-diabetic properties. Found in berries and tea, supports glucose metabolism.
evidence_notes: Emerging research on metabolic and neuroprotective benefits

dosage.special_populations: Metabolic support, antioxidant needs

serious_warnings: Limited safety data at high doses

name: N-Acetyl Cysteine (Fertility)
summary: Antioxidant that supports glutathione, may help PCOS and male fertility.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for PCOS ovulation and male fertility

dosage.with_food: Away from food for best absorption
dosage.special_populations: PCOS, male fertility, antioxidant support

name: NAC (Hearing Protection)
summary: May protect against noise-induced hearing loss. Used preventatively before loud noise exposure.
evidence_notes: Noise-induced hearing loss prevention

dosage.special_populations: Musicians, loud noise exposure

name: N-Acetyl D-Glucosamine
summary: N-Acetyl D-Glucosamine (NAG) is an amino sugar derivative that supports joint cartilage structure and skin barrier function. Evidence for joint health is mixed and moderate, with stronger evidence for gut barrier support.
mechanism_of_action: Provides substrate for glycosaminoglycan synthesis in cartilage matrix
Supports mucin production in intestinal lining
May modulate immune tolerance through dendritic cell signaling
evidence_notes: NAG has shown promise in small studies for joint health, gut barrier support, and inflammatory markers, but larger, well-designed RCTs are limited. Evidence is preliminary and inconsistent, particularly for isolated joint outcomes. Better evidence exists for combinations with glucosamine/chondroitin than NAG alone.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with or without meals; consistent daily dosing recommended
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement, though may be better tolerated with food
dosage.duration_notes: Most studies examining joint benefits used 8-16 week protocols; effects may require consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy and lactation; limited pediatric data

contraindications: Shellfish allergy (some sources derived from shellfish)

medications: May have additive effects with anti-inflammatory agents (theoretical)
supplements: Often combined with glucosamine and chondroitin for joint support
May have synergistic effects with hyaluronic acid

name: N-Acetyl L-Carnosine
summary: N-Acetyl L-Carnosine is an acetylated form of the dipeptide carnosine that may support cellular health through antioxidant and anti-glycation mechanisms. Most research has focused on topical ophthalmic applications, with limited evidence for systemic oral supplementation in humans.
mechanism_of_action: Antioxidant activity via free radical scavenging
Inhibition of advanced glycation end products (AGEs)
Potentially improved cellular carnosine uptake via acetylation
evidence_notes: Most robust evidence exists for topical ophthalmic use (age-related cataracts, corneal health). Oral systemic research in humans is limited and preliminary. Animal studies suggest cellular benefits, but human RCT evidence for oral supplementation is sparse.

dosage.timing: Divided doses with or without food
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; no strong evidence of food interaction
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety data limited; most studies examine 8-24 week durations
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing: insufficient data; diabetic individuals (theoretical benefit for glycation) lack confirmatory human trials

contraindications: Use with caution in individuals with carnosine metabolism disorders (rare)

supplements: May have additive antioxidant effects with other antioxidant supplements (theoretical)

name: N-Acetyl L-Cysteine (NAC)
summary: NAC is a stabilized form of the amino acid L-cysteine that serves as a precursor to glutathione, a major intracellular antioxidant. It has strong evidence for respiratory health and moderate evidence for immune and detoxification support.
mechanism_of_action: Replenishes glutathione stores via cysteine donation
Breaks down mucus protein bonds (mucolytic effect)
Supports phase II liver detoxification enzymes
evidence_notes: Strong clinical evidence for acute respiratory conditions and acetaminophen overdose; moderate evidence for chronic respiratory support and general antioxidant benefits; good safety profile with extensive research history.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; consistency matters more than timing
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with food may reduce stomach upset
dosage.duration_notes: Generally safe for long-term use; clinical protocols often use 600-1800 mg daily
dosage.special_populations: Doses up to 1500 mg daily have been used in respiratory studies; those with cystinuria should avoid supplementation

contraindications: Caution in those prone to kidney stones
Warrants medical consultation if pregnant or nursing

medications: May interact with nitroglycerin (enhanced vasodilation risk)
Concurrent use with acetaminophen is actually protective (NAC is the clinical antidote to acetaminophen overdose)
May affect insulin sensitivity in some contexts
supplements: May enhance effects of other antioxidants when combined

name: N-Acetyl L-Glutamine
summary: N-Acetyl L-Glutamine is an acetylated form of the amino acid glutamine that may support intestinal barrier function and immune health. Evidence for human supplementation is limited and primarily preliminary.
mechanism_of_action: Provides acetylated glutamine for intestinal epithelial cell metabolism
May support tight junction integrity in the gut barrier
Potential glutamine precursor for immune cell function
evidence_notes: Most human evidence is anecdotal or derived from animal studies. Very few robust RCTs in humans specifically examining N-Acetyl L-Glutamine supplementation. Glutamine itself has better evidence, but the acetylated form lacks sufficient clinical validation.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; some suggest post-workout or between meals
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Optimal duration for supplementation is not well established
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnant/nursing women and children not well studied

contraindications: Not recommended for those with glutamine sensitivity
Caution in severe liver or kidney disease

name: N-Acetyl L-Tyrosine
summary: N-Acetyl L-Tyrosine is an acetylated form of the amino acid tyrosine that serves as a precursor to catecholamine neurotransmitters (dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine). Evidence for cognitive and mood benefits is mixed and moderate at best, with most studies showing modest effects under acute stress or sleep deprivation.
mechanism_of_action: Precursor to dopamine and norepinephrine synthesis
Enhanced catecholamine availability during cognitive demand
Potential restoration of neurotransmitter levels during high stress
evidence_notes: Limited RCT evidence with mixed results. Some studies show modest benefits under acute stress or sleep deprivation, but effects are inconsistent and often small. Lacks robust clinical evidence compared to direct dopamine/norepinephrine-supporting compounds. Bioavailability advantages over L-tyrosine are debated.

dosage.timing: Morning or as needed, typically before cognitive demand or stressful situations
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food, though some evidence suggests taking on empty stomach may optimize absorption
dosage.duration_notes: May be taken acutely during high stress or chronically; tolerance possible with prolonged use
dosage.special_populations: Use caution in hyperthyroidism (stimulates thyroid function); monitor in bipolar disorder (potential mood destabilization)

common_side_effects: Mild stimulant effects (jitteriness, insomnia if taken late)
Increased heart rate at higher doses
contraindications: Concurrent use of stimulant medications (potential additive effects)

medications: Thyroid medications: potential synergistic thyroid stimulation
supplements: Other catecholamine precursors (L-DOPA, mucuna pruriens): additive dopamine elevation
Stimulating herbs (caffeine, yohimbine): potential synergistic stimulation

name: N,N-Dimethylglycine HCl
summary: N,N-Dimethylglycine (DMG) is a derivative of the amino acid glycine that functions as a methyl donor and may support methylation pathways and energy metabolism. Limited clinical evidence exists for most purported benefits, with most research conducted in small, dated studies or animal models.
mechanism_of_action: Potential improvement in oxygen utilization efficiency
evidence_notes: Evidence is preliminary and limited. Some small, older studies suggest potential ergogenic effects in athletic populations, but modern high-quality RCTs are lacking. Most mechanistic claims are theoretically sound but lack robust human clinical validation.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Typical supplementation periods in studies range from 4–8 weeks; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy/lactation not established; use only under professional guidance in pediatric populations

contraindications: Choline deficiency or related metabolic disorders (may interact with methylation)
History of mental health disorders involving homocysteine metabolism (theoretical concern)

supplements: Choline, betaine, or other methyl donors (additive effects; unclear clinical significance)

name: NA-R-ALA (Stabilized R-Lipoic Acid)
summary: NA-R-ALA is a sodium salt form of R-lipoic acid designed to improve stability and bioavailability compared to standard alpha-lipoic acid. It functions as a mitochondrial antioxidant and cofactor for energy metabolism enzymes.
mechanism_of_action: Mitochondrial antioxidant scavenging reactive oxygen species
Cofactor for pyruvate dehydrogenase and alpha-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase complexes
Recycling other antioxidants (vitamin C, vitamin E, glutathione)
evidence_notes: R-lipoic acid (the bioactive form) has good clinical evidence for blood sugar support and neuropathy, particularly in diabetes. The NA-R-ALA salt form improves stability/absorption over standard ALA, but comparative human trials are limited. Grade reflects strong evidence for R-lipoic acid pharmacology but more modest evidence specific to the sodium salt formulation.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with or without food; morning dosing common for energy support
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; some evidence suggests food may slightly reduce absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; benefits for metabolic support typically assessed over 8-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Diabetic individuals should monitor blood glucose; may potentiate insulin or glucose-lowering medications

common_side_effects: Sulfur-like body odor (reported with high doses)
contraindications: Concurrent use with blood sugar medications requires monitoring
Active thiamine deficiency (lipoic acid may lower thiamine)

medications: Insulin and oral hypoglycemics (additive glucose-lowering effect — monitor blood glucose)
Thyroid medications (may increase absorption; separate timing recommended)
supplements: Biotin (may compete for absorption at high doses)
Vitamin B1/thiamine (lipoic acid may lower circulating thiamine)

name: NAC (N-Acetyl Cysteine)
summary: Precursor to glutathione, the body's master antioxidant. Supports liver, respiratory, and mental health.
evidence_notes: Well-studied glutathione precursor

dosage.with_food: Away from food for glutathione; with food OK for respiratory
dosage.special_populations: Liver support, respiratory, OCD/addiction

upper_limit.label: No established UL
upper_limit.notes: GI upset at very high doses

serious_warnings: May reduce efficacy of some chemo

practical_notes: Take away from food for best absorption
Take with vitamin C for best effect
May cause histamine issues in some

name: NAC (Sulfur/Detox Focus)
summary: Cysteine precursor providing sulfur for glutathione synthesis and detoxification.
evidence_notes: Essential for glutathione

dosage.special_populations: Detox support, liver health, respiratory

supplements: Mineral replenishment if long-term high dose

citations.title: NAC mechanisms
citations.finding: NAC provides cysteine for glutathione synthesis

citations.title: NAC antioxidant effects
citations.finding: Supports detoxification and reduces oxidative stress

name: β-Nicotinamide Adenine Dinucleotide (NAD+)
summary: NAD+ is a coenzyme essential for cellular energy metabolism, mitochondrial function, and NAD+-dependent enzyme activity (sirtuins, PARPs). While NAD+ levels decline with age, oral supplementation has limited bioavailability; research on direct NAD+ supplementation in humans remains preliminary.
mechanism_of_action: Serves as electron acceptor in glycolysis, citric acid cycle, and electron transport chain
Substrate for NAD+-dependent enzymes (sirtuins, PARP, CD38)
Supports ATP production and redox balance
evidence_notes: Strong mechanistic rationale and animal data support NAD+ biology, but human RCT evidence for oral NAD+ supplementation is limited and inconsistent. Bioavailability concerns exist due to molecular size and intestinal degradation. Preclinical evidence is robust; clinical translation remains preliminary.

dosage.with_food: May improve absorption; food does not significantly impair uptake
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety data in humans is limited; most studies are short-term (4-12 weeks)
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and children not established; use with caution in liver or kidney disease

common_side_effects: Flushing (more common with high-dose niacin; less likely with NAD+ itself)

name: Naringenin

name: Naringin Extract (Citrus hongheensis)

evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials; most evidence comes from in vitro and animal studies. Some preliminary data on antioxidant and cardiovascular effects, but insufficient high-quality RCT evidence in humans to establish efficacy.

Potential interaction with certain statins and immunosuppressants

medications: CYP3A4 substrates (statins, calcium channel blockers, immunosuppressants)

name: NooGandha® Ashwagandha Root and Leaf Extract (3.5% Withanolides)

Reduces cortisol levels and HPA-axis activity
evidence_notes: Ashwagandha has substantial clinical evidence from multiple RCTs showing efficacy for stress, anxiety, and sleep. The 3.5% withanolide standardization ensures consistent bioactive content. Evidence is strong for stress/anxiety outcomes; moderate for cognitive enhancement.

Tru Niagen, Elysium Basis use NR

summary: Mediterranean botanical with oleuropein providing antimicrobial, cardiovascular, and antioxidant benefits. Traditional immune and heart tonic.

name: Orange Powder (Citrus sinensis)

It is commonly used as a nutrient source and flavor additive in supplements, though evidence for isolated benefits beyond those of whole oranges remains limited.

evidence_notes: Whole oranges have well-established nutritional benefits, but isolated orange powder as a supplement ingredient lacks robust clinical trial evidence. Benefits are largely extrapolated from whole fruit research and in vitro studies.

dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use as a food-derived ingredient

dosage.special_populations: Caution in those with low potassium or bone health concerns; not for children without guidance

medications: insulin or diabetes medications (may potentiate effects—monitor)

name: Organic Banana Powder

Delivers natural carbohydrates for energy replenishment
Contributes B vitamins (B6, folate) for metabolic support
Supplies polyphenol antioxidants from fruit content
evidence_notes: While banana as a whole food has well-established nutritional benefits (potassium, carbohydrates, micronutrients), powder form maintains these nutrients but lacks clinical research specific to the processed form. Grade reflects the strong evidence base for banana fruit itself as a nutrition source.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; typically mixed into beverages or foods
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for ongoing use as a whole-food ingredient
dosage.special_populations: Safe for most populations; individuals with kidney disease should monitor potassium intake

Those with banana allergies (rare; often associated with birch pollen allergy)

dosage.timing: With meals; raw or lightly cooked to preserve myrosinase
dosage.with_food: With food; presence of myrosinase enzyme aids conversion

name: Organic Kale Powder

While kale itself is nutrient-dense, evidence for specific health claims from supplemental powder forms is limited compared to whole-food consumption.
mechanism_of_action: Supplies vitamin K for bone and clotting factors
evidence_notes: Kale as a whole food is well-documented as nutrient-dense; however, evidence for supplemental powder forms is moderate and largely extrapolated from whole-food studies. Bioavailability and efficacy of dried/powdered forms requires more human research.

name: Organic Spinach Extract (Spinacia oleracea Leaf)

Thyroid disease (goitrogens in raw spinach, likely reduced in extract)

name: Pantothenic Acid (Vitamin B5)
