name: Pantothenic Acid (Calcium Pantothenate)
summary: A B-complex vitamin required for the synthesis of coenzyme A, which is critical for energy metabolism and synthesis of hormones, cholesterol, and amino acids. Calcium pantothenate is the stable supplemental form.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for deficiency prevention and essential metabolic functions. RCT evidence supports efficacy in normal supplemental ranges. Well-established biochemical role.

dosage.typical_range: 5 mg daily (adequate intake); 5-100 mg daily for supplementation
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; water-soluble excess is excreted
dosage.special_populations: Athletes may benefit from higher intakes; bioavailability is high from supplemental forms

common_side_effects: Diarrhea may occur at very high doses (>10 g daily)
contraindications: Hypersensitivity to pantothenic acid or calcium

name: Papain
summary: Protein-digesting enzyme from papaya. Traditional digestive aid with anti-inflammatory properties.
evidence_notes: Traditional digestive enzyme

dosage.special_populations: Protein digestion

name: Papaya Fruit Extract (Carica papaya)
summary: Papaya fruit extract is derived from the tropical papaya plant and contains natural enzymes (particularly papain) and antioxidants. Limited clinical evidence supports traditional digestive and anti-inflammatory uses, though human studies remain sparse.
mechanism_of_action: Papain enzyme may support protein digestion
Potential anti-inflammatory effects from plant bioactives
evidence_notes: Papaya has a long history of traditional use and laboratory studies show biological activity, but high-quality randomized controlled trials in humans are limited. Most evidence is in vitro or traditional/observational. The 10:1 concentration format lacks specific human clinical validation.

dosage.timing: With or between meals; often taken with food for digestive support
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may be more effective with meals for digestive purposes
dosage.duration_notes: Generally considered safe for long-term use; optimal duration not established in clinical literature
dosage.special_populations: Avoid if pregnant/nursing due to limited safety data. Individuals with papaya allergy should avoid.

common_side_effects: Rare: allergic reactions (cross-reactivity with birch pollen possible)
contraindications: Papaya allergy or latex allergy (cross-reactivity risk)
Bleeding disorders (theoretical concern due to anticoagulant compounds, limited evidence)

medications: Possible interaction with anticoagulants/antiplatelet drugs (theoretical, based on traditional use; clinical significance unclear)
supplements: May have additive effects with other proteolytic enzymes (bromelain, serrapeptase)

name: Parsley Extract (Petroselinum crispum)
summary: Parsley extract is a culinary herb traditionally used for digestive support and as a natural diuretic, containing flavonoids and volatile oils. Limited clinical evidence exists for most purported benefits in humans.
mechanism_of_action: Flavonoid and polyphenol content may provide antioxidant effects
Volatile oils and apiol may support digestive function
Potassium content may contribute to diuretic properties
evidence_notes: Parsley has a long history of traditional use and contains bioactive compounds (flavonoids, apiol, myristicin). However, human clinical trials are limited. Most evidence is from in vitro, animal studies, or traditional use data rather than controlled human trials.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; with food may improve tolerability
dosage.duration_notes: Generally safe for long-term use; some traditional protocols limit to 6–8 weeks before a break
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant women should avoid supplemental parsley extract due to potential uterotonic effects; nursing mothers should consult a healthcare provider

contraindications: Kidney disease or renal impairment (diuretic effect)
Bleeding disorders or concurrent anticoagulant use (contains vitamin K)
Thyroid conditions (goitrogenic potential at very high doses)

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (vitamin K content may reduce warfarin efficacy)
Lithium (may increase lithium levels via diuretic effect)

name: PHGG (Partially Hydrolyzed Guar Gum)
summary: Processed guar fiber that's gentle, well-tolerated, and shown to help IBS and SIBO.
evidence_notes: Good evidence especially for IBS

dosage.with_food: Either way, dissolves in any beverage
dosage.special_populations: IBS, SIBO, those intolerant to other fibers

name: Passion Fruit Extract (Passiflora edulis)
summary: Passion fruit extract is derived from the fruit of Passiflora edulis and contains polyphenols, flavonoids, and other phytochemicals. Limited human clinical evidence exists for specific health claims, though traditional use and in vitro studies suggest potential antioxidant and mild anxiolytic properties.
mechanism_of_action: Potential modulation of GABA receptors (theoretical basis from related Passiflora species)
evidence_notes: Most human research focuses on Passiflora incarnata (passionflower vine); limited direct clinical evidence for Passiflora edulis fruit extract specifically. In vitro and animal studies support antioxidant properties, but human efficacy data are sparse.

dosage.typical_range: 500–2000 mg daily (as powder or extract equivalent)
dosage.timing: Any time of day with meals
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Suitable for long-term use; optimal duration of use not established
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant and nursing women should consult a healthcare provider before use

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated; rare reports of mild gastrointestinal upset
contraindications: Use with caution in individuals taking sedative medications or MAOIs (related Passiflora species have mild sedative properties)

medications: Potential interaction with CNS depressants and sedative medications (theoretical)
supplements: Other calming herbs (additive effects possible but unlikely at typical doses)

name: Passionflower
summary: Passionflower is a traditional herbal anxiolytic used to support relaxation and sleep quality. A 10:1 extract concentrate provides a potent dose; moderate clinical evidence supports mild anxiolytic and sedative effects.
evidence_notes: Gentle, safe anxiolytic herb

dosage.special_populations: Mild anxiety, sleep

name: PCOS Comprehensive Stack
summary: Complete PCOS support: Inositol, Vitamin D, NAC, Omega-3, Spearmint, Berberine if needed.
evidence_notes: Evidence-based comprehensive PCOS approach

dosage.typical_range: Myo-inositol 2-4g + D-chiro 50-100mg + Vitamin D 2000-4000IU + NAC 600-1200mg + Omega-3 2g + Spearmint tea 2 cups + Berberine 1000-1500mg (if metabolic)
dosage.special_populations: PCOS all phenotypes

name: Pea Protein
summary: Plant-based protein from yellow peas. Complete amino acid profile, hypoallergenic, and easily digestible alternative to dairy proteins.
evidence_notes: Good muscle-building potential comparable to whey

dosage.special_populations: Vegans, dairy-sensitive

practical_notes: Combine with rice protein for complete aminos

name: Peak ATP (Adenosine 5'-Triphosphate)
summary: Oral ATP supplement shown to increase strength, power, and muscle excitability. Enhances blood flow and reduces fatigue during exercise.
evidence_notes: Clinical studies show strength and power benefits
mechanism_of_action: Enhances blood flow via purinergic receptors

dosage.special_populations: Strength athletes, power sports

practical_notes: Take on empty stomach 30-60 min pre-workout

name: Pelargonium sidoides (Umckaloabo)
summary: South African geranium root extract with strong evidence for acute bronchitis and respiratory infection treatment.
evidence_notes: Strong clinical evidence for respiratory infections

dosage.timing: At onset of symptoms, continue 2-3 days after resolution
dosage.duration_notes: Up to 3 weeks for acute illness
dosage.special_populations: Adults and children, bronchitis patients

serious_warnings: Stop if signs of liver problems
safety.liver_kidney_notes: Monitor for liver issues, rare hepatotoxicity reported

name: Peppermint (Nausea)
summary: Aromatherapy and oral peppermint for nausea relief, particularly post-operative nausea.
evidence_notes: Good evidence especially for post-op nausea aromatherapy

dosage.typical_range: Aromatherapy: inhale as needed; Tea: 1-2 cups
dosage.special_populations: Post-surgical patients, general nausea

serious_warnings: May worsen GERD if taken orally (use enteric for IBS)

practical_notes: Aromatherapy works well for post-op nausea

name: Peppermint Oil (Enteric-Coated)
summary: Enteric-coated peppermint oil for IBS symptoms, relaxing intestinal smooth muscle and reducing spasms.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for IBS symptom relief

dosage.special_populations: IBS, functional GI disorders

medications: Antacids may dissolve enteric coating early

name: Pepsin
summary: Digestive enzyme derived from porcine stomach lining that breaks down proteins in acidic environments. Works synergistically with hydrochloric acid for protein digestion.
evidence_notes: Well-established digestive enzyme; moderate evidence for protein digestion support

dosage.with_food: Yes - take at start of meal
dosage.special_populations: Often combined with Betaine HCl

serious_warnings: May irritate oral mucosa if capsule opened

medications: May affect absorption of some medications
Avoid with H2 blockers and PPIs

practical_notes: Most effective with Betaine HCl for stomach acid support
Do not open capsule - may irritate mouth and throat

name: Pepsin (Aspergillus niger)
summary: Pepsin is a proteolytic enzyme derived from Aspergillus niger fungus that breaks down proteins in acidic environments, primarily used in digestive support supplements. It is commonly paired with betaine HCl to support gastric protein digestion in individuals with low stomach acid.
mechanism_of_action: Proteolytic enzyme that cleaves peptide bonds in acidic pH (optimal pH 1.5-2.5)
Breaks down dietary proteins into smaller peptides for improved absorption
evidence_notes: Limited clinical evidence specific to Aspergillus niger-derived pepsin. General enzyme therapy shows modest evidence; fungal pepsin efficacy compared to animal-sourced pepsin is not well-established in RCTs. Most evidence is theoretical or from small studies.

dosage.with_food: Must be taken with food; requires acidic gastric environment to be effective
dosage.duration_notes: May be used short-term or as ongoing digestive support; no established maximum duration
dosage.special_populations: Should not be used by individuals with peptic ulcer disease or severe gastritis without medical supervision

medications: Proton pump inhibitors (reduce stomach acid needed for pepsin activation)
supplements: Other digestive enzymes (may have additive effects)

name: Perilla Extract (Perilla frutescens)
summary: Perilla frutescens is a traditional Asian herb containing rosmarinic acid and other polyphenols with potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most research is preliminary or conducted in animal models.
mechanism_of_action: Rosmarinic acid-mediated inhibition of inflammatory pathways
Potential modulation of allergic response and mast cell activation
evidence_notes: Most evidence is from in vitro and animal studies. A few small human trials suggest potential benefit for allergic rhinitis and respiratory symptoms, but sample sizes are limited and methodology variability is high. Insufficient robust RCT data for higher grading.

dosage.timing: With meals for optimal absorption and tolerability
dosage.with_food: Recommended to reduce potential GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Most human studies used perilla for 8–12 weeks; longer-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in pregnancy/lactation without professional guidance; limited safety data in children

common_side_effects: Gastrointestinal upset (nausea, diarrhea) at higher doses
contraindications: Known allergy to Perilla frutescens or Lamiaceae family plants
Bleeding disorders (theoretical risk due to anticoagulant properties in some studies)

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (potential additive effect; use cautiously)

name: Phellinus linteus Extract (Fruiting Body)
summary: Phellinus linteus is a medicinal mushroom traditionally used in Asian medicine with emerging research on immunomodulatory and antioxidant properties. Evidence remains limited and predominantly from in vitro and animal studies, with few human clinical trials.
mechanism_of_action: Potential modulation of immune cell populations
evidence_notes: Most evidence derives from in vitro and animal models. Limited human clinical trials exist. Traditional use in Asian medicine is well-documented, but robust RCT data in humans is insufficient to warrant B-grade classification.

dosage.typical_range: 500-2000 mg daily (standardized extract basis varies by product)
dosage.duration_notes: Typical use duration 4-12 weeks for assessment; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; immunocompromised individuals should seek medical guidance

common_side_effects: Allergic reactions possible in sensitive individuals

name: Phenylethylamine (PEA)
summary: Phenylethylamine (PEA) is an endogenous trace amine that acts as a neuromodulator, primarily known for mood and focus support. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most data is from animal studies or in vitro research.
evidence_notes: Very short-acting mood boost

dosage.special_populations: Acute mood boost

name: Phenylpiracetam
summary: Stimulating racetam with phenyl group. 60x more potent than piracetam with physical and mental stimulation.
evidence_notes: Stimulating racetam

dosage.special_populations: Demanding cognitive tasks

name: PHGG (Partially Hydrolyzed Guar Gum)
summary: Low-FODMAP prebiotic fiber that's well-tolerated even by those with IBS. Minimal gas and bloating.
evidence_notes: Well-tolerated prebiotic for sensitive guts

dosage.special_populations: IBS, SIBO, sensitive digestion

citations.title: PHGG and IBS
citations.finding: Partially hydrolyzed guar gum improves IBS symptoms

citations.title: PHGG prebiotic effects
citations.finding: PHGG increases beneficial gut bacteria

name: Phosphatidic Acid
summary: Signaling lipid that activates mTOR. May enhance muscle protein synthesis response.
evidence_notes: Emerging research for muscle building

dosage.special_populations: Muscle building focus

name: Phosphatidylcholine
summary: Major membrane phospholipid and choline source. Supports brain cell structure and liver health.
evidence_notes: Membrane and liver support

dosage.special_populations: Liver health, choline needs

name: Phosphatidylserine (PS)
summary: Phospholipid that may blunt excessive cortisol response, particularly useful for overtraining.
evidence_notes: FDA-qualified health claim for cognitive function

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive decline, athletes

name: Phospholipid Complex (Absorption)
summary: Phospholipid technology used to enhance absorption of lipophilic compounds. Forms phytosomes improving bioavailability of herbs.
evidence_notes: Established technology, used in many patented forms

dosage.special_populations: Those wanting enhanced absorption

name: Phospholipids
summary: Phospholipids are a class of lipids that form the structural basis of cell membranes and serve as delivery vehicles for omega-3 fatty acids and other fat-soluble compounds. In krill oil, phospholipids enhance bioavailability of EPA/DHA compared to fish oil triglycerides.
primary_uses: Enhanced absorption of omega-3 fatty acids
mechanism_of_action: Structural components of cell membranes and lipoproteins
Facilitate lymphatic absorption of fatty acids before portal circulation
Support cell signaling and membrane fluidity
Serve as precursors to neurotransmitters (e.g., acetylcholine)
evidence_notes: Strong mechanistic basis for enhanced bioavailability of phospholipid-bound omega-3s vs. triglyceride forms (several comparative RCTs). Direct phospholipid supplementation shows moderate evidence for liver and cognitive support, though often studied as part of multicomponent supplements.

forms.form: As component of krill oil and fish oil products

dosage.typical_range: Typically 50–300 mg daily as part of krill/fish oil products; standalone phospholipids 100–600 mg daily
dosage.duration_notes: Chronic supplementation generally safe; benefits for cognition typically observed over weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; limited data in pregnant/nursing populations

common_side_effects: Fishy odor in breath or sweat (from phospholipid-bound omega-3s)
contraindications: Soy allergy (many phospholipid supplements sourced from soy lecithin)

supplements: May enhance absorption of other fat-soluble compounds (vitamins A, D, E, K)

name: Phospholipids (from Sunflower Lecithin)
summary: Phospholipids are structural components of cell membranes that support nutrient absorption and cellular function. Sunflower-derived phospholipids provide a plant-based source without soy or GMO concerns.
mechanism_of_action: Increases cell membrane fluidity and permeability for better nutrient transport
Provides structural support for cell membranes throughout the body
evidence_notes: Good evidence for phospholipids enhancing absorption of co-administered nutrients and supporting membrane health. Moderate evidence for cognitive benefits. Used in clinical lipid delivery systems.

dosage.timing: With meals containing fat-soluble nutrients for optimal absorption enhancement
dosage.with_food: Take with food, preferably with fat-soluble vitamins or other lipophilic compounds
dosage.special_populations: Well-tolerated; no known contraindications in standard populations

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated; rare: mild GI upset, nausea
contraindications: Soy allergy (use sunflower source to avoid)
Fish allergy (only if sourced from fish roe)

supplements: Enhances bioavailability of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)
Improves absorption of lipophilic compounds generally

name: Phosphorus
summary: Essential mineral for bone health, energy metabolism (ATP), and cell membrane structure. Second most abundant mineral in the body.
evidence_notes: Well-established essential nutrient
mechanism_of_action: Component of hydroxyapatite in bones and teeth

dosage.special_populations: Kidney disease patients should limit intake

serious_warnings: High phosphorus can affect calcium balance

supplements: High phosphorus may affect calcium absorption

practical_notes: Most people get enough from diet
Found in meat, dairy, legumes, nuts

name: Phytoestrogen Blend (Menopause)
summary: Combination of plant estrogens from multiple sources for gentle menopause support.
evidence_notes: Combined phytoestrogen approach may help some women

dosage.special_populations: Menopausal women seeking natural support

serious_warnings: Phytoestrogens - cancer history discuss with doctor

name: Pine Bark Extract (Skin Focus)
summary: Potent OPC antioxidant that supports skin elasticity, reduces UV damage, and improves circulation.
evidence_notes: Antioxidant skin support

dosage.special_populations: Skin aging, melasma, circulation issues

name: Pine Pollen Powder
summary: Pine pollen is a traditional botanical used in some cultures for general wellness and vitality, though human clinical evidence supporting specific health claims remains very limited. Most available data is anecdotal or derived from in vitro/animal studies.
mechanism_of_action: Contains amino acids, minerals, and trace elements
Possible phytoandrogen activity (theoretical, not established in humans)
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical trial data. No rigorous RCTs demonstrating efficacy for claimed uses in humans. Traditional use in Chinese and Ayurvedic medicine does not constitute clinical evidence. In vitro and animal studies show bioactive compounds but do not translate reliably to human benefit.

dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; may improve tolerability
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used continuously; optimal duration unknown
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in individuals with pollen allergies; not studied in pregnancy/lactation

contraindications: Known pollen allergy or hay fever
Autoimmune conditions (theoretical concern; not formally contraindicated)

medications: Potential interaction with hormone-sensitive medications (theoretical only; clinical significance unknown)

name: Organic Pineapple Powder
summary: Pineapple powder is a concentrated fruit source containing bromelain (a proteolytic enzyme), vitamin C, and manganese. Most research focuses on bromelain's potential anti-inflammatory and digestive effects, though whole fruit powder efficacy is less established than isolated bromelain extracts.
mechanism_of_action: Bromelain proteolytic enzyme may reduce inflammatory cytokines and support protein digestion
Vitamin C and antioxidants may support immune and connective tissue health
Manganese co-factor for superoxide dismutase antioxidant enzyme
evidence_notes: Bromelain has moderate clinical evidence for digestive and anti-inflammatory support, but whole pineapple powder lacks robust RCT data. Most studies use standardized bromelain extracts rather than fruit powder.

dosage.timing: With meals for digestive support; between meals for systemic effects
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food depending on intended use
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; typical supplementation studies range 4-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe; those with pineapple allergy should avoid

common_side_effects: Mild GI upset at higher doses
contraindications: Bleeding disorders (bromelain may have mild anticoagulant effects)

medications: ACE inhibitors (minor interaction reported with bromelain)
supplements: Other proteolytic enzymes (papain, serrapeptase) - additive effect unclear

name: Piper nigrum Extract (Seed) - 95% Piperine
summary: Piperine is the primary alkaloid from black pepper seeds with potent bioavailability-enhancing properties that significantly increase the absorption of other compounds. It also has mild anti-inflammatory and metabolic support properties.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibition of hepatic and intestinal glucuronidation
evidence_notes: Strong evidence as a bioavailability enhancer; multiple studies demonstrate 1300-2000% increased absorption of curcumin. Clinical evidence supports mild anti-inflammatory effects and metabolic support. Often used as a functional excipient rather than standalone active.

dosage.typical_range: 5-20 mg daily (as bioavailability enhancer); 50-200 mg for standalone use
dosage.timing: With meals or with curcumin/fat-soluble compounds
dosage.with_food: With food and fat sources for optimal effect
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; primarily used in combination products
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy: use only small amounts (culinary levels); nursing: caution advised

medications: CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP1A2 substrates — inhibits metabolism (increases bioavailability of co-administered drugs)
Diabetes medications — may enhance glucose-lowering effects

name: Piperine (Black Pepper Extract)
summary: Alkaloid from black pepper that significantly enhances absorption of many nutrients and supplements by inhibiting drug-metabolizing enzymes and increasing intestinal permeability.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for bioavailability enhancement of curcumin (2000%+), CoQ10, resveratrol, and many other compounds
mechanism_of_action: Inhibits CYP3A4 and P-glycoprotein efflux transporters
Inhibits glucuronidation in liver and intestine

dosage.with_food: Yes, take with supplements to enhance
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use at recommended doses
dosage.special_populations: Caution with narrow therapeutic index drugs

serious_warnings: May increase drug absorption - consult doctor if on medications
contraindications: Concurrent use with drugs having narrow therapeutic index without medical supervision
safety.liver_kidney_notes: May affect drug metabolism - caution with hepatically cleared medications

medications: Increases absorption of many drugs - consult healthcare provider
supplements: Enhances selenium, vitamin B6, vitamin C

practical_notes: Small amount goes a long way

name: Piperine (95% from Piper nigrum seed extract)
summary: Piperine is the primary alkaloid compound in black pepper that enhances nutrient bioavailability and has thermogenic and digestive properties. It is widely used as a bioenhancer to improve absorption of other supplements.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibition of intestinal glucuronidation and P-glycoprotein efflux
Enhanced nutrient absorption in the GI tract
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for bioenhancement of curcumin and other compounds; human studies confirm increased nutrient absorption; moderate evidence for independent health effects; widely used in clinical practice.

dosage.typical_range: 5-20 mg daily (as bioenhancer); up to 15 mg per dose with target nutrients
dosage.timing: With meals containing fat-soluble substances to enhance absorption
dosage.with_food: Strongly recommended with food, especially when used as a bioenhancer
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for chronic use; most bioenhancement studies use acute dosing
dosage.special_populations: Use cautiously in pregnancy; avoid if sensitive to black pepper; caution in GERD or GI ulcers

common_side_effects: Rare: GI irritation, heartburn at higher doses
Thermogenic effect may increase body temperature slightly
contraindications: Allergy to Piper nigrum or black pepper

medications: CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP2D6 substrates (may increase bioavailability and plasma levels)

name: Piracetam
summary: Original racetam and most studied synthetic nootropic. Enhances communication between brain hemispheres.
evidence_notes: Most studied racetam

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive enhancement, elderly

name: Piracetam + Choline Stack
summary: Classic racetam foundation. Choline essential to prevent headaches and enhance effects.
evidence_notes: Classic racetam stack

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive enhancement

name: Plant Protein (Pea/Rice Blend)
summary: Vegan complete protein when combined. Pea + rice provides complete amino acids.
evidence_notes: Evidence comparable to whey when combined correctly

dosage.special_populations: Vegans, dairy-free, egg-free

practical_notes: Blend pea + rice for complete protein

name: Plant Sterols/Stanols
summary: Block cholesterol absorption. FDA-approved claim for heart health. Add to diet.
evidence_notes: FDA-approved heart health claim

dosage.special_populations: Elevated LDL, family history of heart disease

practical_notes: Take with meals for absorption competition

citations.title: Plant sterols and cholesterol
citations.finding: 2g/day reduces LDL by 8-10%

citations.title: Plant sterols systematic review
citations.finding: Consistent LDL-lowering effect across populations

name: PMS Mood Support Stack
summary: PMS mood and symptom support: B6 + Magnesium + Calcium + Chasteberry.
evidence_notes: Components studied for PMS

dosage.special_populations: PMS sufferers

name: Polygala tenuifolia
summary: A traditional Chinese herb used for centuries to support memory, cognitive clarity, and emotional balance; the 20:1 extract represents a concentrated form standardized to preserve bioactive constituents. Modern research suggests potential nootropic and anxiolytic properties, though human clinical evidence remains limited.
evidence_notes: Traditional cognitive herb

dosage.special_populations: Memory, mild anxiety

name: Polygonum cuspidatum Extract (98% Trans-Resveratrol)
summary: A polyphenol extract from Japanese knotweed root standardized to 98% trans-resveratrol, a compound studied for antioxidant and cardiovascular support properties. Evidence supports resveratrol's antioxidant activity in vitro and some human studies, though clinical outcomes remain modest.
mechanism_of_action: Free radical scavenging and antioxidant activity
evidence_notes: Resveratrol has solid mechanistic evidence in vitro and animal models; human RCTs show modest benefits for cardiovascular markers and antioxidant status, but clinical outcomes are inconsistent. The 98% standardization is a strength for quality control.

dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble polyphenol benefits from dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Chronic use (8+ weeks) generally studied; benefits may require sustained intake
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; may interact with hormone therapies

contraindications: Estrogen-sensitive conditions (may have weak estrogenic activity)
History of cancer (conflicting data; consult provider)

medications: Hormone replacement therapy (theoretical weak estrogenic activity)

name: Polypodium Leucotomos
summary: Fern extract providing internal sun protection. Popular in dermatology for UV sensitivity.
evidence_notes: Dermatologist-recommended for sun sensitivity

dosage.special_populations: Fair skin, sun sensitivity, outdoor activities

name: Polyunsaturated Fat
summary: A macronutrient class containing multiple double bonds in the carbon chain, including omega-3 and omega-6 fatty acids essential for cell membrane structure and inflammatory regulation. Found abundantly in fish oil supplements as a source of EPA and DHA.
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence supports cardiovascular and metabolic benefits of dietary PUFAs, particularly omega-3 sources. Large prospective studies demonstrate consistent associations with reduced cardiovascular events.

dosage.typical_range: 1-3 grams daily (as part of total fat intake)
dosage.duration_notes: long-term consumption recommended for sustained cardiovascular benefits
dosage.special_populations: pregnant women should prioritize DHA-containing sources; individuals on anticoagulants should monitor intake

medications: antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel) - additive effect at high doses

name: Pomegranate Extract
summary: Potent antioxidant fruit extract rich in punicalagins and ellagic acid. Supports cardiovascular health, exercise performance, and prostate health.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for antioxidant effects and cardiovascular benefits
mechanism_of_action: Rich in punicalagins and ellagic acid

dosage.typical_range: 100-500 mg extract or 200-500 mL juice daily
dosage.special_populations: Cardiovascular support, athletes, prostate health

practical_notes: Whole fruit extract preferred over juice concentrate
Popular for prostate health in men

name: Pomegranate Juice Powder
summary: Pomegranate juice powder is a concentrated form of pomegranate fruit rich in polyphenols, particularly punicalagins and anthocyanins, with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Evidence supports benefits for cardiovascular health, inflammation, and endothelial function.
mechanism_of_action: Punicalagins and anthocyanins scavenge free radicals
Improves nitric oxide bioavailability, supporting vascular endothelial function
May inhibit ACE activity, supporting blood pressure regulation
evidence_notes: Multiple well-designed RCTs show modest but consistent benefits for blood pressure, endothelial function, and lipid profiles. Strong antioxidant capacity confirmed in multiple studies. Effect sizes are moderate; benefits typically appear after 4-8 weeks of use.

dosage.typical_range: 150-240 mL fresh juice daily, or 500-1000 mg powder daily (typically standardized to 30-65% punicalagins)
dosage.with_food: With or without food; may be better tolerated with food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; benefits typically appear after 4-8 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Well-tolerated in most populations; those with kidney disease should monitor potassium content

common_side_effects: Mild GI upset or constipation in sensitive individuals
Potential for staining teeth or clothing (juice form)
contraindications: Severe chronic kidney disease (monitor potassium due to high fruit content)

medications: CYP3A4 inhibition potential — may affect metabolism of statins, calcium channel blockers, immunosuppressants (though clinical significance is generally low)
Warfarin — possible interaction; monitor INR if consuming large amounts
ACE inhibitors, ARBs — additive blood pressure effects (monitor blood pressure)
supplements: Other polyphenol-rich supplements — additive antioxidant effects (likely beneficial but monitor for interactions)

name: POMELLA® Pomegranate Extract (Punica granatum fruit)
summary: POMELLA is a standardized pomegranate fruit extract rich in ellagic acid and punicalagins, polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Research suggests benefits for cardiovascular health, blood pressure, and cognitive function, though evidence remains moderate.
mechanism_of_action: Reduction of oxidative stress and free radicals
evidence_notes: Pomegranate extract has multiple published RCTs supporting cardiovascular and antioxidant benefits, with consistent effects on blood pressure and endothelial function. POMELLA specifically is well-standardized and studied, but long-term efficacy data is limited compared to established cardiovascular agents.

dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble polyphenols absorb better with food
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent daily use for 8-12 weeks typically needed to observe cardiovascular benefits
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe; those on anticoagulants should consult healthcare provider due to potential additive effects

common_side_effects: Rare: mild gastrointestinal upset at high doses
contraindications: Concurrent use with anticoagulant medications (warfarin, etc.) — potential additive effects
Concurrent use with antiplatelet agents — use cautiously and monitor

medications: Anticoagulants (warfarin, apixaban) — potential additive anticoagulant effects
CYP3A4 substrates — pomegranate may inhibit this enzyme
supplements: Other polyphenol-rich extracts — additive antioxidant effects (generally favorable)
Fish oil/omega-3 — may enhance cardiovascular effects

name: Poria
summary: Poria is a medicinal fungus used in Traditional Chinese Medicine primarily as a digestive and immune-supporting agent. It contains polysaccharides and triterpenes with immunomodulatory and mild sedative properties.
evidence_notes: Limited human RCT evidence; traditional use is extensive in TCM; most evidence is from in vitro studies or small observational reports; quality studies are lacking.

dosage.timing: Typically with meals or as directed in traditional formulas
dosage.with_food: Often combined with other herbs in decoctions; take with food if GI sensitive
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use spans weeks to months; safe for long-term use based on history
dosage.special_populations: Minimal data in pregnancy/nursing; generally considered safe in traditional medicine

contraindications: Kidney qi deficiency (TCM contraindication — excessive diuretic effect)

supplements: Other diuretic or drying herbs (additive effects)

name: Post-Workout Recovery Shake
summary: Protein + carbs for optimal recovery. The anabolic window is real but wider than thought.
evidence_notes: Protein + carb combination well-established for recovery

dosage.special_populations: All athletes post-training

common_side_effects: GI upset if drunk too fast

practical_notes: Anabolic window is 2-3 hours, not 30 min

name: Postbiotics
summary: Metabolites and cell components from probiotics without live organisms. Good option for those who can't tolerate live probiotics.
evidence_notes: Emerging category with benefits
mechanism_of_action: Includes SCFAs, peptides, cell wall components

dosage.special_populations: Immunocompromised who can't use live probiotics

name: Potassium
summary: Essential electrolyte for blood pressure and heart function. Most Americans are deficient.
evidence_notes: Essential, widely deficient

dosage.special_populations: Hypertension, diuretic users, heavy sweaters

moderate_dose.label: 99 mg per supplement pill
moderate_dose.notes: FDA caps OTC pills at 99 mg

optimal_dose.label: Diet-first; supplements limited
optimal_dose.notes: Most intake should come from food

upper_limit.label: No UL from food; supplement caution if kidney issues

citations.title: Potassium and blood pressure
citations.finding: Increased potassium intake reduces blood pressure

citations.title: Potassium and cardiovascular health
citations.finding: Higher intake associated with lower stroke risk

name: Potassium Bicarbonate
summary: Potassium bicarbonate is a mineral salt that provides bioavailable potassium, an essential electrolyte critical for cardiac function, muscle contraction, and blood pressure regulation. It is commonly used as a potassium supplementation form with potential alkalizing properties.
mechanism_of_action: Replaces intracellular and extracellular potassium to maintain Na+/K+ ATPase function
Supports cardiac action potential and muscle excitability
May provide mild alkalizing effect through bicarbonate ion
evidence_notes: Potassium is an essential mineral with extensive RCT evidence supporting its role in blood pressure regulation, cardiovascular health, and electrolyte homeostasis. Potassium bicarbonate specifically has been studied for bioavailability and safety in clinical trials.

dosage.typical_range: 1,500–3,500 mg daily (adequate intake varies by age/sex; upper limit typically 4,000–5,600 mg from supplements)
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; spread throughout day if high doses
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with food may reduce GI irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use when intake remains within recommended ranges
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with kidney disease, diabetes, or on ACE inhibitors/ARBs should consult a healthcare provider before supplementing; pregnant/nursing women should follow RDA guidelines

common_side_effects: Gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, diarrhea) at high doses
Mild bitter taste in powder form
contraindications: Concurrent use with potassium-sparing diuretics without medical supervision

medications: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) — increased hyperkalemia risk
Angiotensin II receptor blockers (ARBs) — increased hyperkalemia risk
NSAIDs — may reduce renal potassium excretion
supplements: Other potassium supplements — risk of excessive intake
High-dose magnesium — may potentiate electrolyte effects

name: Potassium (as Potassium Chloride)
summary: Potassium is an essential electrolyte mineral critical for heart rhythm, muscle contraction, and blood pressure regulation. Strong clinical evidence supports potassium supplementation for maintaining normal cardiovascular and neuromuscular function, particularly in populations with deficiency or high sodium intake.
mechanism_of_action: Maintains intracellular osmotic pressure and fluid balance
Regulates cardiac action potentials and heart rhythm
Modulates vascular tone and blood pressure
evidence_notes: Potassium is a well-established essential nutrient with extensive RCT evidence supporting cardiovascular and blood pressure benefits. Deficiency correction is well-documented in clinical literature.

dosage.typical_range: 1500-4700 mg daily (Adequate Intake: 2600 mg for women, 3400 mg for men)
dosage.timing: With meals or water; divided doses preferred
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term daily use appropriate for maintenance; therapeutic dosing should follow clinical guidance
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with kidney disease, diabetes, or taking ACE inhibitors/potassium-sparing diuretics require medical supervision. Supplementation contraindicated in hyperkalemia.

common_side_effects: Nausea and vomiting (especially on empty stomach)
contraindications: Taking potassium-sparing diuretics without medical supervision
ACE inhibitor or ARB use (without physician approval)
Hyperkalemia or elevated serum potassium levels

medications: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) — increased potassium levels
Angiotensin II receptor blockers (losartan, valsartan) — increased potassium levels
supplements: High-dose sodium supplementation — may counteract potassium benefits
Other potassium-containing supplements — cumulative effect risk

name: Potassium Citrate
summary: Essential electrolyte for heart, muscle, and fluid balance. Citrate form may help prevent kidney stones.
evidence_notes: Essential electrolyte

dosage.special_populations: Kidney disease consult doctor

citations.title: Potassium and blood pressure
citations.finding: Increased potassium intake reduces blood pressure

citations.title: Potassium and cardiovascular health
citations.finding: Higher potassium intake associated with lower stroke risk

name: Potassium (as Potassium Gluconate)
summary: Potassium is an essential electrolyte critical for cardiac function, muscle contraction, and fluid balance. Potassium gluconate is an organic salt form commonly used in supplements for bioavailability and gastrointestinal tolerance.
evidence_notes: Potassium is an essential mineral with extensive clinical evidence supporting its role in cardiovascular health, blood pressure regulation, and electrolyte homeostasis. Well-established RCT evidence from decades of research.

dosage.timing: With meals; split doses if taking higher amounts
dosage.with_food: Recommended to reduce gastrointestinal irritation and enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term supplementation is appropriate for individuals with inadequate dietary intake; compliance should be monitored
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women: 2,600–2,900 mg daily; do not exceed recommended intake without medical supervision. Individuals with kidney disease, ACE inhibitor use, or potassium-sparing diuretics require medical monitoring.

contraindications: Kidney disease or impaired renal function
Concurrent use of ACE inhibitors or potassium-sparing diuretics without medical oversight

medications: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) — increased hyperkalemia risk
Potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone) — additive effect, hyperkalemia risk
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) — may reduce potassium excretion
Beta-blockers — may increase serum potassium levels
Trimethoprim-containing antibiotics — may increase potassium retention
supplements: Other potassium supplements — cumulative dose risk
Salt substitutes (often potassium-based) — additive potassium content

name: Potassium (as potassium iodide)
summary: An essential electrolyte critical for nerve and muscle function, heart rhythm, and blood pressure regulation. Potassium iodide is a dual-purpose compound that delivers both potassium and iodine in one ingredient.
mechanism_of_action: Essential for sodium-potassium ATPase pump function
evidence_notes: Extensive evidence for essential role in electrolyte balance and cardiovascular health; well-characterized mechanisms; strong evidence for cardiovascular benefits from adequate intake.

dosage.typical_range: In multivitamins, typically minimal amounts (under 10 mg); adequate intake for adults 51+ is 1,000-1,200 mg daily from food sources primarily
dosage.timing: With meals to avoid GI upset
dosage.with_food: Should be taken with food to prevent stomach irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Multivitamin amounts are typically very low and safe; high-dose supplementation requires medical supervision
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with kidney disease, heart disease, or taking ACE inhibitors/ARBs should consult healthcare provider; those on certain blood pressure medications need monitoring

common_side_effects: GI upset when taken on empty stomach
contraindications: Use with ACE inhibitors, ARBs, NSAIDs without medical supervision

medications: ACE inhibitors: may increase potassium levels dangerously
ARBs: similar concern as ACE inhibitors
Diuretics: may deplete potassium (potassium-sparing diuretics increase it)
Cardiac glycosides: potassium status affects toxicity

name: Potassium Orotate
summary: Potassium orotate is a mineral salt combining potassium with orotic acid, proposed to support cellular energy metabolism and cardiovascular function. Evidence for orotate forms is limited compared to standard potassium sources, with most research on orotic acid itself rather than the potassium salt.
mechanism_of_action: Potassium supports cellular membrane potential and electrolyte homeostasis
Orotic acid is involved in nucleotide synthesis and may support cellular energy metabolism
Combined form proposed to enhance bioavailability and utilization
evidence_notes: Potassium itself has strong evidence (grade A) for electrolyte balance, but orotate as a delivery form lacks robust human clinical trials. Limited peer-reviewed data on potassium orotate specifically; most support derives from theoretical mechanisms and older European research on orotic acid.

dosage.timing: Can be divided into 2-3 doses; timing flexible
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; food may enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use as part of balanced mineral intake
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with kidney disease, hyperkalemia, or on ACE inhibitors/ARBs should consult healthcare provider before supplementing potassium

contraindications: Chronic kidney disease or renal impairment
Concurrent use of potassium-sparing diuretics without medical supervision
ACE inhibitor or ARB use without physician guidance

medications: ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril) - may increase potassium levels
Angiotensin II receptor blockers (losartan, valsartan) - may increase potassium levels
supplements: Other potassium supplements - additive effect; coordinate dosing
High-dose magnesium - may affect mineral balance

name: Potassium (for Relaxation)
summary: Essential electrolyte for muscle relaxation and nerve function. Often deficient.
evidence_notes: Essential electrolyte

dosage.special_populations: Muscle cramps, hypertension

serious_warnings: Hyperkalemia if too much + kidney issues

citations.title: Potassium and muscle function
citations.finding: Adequate potassium prevents muscle cramps and supports relaxation

citations.title: Potassium health effects
citations.finding: Potassium important for nerve and muscle function

name: PQQ (Pyrroloquinoline Quinone)
summary: Supports mitochondrial biogenesis - the creation of new mitochondria. Works synergistically with CoQ10.
evidence_notes: Promotes new mitochondria

dosage.special_populations: Aging, cognitive support

name: Pramiracetam
summary: Most potent racetam for raw memory enhancement. Highly fat-soluble with focused cognitive effects.
evidence_notes: Strongest memory-focused racetam

dosage.special_populations: Memory-focused goals

practical_notes: Must take with fat and choline

name: Pregnenolone
summary: Precursor hormone supporting overall steroid hormone production including cortisol.
evidence_notes: Limited clinical evidence

dosage.special_populations: Age-related decline

name: Micronized Pregnenolone
summary: A steroid hormone precursor derived from cholesterol, positioned as the upstream substrate for both DHEA and progesterone pathways. Micronization improves bioavailability.
evidence_notes: Limited human RCT evidence; most data from preclinical and animal studies. Few rigorous studies in healthy adults. Micronization may improve absorption but clinical significance unclear.

dosage.with_food: With food, preferably containing fat, to enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Effects may take 4-8 weeks to manifest; long-term safety data in healthy adults is limited
dosage.special_populations: Not recommended during pregnancy/nursing; use cautiously in hormone-sensitive conditions

medications: May interact with hormone replacement therapy

name: PrimaVie® Purified Shilajit
summary: PrimaVie® is a standardized, purified shilajit extract containing fulvic acid and trace minerals, traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine for energy and vitality. Limited but emerging clinical evidence suggests potential benefits for energy metabolism and nutrient absorption, though human studies remain preliminary.
mechanism_of_action: Fulvic acid enhances mineral and nutrient absorption
evidence_notes: PrimaVie® is a proprietary standardized form with a few published human studies showing modest benefits for energy and cognitive function, but the evidence base remains moderate and limited. Most research is traditional/observational or conducted in small populations. Standardization to fulvic acid content supports consistency but does not elevate overall evidence confidence.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with food or on empty stomach; food may enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; traditional use suggests 2-8 weeks for noticeable effects
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; may interact with anticoagulants or medications affecting iron absorption

common_side_effects: Rare: headache or dizziness at high doses
contraindications: Gout or elevated uric acid (shilajit may raise uric acid levels)
Active bleeding disorders (may have anticoagulant properties)
Sickle cell disease or thalassemia (iron accumulation risk)

medications: Iron supplements (may enhance absorption, monitor serum iron)
supplements: Other mineral supplements (additive trace mineral intake)

name: PRL-8-53
summary: Obscure synthetic nootropic with dramatic memory enhancement in limited studies.
evidence_notes: Limited but impressive research

dosage.special_populations: Memory experimentation

name: Progesterone Cream (Bio-identical)
summary: Actual progesterone in cream form, requires practitioner guidance for hormone therapy.
evidence_notes: Effective for progesterone delivery when properly prescribed

dosage.special_populations: Women with low progesterone, luteal phase defect, menopause

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Used in some pregnancy support protocols - Rx only

name: Prokinetic Herbal Blend
summary: Combination of ginger, artichoke, and other herbs supporting healthy digestive motility.
evidence_notes: Component herbs well-studied

dosage.special_populations: Gastroparesis, post-SIBO prevention, slow transit

name: Prostate Health Stack
summary: Comprehensive prostate support: Saw Palmetto, Beta-Sitosterol, Pygeum, Nettle Root, Zinc, Lycopene.
evidence_notes: Evidence-based comprehensive prostate approach

dosage.typical_range: Saw Palmetto 320mg + Beta-Sitosterol 60-130mg + Pygeum 100mg + Nettle Root 300mg + Zinc 15-30mg + Lycopene 10-15mg + Selenium 100-200mcg
dosage.special_populations: Men with BPH or seeking prostate health

name: Protein
summary: Protein is a macronutrient composed of amino acids essential for building and repairing muscle tissue, supporting immune function, and maintaining overall body composition. It is obtained through diet or supplementation and is critical for athletes, active individuals, and general health maintenance.
mechanism_of_action: Provides amino acids for muscle protein synthesis
Stimulates mTOR pathway for anabolic signaling
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence demonstrates protein supplementation significantly supports muscle growth and recovery, particularly when combined with resistance training. Well-established macronutrient with decades of research.

dosage.timing: Post-workout timing is most effective; can be consumed throughout the day
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; post-workout absorption may be enhanced with carbohydrates
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used long-term as part of regular supplementation
dosage.special_populations: Requirements increase with age (65+ may need 1.2-1.6 g/kg body weight); individualize based on activity level and training goals

common_side_effects: Bloating or gas (particularly with plant-based proteins)

supplements: May enhance effects of resistance training when combined with creatine

name: Prunes/Prune Juice (Sorbitol)
summary: Natural food-based laxative containing sorbitol and fiber for gentle constipation relief.
evidence_notes: Well-established, gentle, food-based

dosage.special_populations: Mild constipation, elderly, those preferring food-based

name: Psyllium Husk
summary: Soluble fiber that forms a gel in the gut, supporting regularity, cholesterol reduction, and blood sugar management.
evidence_notes: FDA-approved health claims for cholesterol

dosage.timing: Before meals with large glass of water
dosage.special_populations: Increase water intake

medications: Separate from all meds by 2 hours

practical_notes: Drink 8+ oz water with each dose

citations.finding: Psyllium significantly reduces LDL cholesterol

citations.title: Psyllium and glycemic control
citations.finding: Psyllium reduces postprandial glucose response

name: Pterostilbene
summary: Methylated resveratrol with 4x better bioavailability. Similar longevity pathway activation.
evidence_notes: Better absorbed resveratrol analog

dosage.special_populations: Longevity, cognitive focus

moderate_dose.label: 50-150 mg/day
moderate_dose.notes: Lower dose due to better bioavailability vs resveratrol

name: Pumpkin Powder (Cucurbita pepo)
summary: Pumpkin powder is a whole-food ingredient derived from pumpkin fruit containing carotenoids, fiber, and various phytonutrients. Limited clinical evidence exists for isolated health claims, though pumpkin is a nutrient-dense food with traditional use in digestive and general wellness applications.
mechanism_of_action: Provides carotenoids (beta-carotene, lutein) with antioxidant properties
Contains dietary fiber supporting digestive function
Supplies micronutrients including potassium, magnesium, and vitamin C
evidence_notes: Pumpkin is a whole food with established nutrient content, but clinical evidence specifically testing pumpkin powder supplementation is limited. Most evidence is observational or relates to whole pumpkin consumption. Grade C reflects preliminary/moderate evidence base for supplement form.

dosage.typical_range: 1-3 teaspoons (5-15 g) daily or as directed
dosage.timing: Any time of day; can be mixed into beverages, smoothies, or foods
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; mixing with fat may enhance carotenoid absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use as a whole-food supplement
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for most populations; individuals with pumpkin allergy should avoid

common_side_effects: Rarely reported; mild gastrointestinal effects possible if sensitive to high fiber
contraindications: Cross-reactivity with Cucurbitaceae family allergies (melon, cucumber, zucchini)

name: Pumpkin Seed
summary: Pumpkin seed oil is a rich source of polyunsaturated fatty acids, phytosterols, and polyphenols that may support prostate and urinary health, particularly in men with benign prostatic hyperplasia. Limited clinical evidence supports its traditional use, with most benefits attributed to its lipid and micronutrient composition.
primary_uses: Urinary function in benign prostatic hyperplasia
evidence_notes: Some clinical evidence for hair

dosage.special_populations: Hair thinning, DHT-sensitive hair loss

name: Punarnava
summary: Ayurvedic herb for kidney and fluid balance. Traditional diuretic and rejuvenative supporting healthy water balance.
evidence_notes: Traditional use for kidney and water balance, limited modern research

dosage.special_populations: Fluid retention, kidney support

safety.liver_kidney_notes: Traditional kidney support but avoid in severe kidney disease

name: Pycnogenol (Pine Bark Extract)
summary: Antioxidant that enhances nitric oxide production. Cardiovascular and pump support.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for cardiovascular and NO

dosage.special_populations: Cardiovascular support, pump

name: Pycnogenol (Prelox for ED)
summary: Pine bark extract that with L-Arginine (Prelox) has evidence for erectile dysfunction.
evidence_notes: Good evidence when combined with L-Arginine (Prelox)

dosage.special_populations: Mild-moderate ED, endothelial dysfunction

practical_notes: Prelox (with L-Arginine) is the studied formula

name: Pygeum Africanum Extract
summary: Pygeum africanum bark extract is traditionally used to support urinary and prostate health, with moderate clinical evidence suggesting potential benefits for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) symptoms. Active compounds include phytosterols and triterpenes that may influence prostate function.
mechanism_of_action: Phytosterol content may inhibit prostaglandin and leukotriene synthesis
Potential anti-inflammatory effects on prostate tissue
May modulate DHT-related pathways in prostate cells
evidence_notes: Multiple clinical trials demonstrate moderate efficacy for BPH symptom relief (improved urinary flow, reduced nocturia), though evidence is not as robust as saw palmetto or finasteride. Studies primarily from European sources with reasonable methodological quality.

dosage.typical_range: 100-200 mg daily (standardized to 14% triterpenes or similar)
dosage.timing: With meals, typically divided into 1-2 doses
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to improve absorption and reduce GI irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Clinical benefits typically observed after 6-12 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: avoid use; consult healthcare provider if using with hormone-sensitive conditions

contraindications: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy (theoretical risk)
Hormone-sensitive prostate or breast conditions (use with caution)

medications: 5-alpha reductase inhibitors like finasteride (additive but generally considered safe)
supplements: Saw palmetto (both traditional prostate agents; may have complementary effects)

name: Pygeum Africanum (Prostate)
summary: African tree bark extract with evidence for BPH and urinary symptoms.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for BPH symptoms, often combined with saw palmetto

dosage.special_populations: BPH, especially with nocturia

name: Pyridoxal 5'-Phosphate
summary: The active coenzyme form of vitamin B6, directly utilized in amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and immune function without requiring hepatic conversion.
evidence_notes: Well-established biochemical role as active B6 form. Strong evidence for deficiency correction and role in disease prevention. Bioavailable and effective in clinical settings.

dosage.with_food: Take with food for optimal absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; effects accumulate over time
dosage.special_populations: Safe in pregnancy (supports fetal development); safe for all ages at recommended doses

common_side_effects: Rare at typical doses; very well tolerated

medications: Levodopa (L-DOPA): may reduce efficacy at high B6 doses
supplements: Generally compatible with other B vitamins and supplements

name: Pyroglutamic Acid
summary: An amino acid intermediate in glutathione metabolism and protein synthesis. Used as a nootropic due to proposed cognitive and memory-supporting properties.
evidence_notes: Older clinical literature (1970s-1980s) suggests cognitive benefits, but modern RCT evidence is sparse. Primarily used in European supplements; limited contemporary human data.

dosage.duration_notes: Typically used long-term; effects may develop gradually over weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally regarded as safe; limited pediatric data

common_side_effects: Rare; mild GI upset possible at high doses

supplements: Compatible with most amino acid supplements

name: PQQ (Pyrroloquinoline Quinone)
summary: Redox cofactor supporting mitochondrial biogenesis and cognitive function. One of few compounds shown to stimulate new mitochondria formation.
evidence_notes: Unique mitochondrial biogenesis support, growing research

dosage.special_populations: Mitochondrial support, cognitive enhancement

practical_notes: Take morning to avoid sleep issues

name: Quercetin
summary: Quercetin is a flavonoid antioxidant with anti-inflammatory properties; the Phytosome® delivery system uses phospholipids to enhance absorption compared to standard quercetin. Evidence supports potential benefits for allergies, cardiovascular health, and exercise recovery, though human clinical data remains moderate.
evidence_notes: Broad anti-inflammatory effects

dosage.special_populations: Allergies, inflammation

name: Quercetin (Immune)
summary: Flavonoid with broad antiviral activity, zinc ionophore function, and anti-inflammatory properties.
evidence_notes: Promising research for viral infections

dosage.duration_notes: During illness or ongoing for allergies
dosage.special_populations: Those seeking antiviral or allergy support

serious_warnings: May affect thyroid function at very high doses
safety.liver_kidney_notes: Caution at high doses with kidney issues

practical_notes: Take with zinc for antiviral effect

name: Quercetin Dihydrate (from Sophora japonica bud extract)
summary: A plant-derived bioflavonoid extracted from Sophora japonica buds with potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Quercetin is one of the most abundant flavonoids in plant foods.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects; moderate evidence for seasonal allergy support. Bioavailability improved with dihydrate form.

dosage.typical_range: 250–1000 mg daily, often split into 2–3 doses
dosage.with_food: With food; bioavailability enhanced by fat or quercetin transporters
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for continuous use; effects may accumulate over weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe; those on blood thinners should monitor with healthcare provider

common_side_effects: Rare; headache or stomach upset at high doses

medications: May have mild interactions with blood thinners and certain antibiotics

name: Quercetin (as Zinc Ionophore)
summary: Quercetin functions as a zinc ionophore, helping transport zinc into cells. Synergistic pairing for immune and metabolic benefits.
evidence_notes: Ionophore function established in research

dosage.special_populations: Immune support, viral illness

name: Quercetin (Senolytic Focus)
summary: Flavonoid with senolytic properties (clears senescent cells) when combined with dasatinib or fisetin.
evidence_notes: Part of senolytic protocols

dosage.special_populations: Anti-aging, longevity protocols

serious_warnings: Thyroid hormone concerns at very high doses

name: R-ALA Cyclodextrin
summary: R-ALA (R-enantiomer of alpha-lipoic acid) complexed with cyclodextrin is designed to enhance bioavailability and stability of R-ALA, the more biologically active form of lipoic acid. R-ALA has antioxidant and mitochondrial support properties with evidence primarily from in vitro and animal studies.
mechanism_of_action: Direct free radical scavenging and antioxidant activity
Cofactor for mitochondrial α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and pyruvate dehydrogenase complexes
Regeneration of endogenous antioxidants (vitamins C and E, glutathione)
Cyclodextrin complexation enhances solubility and absorption
evidence_notes: R-ALA itself has moderate human evidence for peripheral neuropathy and glucose metabolism support, but most evidence is for racemic ALA. The cyclodextrin complex form lacks dedicated human clinical trials; bioavailability claims are based on pharmaceutical technology rationale rather than direct human validation.

dosage.timing: With meals for consistency; some practitioners recommend morning dosing
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food, though fat-soluble properties suggest food may aid absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Typical use 8-12 weeks for assessment of effects; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: limited data, caution advised. Diabetics taking medications: monitor blood glucose due to potential synergistic effects.

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to lipoic acid or cyclodextrin
Caution in patients with thiamine deficiency (ALA may further reduce thiamine)

medications: Diabetes medications: potential additive glucose-lowering effects, monitor blood glucose
Thyroid medications: some evidence ALA may reduce absorption, separate dosing recommended
Chemotherapy agents: limited data, consult healthcare provider
supplements: Vitamin E, C, other antioxidants: potential redundancy or synergy (not well-characterized)

name: R-Alpha Lipoic Acid
summary: R-alpha lipoic acid is the biologically active S-enantiomer of alpha-lipoic acid, a mitochondrial cofactor with antioxidant and metabolic effects. It has stronger evidence for neuropathic pain and metabolic health compared to the racemic mixture, though human data remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Recycling of vitamins C and E
evidence_notes: Strong mechanistic rationale and good clinical evidence for diabetic neuropathy (especially IV formulations) and metabolic markers. R-enantiomer shows superior bioavailability and activity versus racemic ALA. Oral data less robust than IV but still supportive.

dosage.timing: With or without food; divided doses may improve absorption
dosage.with_food: Can take with or without food, though food may slow absorption slightly
dosage.duration_notes: Typically requires 4-12 weeks of consistent use for neuropathy effects; can be used long-term
dosage.special_populations: Diabetic individuals should monitor blood glucose; may enhance insulin sensitivity

common_side_effects: Hypoglycemia risk in diabetics on medications
contraindications: Thiamine (B1) deficiency risk with very high doses
Caution in those with biotin deficiency

supplements: High-dose biotin may compete for absorption
Synergistic with other antioxidants (vitamins C, E, CoQ10)

name: Racetam + Choline Stack
summary: Foundational nootropic combination. Choline prevents headaches and enhances racetam effects.
evidence_notes: Essential combination for racetam users

dosage.typical_range: Varies by racetam + 300-600mg Alpha-GPC or CDP-choline
dosage.special_populations: All racetam users

name: Raspberry Ketones
summary: Raspberry ketones are aromatic compounds found in red raspberries that have been studied for potential metabolic and fat-loss effects, primarily in animal models. Human clinical evidence is limited and results have been modest at best.
evidence_notes: Limited human RCT data; most evidence from cell culture and animal studies. Available human studies show minimal or inconsistent weight loss effects. Marketed heavily but lacks robust clinical validation for claimed benefits.

dosage.with_food: may improve absorption; commonly taken with food
dosage.duration_notes: typical supplementation periods in studies range 4-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: safety data in pregnant/nursing women is limited; not recommended

common_side_effects: rare reports of jitteriness or mild GI upset

name: Raspberry Powder (Rubus idaeus)
summary: Raspberry powder is a concentrated form of red raspberry fruit containing polyphenols, anthocyanins, and ellagic acid with antioxidant properties. Research supports modest benefits for antioxidant status and metabolic health, though human clinical evidence remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Antioxidant activity via anthocyanins and ellagic acid
evidence_notes: While raspberry contains well-characterized bioactive compounds (anthocyanins, ellagic acid, vitamin C), human clinical trials are limited and mostly small. Animal and in vitro studies show promise, but robust RCT evidence in humans is lacking. Grade C reflects moderate preliminary evidence.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may enhance absorption of fat-soluble compounds if taken with meals
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; benefits accumulate with consistent intake over weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy in food amounts; consult healthcare provider if using therapeutically during pregnancy or lactation

common_side_effects: Potential mild digestive upset at very high doses
contraindications: Individuals with salicylate sensitivity may theoretically react (salicylate-containing plant)

medications: May have mild anticoagulant properties; theoretical interaction with blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin) at very high doses, though unlikely at supplement levels
supplements: No significant documented interactions with common supplements

name: Rauwolscine
summary: Rauwolscine is an alkaloid from Rauwolfia vomitoria that acts as an alpha-2 adrenergic receptor antagonist, structurally similar to yohimbine but with potential distinct pharmacological properties. It is used for thermogenesis and fat loss support.
mechanism_of_action: Inhibition of negative feedback on norepinephrine release
Enhanced fat oxidation and metabolic rate
evidence_notes: Limited direct human evidence compared to yohimbine. Most data is derived from in vitro studies and pharmacological theory. Some animal studies support thermogenic effects, but robust RCT data in humans is lacking.

dosage.timing: Morning or early afternoon, away from bedtime
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; consistent timing recommended
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used for 8-12 week cycles with breaks; long-term safety data is limited
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in individuals with cardiovascular conditions, hypertension, or anxiety disorders; not recommended during pregnancy or breastfeeding

common_side_effects: Increased heart rate and blood pressure
contraindications: Concurrent use with stimulants or certain psychiatric medications

medications: Antidepressants (especially SSRIs, SNRIs — potential serotonin interaction)
supplements: Other adrenergic agonists (caffeine, synephrine, yohimbine — additive effects)

name: Red Clover (Isoflavones)
summary: Isoflavone-rich herb with mild estrogenic activity for menopause symptoms.
evidence_notes: Mixed evidence, mild effects for menopause

dosage.special_populations: Menopausal women seeking mild support

name: Red Light Therapy (Photobiomodulation)
summary: Low-level light therapy using red and near-infrared wavelengths. Supports mitochondrial function, recovery, skin, and pain.
evidence_notes: Growing research base for multiple applications

dosage.special_populations: Recovery, skin, pain, mitochondrial support

name: Red Maca Powder
summary: Red maca is a root vegetable from Peru traditionally used for sexual function and fertility, with preliminary evidence suggesting potential benefits for sexual desire and reproductive health. Most clinical evidence remains limited and further research is needed to establish efficacy.
mechanism_of_action: Contains alkaloids and glucosinolates that may influence hormonal signaling
Potential vasodilatory effects on blood flow
Possible adaptogenic properties supporting stress resilience
evidence_notes: Moderate preliminary evidence exists for maca's effects on sexual function in small RCTs, but sample sizes are limited and research quality varies. Red maca specifically has less robust evidence than generic maca extracts. Grade reflects preliminary but not yet conclusive clinical data.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with meals or as part of beverages
dosage.with_food: May be better tolerated with food
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests 6-12 weeks for noticeable effects; most studies examine 8-12 week periods
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: limited safety data; use with caution. Those with hormone-sensitive conditions should consult healthcare provider.

contraindications: Thyroid disorders (may contain goitrogens in high amounts)

medications: Blood pressure medications (maca may have mild vasodilatory effects)

name: Red Yeast Rice
summary: Contains natural statins (monacolin K). Effective but needs same precautions as Rx statins.
evidence_notes: Contains natural statin compounds

dosage.special_populations: High cholesterol, statin-intolerant

serious_warnings: Same as prescription statins (muscle damage, liver)

citations.title: Red yeast rice for hyperlipidemia
citations.finding: Reduces LDL cholesterol comparably to low-dose statins

citations.title: Red yeast rice safety review
citations.finding: Generally well-tolerated; contains natural monacolin K

name: Reishi
summary: The 'mushroom of immortality' - adaptogenic fungus that modulates immune function, reduces stress, and supports longevity.
evidence_notes: Good immune and calming evidence

dosage.special_populations: Immune, sleep, stress

upper_limit.label: Mild stomach upset at high doses

name: Reishi (Ganoderma lucidum s.l.)
summary: Reishi is a polypore mushroom used for centuries in Traditional Chinese Medicine as an adaptogenic tonic for stress, sleep, and immune support. Moderate clinical evidence supports anxiolytic and sleep-promoting effects.
evidence_notes: Multiple human trials show benefits for sleep, anxiety, and immune markers. Evidence is moderate quality; some studies are small or lack rigorous controls, but consistent benefit signals exist.

dosage.typical_range: 1,000–2,500 mg daily (dried mycelium biomass); extract forms often 300–900 mg
dosage.timing: evening for sleep support; morning/throughout day for general adaptogenic effects
dosage.with_food: may be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term chronic use; benefits may emerge over 4–12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; immunosuppressed patients should check with provider

medications: potential weak anticoagulant effect at high doses
possible interaction with sedating medications (additive effect)

name: Relora (Magnolia + Phellodendron)
summary: Patented blend for stress eating and cortisol. Combines magnolia bark and phellodendron.
evidence_notes: Studied for stress-related eating and cortisol

dosage.timing: With meals or before stressful situations
dosage.special_populations: Stress eating, high cortisol

name: Rephyl® (Piper nigrum) Extract
summary: Rephyl® is a standardized black pepper (Piper nigrum) fruit extract concentrated for beta-caryophyllene (6%), a sesquiterpene with potential anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective properties. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most research is in vitro or animal models.
evidence_notes: Beta-caryophyllene shows promising in vitro and animal data for anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects, but human clinical trials in humans are limited and small. Rephyl® as a proprietary standardized extract lacks dedicated RCT evidence in humans.

dosage.typical_range: 500-1500 mg daily (as standardized extract; approximately 30-90 mg beta-caryophyllene)
dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble compound absorption enhanced with food
dosage.duration_notes: No standard duration established; longer-term safety data lacking
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; use in children not established

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to black pepper or related Piperaceae species
Caution with aspirin sensitivity or bleeding disorders (preliminary evidence for mild anticoagulant effect)

medications: Potential interaction with anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (limited evidence)
supplements: Interaction with other CB2 modulators not well characterized

name: Rephyll (Piper nigrum) Extract with Beta-Caryophyllene
summary: Rephyll is a standardized black pepper (Piper nigrum) fruit extract concentrated for beta-caryophyllene (BCP), a sesquiterpene with preliminary evidence for anti-inflammatory and neuroprotective effects. Most human data remains limited, with the majority of research conducted in vitro or in animal models.
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trial data; most evidence is preclinical (in vitro/animal). Beta-caryophyllene shows mechanistic promise but lacks robust RCT confirmation in humans. Rephyll as a branded extract is under-studied relative to component BCP.

dosage.typical_range: 100-200 mg daily (based on 6-10% BCP concentration; equivalent to ~6-20 mg BCP)
dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble compound benefits from dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: No established maximum duration; most preliminary studies use 4-12 week periods
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/nursing: insufficient data; those on anticoagulants should consult healthcare provider

contraindications: Anticoagulant/antiplatelet medications (theoretical bleeding risk; limited evidence)
Allergy to Piper nigrum or related plants

medications: Potential CYP3A4 interaction (black pepper compounds; minor concern)
supplements: No major interactions documented; piperine (another black pepper component) may enhance absorption of other supplements

name: Resistant Starch
summary: Starch that resists digestion and feeds colon bacteria. Supports gut health and insulin sensitivity.
evidence_notes: Emerging prebiotic benefits

dosage.special_populations: Diabetics, gut health focus

practical_notes: Start with 1 tsp and build up

name: Resveratrol
summary: Polyphenol from grapes activating longevity pathways. Sirtuin activator studied for cardiovascular and anti-aging benefits.
evidence_notes: Sirtuin activator; mixed human evidence

dosage.special_populations: Longevity, cardiovascular focus

name: Rhodiola
summary: Adaptogen that helps the body adapt to stress, supporting HPA axis and adrenal function.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for stress adaptation and fatigue

dosage.special_populations: Stress-related fatigue, burnout, adrenal support

upper_limit.label: Avoid with bipolar (activation)
upper_limit.notes: Take in morning, may be stimulating

name: Rhodiola crenulata Extract (Root)
summary: Rhodiola crenulata is an alpine adaptogenic herb native to the Himalayas, standardized for salidroside content, traditionally used to support stress resilience and mental performance. Research is limited compared to Rhodiola rosea, with preliminary evidence suggesting potential benefits for fatigue and cognitive function.
mechanism_of_action: Salidroside and rosiridin bioactive compounds may enhance mitochondrial function
evidence_notes: Rhodiola crenulata has substantially less clinical research than Rhodiola rosea. Available studies are small, preliminary, or conducted in non-English journals. Salidroside standardization is rational but human efficacy data is limited. Grade C reflects moderate/preliminary evidence with promising signals but insufficient RCT evidence.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Adaptogenic benefits typically emerge after 2-4 weeks of consistent use; some studies used durations of 4-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Limited safety data in pregnancy; avoid in individuals with bipolar disorder or those taking mood-stabilizing medications

common_side_effects: Rare: mild headache, dry mouth, insomnia (if taken too late in day)

medications: MAOI inhibitors (theoretical risk due to monoamine activity)
supplements: Other adaptogens or stimulating nootropics (potential additive effects)

name: Rhodiola (for Endurance)
summary: Adaptogen reducing perceived exertion and enhancing endurance performance.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for endurance and anti-fatigue

dosage.special_populations: Endurance athletes, high training load

name: Rhodiola rosea
