summary: Rhodiola rosea is a traditional adaptogenic herb used to support stress resilience, mental fatigue, and mood. Clinical evidence shows modest benefits for stress-related fatigue and cognitive performance, though effects are generally moderate in magnitude.
evidence_notes: Well-studied adaptogen for mental performance

dosage.special_populations: High stress, mental fatigue

upper_limit.label: Avoid with bipolar (activation)
upper_limit.notes: Take in morning, may be stimulating

name: Rhodiola rosea Extract (root) - Rosavins + Salidroside
summary: Rhodiola rosea is a well-researched Arctic adaptogenic herb standardized to contain both rosavins and salidroside, the two primary active compound classes supporting stress resilience and mental performance. This dual-marker formulation provides comprehensive active constituent coverage.
mechanism_of_action: Modulation of HPA axis and cortisol regulation
Cellular energy production and mitochondrial function
evidence_notes: Solid RCT evidence from multiple studies demonstrating efficacy for stress, fatigue, and cognitive function. Standardized to both rosavins (3%) and salidroside (1%), the two major bioactive classes identified in clinical research.

dosage.timing: Morning to early afternoon; avoid evening to prevent insomnia
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; consistent timing enhances benefits
dosage.duration_notes: Effects often observed within 1-2 weeks; safe for long-term use (6+ months). Adaptogenic effects compound over time.
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; caution in pregnancy/lactation and bipolar disorder

medications: Diabetes medications (may enhance hypoglycemic effect; monitor)
Blood pressure medications (possible additive effect; monitor)
supplements: Stimulant herbs (caffeine, guarana) — may increase jitteriness

name: Rhodiola rosea Extract (root) - Salidroside
summary: Rhodiola rosea standardized to salidroside alone is a single-marker extract of this well-researched adaptogenic herb, providing one of the two major bioactive compound classes for stress resilience and cognitive support. This formulation emphasizes one of the key active constituents.
mechanism_of_action: Modulation of HPA axis and stress hormone regulation
evidence_notes: Solid RCT evidence for R. rosea; however, this single-marker (3% salidroside only) formulation lacks rosavins, which are also identified as important bioactive compounds in clinical research. Salidroside alone supports efficacy but a dual-standardized form may offer more comprehensive benefits.

dosage.timing: Morning to early afternoon; avoid evening dosing
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; consistent timing recommended
dosage.duration_notes: Effects typically observed within 1-2 weeks; appropriate for long-term use. Adaptogenic benefits compound with regular use.
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; use caution in pregnancy/lactation and bipolar disorder

medications: Diabetes medications (may potentiate; monitor blood glucose)
Antihypertensive agents (possible additive blood pressure effect)

name: Riboflavin (Vitamin B2)
summary: Riboflavin is an essential B vitamin that functions as a coenzyme in energy metabolism, antioxidant defense, and cellular respiration. Well-established supplementation supports normal energy production and may help prevent deficiency-related conditions.
mechanism_of_action: Converted to FAD and FMN, essential cofactors for oxidoreductase enzymes
Participates in amino acid and fatty acid oxidation
Contributes to glutathione reductase activation in antioxidant systems
evidence_notes: Riboflavin is an essential micronutrient with well-established biochemical roles and strong RCT evidence for migraine prophylaxis at higher doses. Decades of research confirm its necessity for human health and metabolic function.

dosage.typical_range: 1.1–1.3 mg daily (RDA); 200–400 mg daily for migraine prevention
dosage.timing: Any time of day; may be taken with meals for better tolerance
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; water-soluble vitamin
dosage.duration_notes: Migraine prevention studies typically used 8–12 weeks duration; daily intake for ongoing support
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/lactating women: 1.4–1.6 mg/day; higher doses generally well-tolerated across populations

common_side_effects: Yellow discoloration of urine (harmless, due to excess water-soluble vitamin excretion)
Rare: mild gastrointestinal upset at very high doses
contraindications: No significant contraindications; water-soluble excess is rapidly excreted
Caution in patients with known riboflavin sensitivity (rare)

medications: Phenothiazines, tricyclic antidepressants: may increase riboflavin requirements
supplements: No major supplement interactions; combines safely with other B vitamins in B-complex formulas

name: Rice Bran
summary: The outer layer of rice grain, rich in fiber, B vitamins, vitamin E (tocotrienols), and antioxidants. Supports cardiovascular and digestive health.
mechanism_of_action: Provides soluble and insoluble fiber for digestive health
Contains tocotrienols (vitamin E forms) with antioxidant activity
Phytosterols may help reduce cholesterol absorption
evidence_notes: Good evidence for fiber benefits and cholesterol effects; tocotrienol research is promising.

forms.description: Processed to extend shelf life while preserving nutrients

dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for most adults

common_side_effects: Initial gas/bloating if increasing fiber quickly

medications: May affect absorption of some medications if taken together

name: Rice Protein
summary: Plant-based protein from brown rice. Hypoallergenic and pairs well with pea protein for complete amino acid profile.
evidence_notes: Good plant protein option, comparable to whey when combined with pea

dosage.special_populations: Vegans, allergen-sensitive

name: Rose Hips Powder (Rosa canina)
summary: Rose hips are the fruit of the Rosa canina plant, rich in vitamin C, polyphenols, and carotenoids, traditionally used for immune support and anti-inflammatory benefits. The powder form provides concentrated nutrients from the dried fruit.
mechanism_of_action: High vitamin C content supports immune function
evidence_notes: Good evidence for immune support due to high vitamin C content and antioxidant compounds. Moderate evidence for joint health and inflammation reduction in several clinical trials.

dosage.with_food: With food to enhance absorption and reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used long-term; effects on immune and joint health may develop over weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: consult healthcare provider; those with kidney stones should monitor oxalate intake

medications: Warfarin (high vitamin K content may interact)
supplements: None significant; complements other immune-supporting compounds

name: Organic Rosehip Extract (Rosa canina)
summary: Rosehip is a fruit extract rich in vitamin C, polyphenols, and carotenoids with preliminary evidence supporting joint health and skin barrier function. Most human studies show modest effects on joint discomfort and skin appearance, though evidence quality remains moderate.
mechanism_of_action: High vitamin C content supports collagen synthesis
Polyphenols (gallic acid, catechin) provide antioxidant effects
Anti-inflammatory compounds may reduce joint inflammation
evidence_notes: Multiple human RCTs on joint health (primarily osteoarthritis) show modest positive effects; several controlled studies on skin health outcomes. Grade B reflects consistent but moderate-magnitude effects in good-quality studies, though long-term data is limited.

dosage.typical_range: 500–1500 mg daily (equivalent to 5–10 g dried fruit)
dosage.timing: With meals to improve absorption and minimize GI upset
dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble compounds and vitamin C absorption optimized with food
dosage.duration_notes: Joint health studies typically run 8–12 weeks; skin effects may require 4–8 weeks; long-term safety data beyond 6 months is limited
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy/lactation due to vitamin C and nutrient profile, but medical consultation recommended; may interact with certain medications (see interactions)

common_side_effects: Kidney stone risk in susceptible individuals (high vitamin C content)
contraindications: Individuals with kidney disease or history of kidney stones (due to oxalate and vitamin C content)
Potential interactions with anticoagulants and antiplatelet medications
Avoid in patients with iron metabolism disorders (vitamin C increases iron absorption)

medications: Warfarin and anticoagulants (vitamin K and polyphenols may have minor effects)
Aspirin and NSAIDs (additive anti-inflammatory effects; monitor for bleeding risk)
Iron supplements (vitamin C increases absorption; may require dose adjustment)
supplements: Other high-dose vitamin C sources (increased risk of kidney stones)
Iron supplements (enhanced absorption; monitor serum iron levels)

name: Rosemary Extract
summary: Rosemary extract is a polyphenol-rich herbal ingredient containing carnosic acid and carnosol, traditionally used for cognitive and antioxidant support. Preliminary evidence suggests potential benefits for memory and neuroprotection, though human clinical data remains limited.
evidence_notes: Aromatic cognitive enhancer

dosage.special_populations: Memory, antioxidant

name: Royal Jelly
summary: Royal jelly is a nutrient-dense secretion produced by honeybees containing proteins, amino acids, vitamins, and bioactive compounds. Limited human clinical evidence supports claims of energy, immune, or anti-aging benefits, with most studies being small or conducted in animal models.
evidence_notes: Traditional use, some research on various benefits

dosage.special_populations: Vitality, skin, energy

name: Royal Sun Blazei (Agaricus brasiliensis f. blazei)
summary: Royal Sun Blazei is a Brazilian medicinal mushroom rich in beta-glucans, traditionally used in Japanese and Brazilian folk medicine for immune support. Human evidence is very limited; studies are mostly small or preliminary.
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical data. Most evidence derives from in vitro and animal studies. A few small human studies exist but lack rigorous design. Requires further investigation.

dosage.duration_notes: Limited data on optimal duration; generally considered safe for chronic use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women and immunocompromised patients should consult healthcare provider

contraindications: autoimmune disease (immune stimulation may be problematic)

name: Rutaecarpine
summary: Rutaecarpine is an alkaloid compound derived from Evodia rutaecarpa (Chinese herb) traditionally used in herbal medicine. Emerging research suggests potential thermogenic and metabolic effects, though human clinical evidence remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Traditional use for circulation and warming
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical data. Most evidence is in vitro or animal-based. Insufficient RCTs for sleep efficacy in humans. Marketed as thermogenic/circulation support but sleep-specific evidence is weak.

dosage.with_food: can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: typical use in sleep blends is short-term (acute sleep support)
dosage.special_populations: Insufficient data for pregnancy/nursing; use with caution in cardiovascular conditions

name: S-Acetyl L-Glutathione
summary: S-Acetyl L-Glutathione is a stabilized form of the tripeptide glutathione designed to improve bioavailability and reduce oxidative stress. Limited human clinical evidence supports efficacy, though animal and in vitro studies suggest potential cellular protective effects.
mechanism_of_action: Support for glutathione peroxidase and other antioxidant enzymes
Improved oral bioavailability compared to unacetylated glutathione
evidence_notes: Acetylation improves stability and absorption compared to standard glutathione, but human clinical trials directly measuring physiological outcomes remain limited. Most evidence comes from in vitro and animal studies. Bioavailability claims are promising but not definitively established in humans.

dosage.timing: With or without food; typically once or twice daily
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may improve absorption with a light meal
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety data in humans is limited; typical use spans weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, nursing, and children not well-established; consult healthcare provider

contraindications: Hypersensitivity to glutathione or acetylated compounds
Caution in individuals on immunosuppressive therapy (glutathione may enhance immune function)

medications: Potential interaction with chemotherapy agents (glutathione may reduce efficacy in some protocols)
May interact with antiretroviral therapy—consult healthcare provider
supplements: May have additive effects with other antioxidants (vitamin C, vitamin E, alpha-lipoic acid)
N-acetyl cysteine (NAC) may be synergistic as a glutathione precursor

name: S-Phenylacetyl L-Glutathione
summary: S-Phenylacetyl L-Glutathione is a modified form of glutathione designed for improved bioavailability and potential blood-brain barrier penetration. Limited human evidence exists; most data is from cell culture and animal studies.
mechanism_of_action: Delivers glutathione or its precursors across the blood-brain barrier
Potential antioxidant and free radical scavenging activity
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical data. Mechanism is theoretically sound but empirical evidence in humans is sparse. Most supporting evidence comes from in vitro studies and animal models. No large RCTs in human populations.

dosage.timing: No established optimal timing; morning dosing suggested
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety and efficacy duration unclear due to limited research
dosage.special_populations: Insufficient data for pregnant/nursing women, children, or those with severe liver disease

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated; specific adverse event data limited
contraindications: Caution in individuals with severe liver or kidney impairment
Insufficient safety data in pregnancy and lactation

medications: Potential interactions with chemotherapy agents (theoretical, based on glutathione's antioxidant role)
supplements: May have additive effects with other antioxidant supplements

name: Sabroxy (Oroxylum indicum) Extract
summary: Sabroxy is a standardized extract from Oroxylum indicum bark containing oroxylin A, a flavonoid compound studied for potential neuroprotective and cognitive effects. Research is limited primarily to in vitro and animal models, with minimal human clinical data available.
evidence_notes: Evidence is primarily limited to in vitro studies and animal models. Oroxylin A shows promise in preclinical research but lacks adequate human clinical trials to establish efficacy or optimal dosing. No robust RCT data in human subjects currently available.

dosage.typical_range: 300-600 mg daily (based on traditional use and early studies)
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; may be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Optimal duration of use not established; human studies lacking
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnant women, nursing mothers, and children not established; use with caution

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated in limited human exposure
Insufficient data for comprehensive side effect profile
contraindications: Known hypersensitivity to Acanthaceae family plants

medications: Potential interaction with GABAergic drugs (benzodiazepines, barbiturates) — theoretical concern based on proposed mechanism; clinical significance unknown
supplements: Other GABAergic supplements (L-theanine, GABA, valerian) — potential additive effects; clinical data lacking

name: Saccharomyces boulardii
summary: Beneficial yeast probiotic that's antibiotic-resistant. Excellent for diarrhea, C. diff, and antibiotic support.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for diarrhea prevention

dosage.special_populations: Antibiotic users, recurrent C. diff

serious_warnings: Fungemia in immunocompromised with central lines

citations.title: S. boulardii for diarrhea prevention
citations.finding: Significantly reduces antibiotic-associated and traveler's diarrhea

citations.title: S. boulardii systematic review
citations.finding: Effective for C. difficile infection prevention

name: Safflower Oil
summary: Safflower oil is a plant-based oil rich in polyunsaturated fatty acids, commonly used as a carrier for conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) in supplement formulations. Evidence for independent health benefits beyond its fatty acid content remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Provides omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acids (primarily linoleic acid)
Used as delivery vehicle for fat-soluble compounds like CLA
evidence_notes: Safflower oil itself has limited independent research. Some studies suggest modest effects on inflammation or lipid metabolism, but evidence is preliminary and inconsistent. Grade reflects that it is primarily used as a carrier oil rather than an active ingredient with established benefits.

dosage.typical_range: 1,000–3,000 mg daily (as part of CLA or multi-ingredient formulas)
dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble and better absorbed with dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used long-term; no established maximum duration
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy and lactation in typical dietary amounts; those with seed allergies should use caution

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal upset (when used in high doses)
contraindications: History of allergic reaction to Asteraceae family plants

medications: Blood thinners (warfarin, aspirin) — theoretical interaction due to omega-6 content; monitor if using concurrently
supplements: Other high-dose omega-6 sources — may shift omega-6:omega-3 balance if combined with minimal omega-3 intake

name: Safflower Seed Oil Concentrate
summary: Safflower seed oil concentrate is used as a carrier and source of conjugated linoleic acid (CLA), a polyunsaturated fatty acid with preliminary evidence for body composition support. The concentrate typically provides 750-1250 mg of CLA per serving in supplement formulations.
mechanism_of_action: CLA may modulate fat metabolism and promote fat oxidation
Potential effects on adipose tissue regulation
Serves as carrier oil for CLA bioavailability
evidence_notes: CLA itself has moderate evidence from mixed clinical trials showing modest body composition benefits (typically 0.5-1.5 kg fat loss over 12 weeks). However, safflower oil as a delivery vehicle lacks robust direct clinical data; efficacy depends on CLA content and quality standardization.

dosage.typical_range: 750-1500 mg CLA daily (from safflower oil concentrate)
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent use for 12+ weeks typically needed to observe potential effects
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/lactation: limited safety data; consult healthcare provider

contraindications: Bleeding disorders (theoretical risk from increased polyunsaturated fat intake)

supplements: Other omega fatty acid supplements (additive polyunsaturated fat intake)

name: Saffron Extract
summary: A standardized saffron stigma extract containing 7.5% crocins and 1.0% safranals, the primary active compounds associated with mood, vision, and antioxidant support. One of the most expensive spices with a growing clinical research base.
evidence_notes: Well-studied for depression

forms.form: Stigma extract, standardized to 7.5% crocins and 1.0% safranals

dosage.special_populations: Mild depression, PMS

name: Sage Extract
summary: Herb improving memory and attention, particularly studied in elderly. Inhibits acetylcholinesterase.
evidence_notes: Memory-enhancing herb for aging

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive aging, memory

common_side_effects: Thujone content concern at high doses
serious_warnings: Thujone toxicity at very high doses

name: Salidroside
summary: Salidroside is an active phenolic compound found in Rhodiola rosea that exhibits antioxidant and neuroprotective properties. Limited human evidence supports traditional use for stress adaptation and mental fatigue, though most research is conducted in vitro or in animal models.
evidence_notes: Salidroside shows promise in cell and animal studies for neuroprotection and stress resilience, but human clinical data remains limited. Efficacy likely depends on Rhodiola rosea whole-plant synergy rather than isolated compound.

dosage.typical_range: 50-300 mg daily (as isolated compound or via Rhodiola extract)
dosage.timing: Morning to early afternoon preferred for adaptogenic effect
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Adaptogenic effects typically require 2-4 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Limited safety data in pregnancy; caution in bipolar disorder due to potential mood effects

common_side_effects: Minimal reported; rare insomnia or restlessness at high doses

medications: Potential interaction with monoamine oxidase inhibitors (theoretical)
supplements: Caution with other adaptogenic herbs; additive stimulant effects possible

name: Salmon Oil
summary: Salmon oil is a marine source of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, primarily EPA and DHA, which have well-established cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. Multiple RCTs support its use for triglyceride reduction and heart health maintenance.
mechanism_of_action: EPA and DHA reduce inflammatory markers and triglycerides
Modulates platelet aggregation and endothelial function
Supports neuronal membrane composition and synaptic plasticity
evidence_notes: Strong RCT evidence for cardiovascular benefits, particularly triglyceride reduction and blood pressure support. Well-documented safety and efficacy profile across multiple large-scale studies.

dosage.timing: With meals to enhance absorption and reduce gastrointestinal upset
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use; benefits typically observed within 4-12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; those on anticoagulants should monitor with their physician

contraindications: Bleeding disorders (high doses may increase bleeding risk)
Concurrent use with anticoagulant medications without medical supervision

medications: Antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel) — may increase bleeding risk at high doses
NSAIDs — combined use may increase gastrointestinal risk
supplements: Other omega-3 supplements (risk of excessive omega-3 intake)

name: Salvia officinalis Extract (Sage Leaf)
summary: Salvia officinalis (common sage) leaf extract standardized to rosmarinic acid has been traditionally used to support cognitive function and memory, with emerging evidence suggesting antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties relevant to neuroprotection.
mechanism_of_action: Rosmarinic acid provides antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects
May inhibit acetylcholinesterase, supporting cholinergic signaling
evidence_notes: Sage extract has moderate traditional use and some clinical studies support cognitive benefits, but high-quality RCT evidence in humans remains limited. Rosmarinic acid standardization is favorable for consistency, but the body of evidence is not yet robust enough for grade B.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; rosmarinic acid absorption may be improved with dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; benefits may develop over 4-8 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; limited safety data in pregnancy and lactation—use with caution or avoid

common_side_effects: Rare; generally well-tolerated at recommended doses
Possible mild gastrointestinal effects or headache in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Individuals with seizure disorders (sage may have proconvulsant properties at very high doses)
Those with thujone sensitivity (traditional sage contains low levels of thujone)

medications: May have additive effects with acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., donepezil)
Potential interaction with drugs metabolized by CYP3A4 (in vitro evidence; clinical relevance unclear)
supplements: Additive antioxidant effects with other antioxidant supplements (generally beneficial but monitor for excess)

name: Salvia officinalis Extract (Sage Leaf) - 2.5% Rosmarinic Acid
summary: Salvia officinalis (garden sage) is a traditional Mediterranean herb containing rosmarinic acid and other polyphenols with demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Limited but emerging evidence suggests potential cognitive and memory-supporting effects, though human clinical data remains modest.
evidence_notes: Human cognitive studies are limited and small; most evidence derives from in vitro and animal models. A few clinical trials show modest benefits for memory and attention, but sample sizes are small and designs vary. Rosmarinic acid content is well-characterized chemically, but clinical efficacy requires more robust RCT evidence.

dosage.timing: With meals for better absorption and reduced GI irritation
dosage.with_food: Recommended to enhance absorption of lipophilic compounds
dosage.duration_notes: Effects may take 2-4 weeks of consistent use; traditional use spans months
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider before use; may interact with certain medications

contraindications: Seizure disorders (theoretical risk; high doses traditionally contraindicated)

medications: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., donepezil) — potential additive effects
supplements: Other cholinergic or neuroprotective herbs (e.g., ginkgo, bacopa) — additive effects possible

name: SAM-e
summary: Universal methyl donor supporting mood, joints, and liver. Comparable to antidepressants.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for depression

dosage.special_populations: Depression, joints, liver

citations.title: SAM-e for depression meta-analysis
citations.finding: SAM-e effective for depression, comparable to some antidepressants

citations.title: SAM-e mechanisms
citations.finding: SAM-e supports methylation and neurotransmitter synthesis

name: Sambucus nigra (Elder) Extract
summary: Elderberry extract from Sambucus nigra contains anthocyanins and other polyphenols with traditional use for immune and respiratory support. Modern research shows moderate evidence for reducing cold/flu duration and severity.
mechanism_of_action: May inhibit viral replication and reduce inflammatory response
Stimulates cytokine production for innate immunity
evidence_notes: Good evidence from multiple RCTs showing elderberry reduces cold/flu duration and symptom severity when taken prophylactically or within 48 hours of symptom onset. Standardization to anthocyanins strengthens product quality.

dosage.typical_range: 300–1,000 mg daily (equivalent to 3,200–8,000 mg fresh berries standardized to anthocyanins)
dosage.timing: With meals for better absorption and tolerance
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for short-term use (acute illness) and preventive use during cold/flu season (up to 3 months). May taper once viral season passes.
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in autoimmune conditions (may over-stimulate immunity); pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; pediatric dosing should be reduced (50–75% of adult dose)

contraindications: Autoimmune diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, lupus) — may exacerbate
Scheduled surgery (may interfere with immune management)

medications: Diabetes medications (some compounds may affect glucose; monitor blood sugar)
Diuretics (elderberry may have mild diuretic effect)
supplements: Other immune stimulants (echinacea, andrographis) — additive effect; can be beneficial but monitor

name: Sarcosine
summary: Sarcosine is an endogenous amino acid and glycine metabolite that modulates NMDA receptor function and may have potential benefits for cognitive function and mood. Limited human clinical evidence supports its use as a nootropic agent.
mechanism_of_action: Antagonizes glycine transporter-1 (GlyT1), increasing glycine availability at NMDA receptors
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials, primarily small studies and preliminary research. Most evidence derives from mechanistic and animal studies. Human efficacy data for cognitive benefits remain preliminary.

dosage.timing: Divided doses, typically once or twice daily
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety data limited; typical studies use 4-12 week durations
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should avoid; limited safety data in pediatric populations

contraindications: Schizophrenia or psychotic disorders (potential risk of exacerbation)

supplements: D-serine (similar mechanism; concurrent use not well-studied)

name: Sarsaparilla Root Extract
summary: Sarsaparilla root has a long history in traditional medicine for joint and skin health, though modern clinical evidence is limited. Current research does not strongly support efficacy claims in humans.
mechanism_of_action: Contains saponins and other phytochemicals that may have anti-inflammatory properties
evidence_notes: Very limited clinical evidence in humans. Most supporting data comes from traditional use and in vitro/animal studies. No large-scale RCTs in contemporary populations.

dosage.typical_range: 1-2 grams daily (as powder or equivalent extract)
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: No standard duration established; traditional use suggests long-term consumption
dosage.special_populations: Not recommended for pregnant/nursing women; limited safety data in children

contraindications: Those with known allergy to Smilax species

medications: Possible interaction with blood thinners (insufficient evidence)
supplements: Limited known interactions; use caution with other herbal preparations

name: Saturated Fat
summary: Saturated fat is a macronutrient that provides 9 calories per gram and is found naturally in animal products and some plant oils. Its role in health is complex and depends on context, overall diet composition, and individual metabolic factors.
mechanism_of_action: provides dietary energy via ATP production
evidence_notes: Well-established macronutrient with extensive research showing mixed health effects depending on overall dietary pattern, food source, and individual factors. Not a supplement but a nutritional component.

dosage.typical_range: Dietary intake; typically 20-35g daily depending on caloric needs
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with cardiovascular concerns may benefit from limiting intake; discuss with healthcare provider

name: Saw Palmetto
summary: Saw palmetto extract is derived from the fruit of Serenoa repens and has been studied primarily for prostate and urinary health support. Evidence suggests modest benefits for benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH) symptoms, though results are mixed and effects may be modest compared to pharmaceutical alternatives.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for BPH symptom improvement

dosage.special_populations: Men with BPH, urinary symptoms

practical_notes: Similar mechanism to finasteride but weaker
More studied for prostate than PCOS

name: Schisandra
summary: Five-flavor berry adaptogen. Supports liver, stress resilience, and endurance.
evidence_notes: Good adaptogenic evidence

dosage.special_populations: Liver support + stress

name: Schisandra chinensis Extract (Seed)
summary: Schisandra chinensis is a traditional Chinese adaptogenic berry standardized for schisandrin content, used to support stress resilience, mental clarity, and physical endurance. Limited but promising clinical evidence suggests benefits for cognitive performance and stress-related fatigue, though larger human studies are needed.
mechanism_of_action: Antioxidant and hepatoprotective activity via schisandrin lignans
Potential dopamine and serotonin system modulation
evidence_notes: Schisandra has centuries of traditional use and preliminary human studies support cognitive and stress-related benefits, but rigorous RCTs in Western populations remain limited. Standardization to 3% schisandrins is evidence-informed but not universally established. Grade reflects moderate preliminary evidence without large-scale RCT validation.

dosage.timing: Morning or split morning/afternoon to avoid evening stimulation
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; consistent timing recommended
dosage.duration_notes: Adaptogens often benefit from consistent use over 4–12 weeks; effects may build over time
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should avoid; caution in patients with seizure disorders or on sedating medications

common_side_effects: Mild stimulant effects (insomnia if taken late in day)

medications: CYP3A4 and CYP2D6 substrates (potential mild inhibition; monitor)
supplements: Other adaptogens or stimulating nootropics (additive tone)

name: SDA (Stearidonic Acid)
summary: A plant-based omega-3 that converts to EPA 3-5x more efficiently than ALA. Found in ahiflower and echium oils, making it the most efficient plant omega-3 for EPA production.
evidence_notes: Emerging research shows efficient conversion to EPA
mechanism_of_action: Converts to EPA at 3-5x efficiency of ALA

dosage.special_populations: Vegans seeking meaningful EPA levels

serious_warnings: Same bleeding considerations as other omega-3s

name: Sea Buckthorn
summary: Nutrient-rich berry and seed oil from hardy shrub. Contains rare omega-7 plus omegas 3, 6, 9. Supports skin, mucous membranes, and cardiovascular health.
evidence_notes: Traditional use with research on skin and mucous membranes

dosage.special_populations: Dry eyes, vaginal dryness, skin health

practical_notes: Different benefits from seed vs berry oil

name: Sea Moss Powder (Chondrus crispus)
summary: Sea moss is a nutrient-dense red algae containing minerals (iodine, potassium, calcium), polysaccharides, and carrageenan. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most claims are based on traditional use and in vitro studies.
mechanism_of_action: Provides bioavailable minerals (iodine, potassium, magnesium, calcium)
Carrageenan may have demulcent properties in GI tract
evidence_notes: Limited human RCT data. Traditional use is well-established, and mineral composition is well-documented. However, specific bioavailability, efficacy for claimed uses, and safety in supplement form require more rigorous clinical evidence.

dosage.timing: Can be taken anytime; often added to smoothies or taken with meals
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; may be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used long-term as a nutritional supplement; no established duration limits
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult provider (iodine levels); those with iodine sensitivity or thyroid conditions requiring iodine restriction should use cautiously

common_side_effects: Potential GI effects (digestive upset, bloating) if sensitive to polysaccharides
Iodine-related effects in excessive amounts (rare at typical doses)
contraindications: Hyperthyroidism or conditions requiring iodine restriction (use cautiously)
Heavy metal contamination in some batches; sourcing matters

medications: Thyroid medications: High iodine content may interfere with levothyroxine and other thyroid drugs; monitor TSH if concurrent use
supplements: Other iodine sources: Risk of iodine overload if combined with kelp, iodized salt, or other iodine supplements

name: Selank
summary: Russian anxiolytic peptide related to Semax. Reduces anxiety while enhancing cognition.
evidence_notes: Anxiolytic nootropic peptide

dosage.special_populations: Anxiety, stress

name: Selenium
summary: Essential trace mineral for glutathione peroxidase activity, thyroid function, and optimal immune response to viral infections.
evidence_notes: Well-established for thyroid function and protection

dosage.special_populations: Hashimoto's, hypothyroid, those on iodine

name: Selenium (as Fermented Selenium Glycinate)
summary: Selenium is an essential trace mineral incorporated into selenoproteins that provide antioxidant defense and thyroid hormone metabolism. Glycinate chelation may improve absorption compared to inorganic forms.
mechanism_of_action: Component of glutathione peroxidase and thioredoxin reductase
evidence_notes: Strong RCT evidence supports selenium's role in antioxidant defense and thyroid health. Organic/chelated forms show improved bioavailability in comparison studies.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Ongoing supplementation recommended; upper limit generally 400 mcg/day
dosage.special_populations: RDA: 55 mcg (adults), 60 mcg (pregnant), 70 mcg (lactating), 20-40 mcg (children). Upper limit: 400 mcg/day to avoid selenosis.

contraindications: Excessive iodine intake combined with high selenium (may worsen thyroid dysfunction)

supplements: High-dose vitamin C (may reduce selenium availability)

name: Selenium (as High Selenium Yeast)
summary: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that acts as a cofactor for selenoproteins, including glutathione peroxidase, a powerful antioxidant enzyme. High selenium yeast is an organic form containing selenium incorporated into yeast proteins.
mechanism_of_action: cofactor for glutathione peroxidase and other selenoproteins
evidence_notes: Selenium has good evidence for antioxidant and thyroid support. Selenized yeast provides selenium in a bioavailable organic form, showing favorable absorption and retention compared to inorganic selenite.

dosage.with_food: can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: ongoing for general health; avoid exceeding 400 mcg daily
dosage.special_populations: pregnant/nursing women: 60-70 mcg daily; those with autoimmune thyroid disease should consult provider

common_side_effects: garlic odor on breath (at high doses)
contraindications: do not exceed 400 mcg daily long-term; Hashimoto's thyroiditis (may require caution with high doses)

medications: may interact with thyroid medications at very high doses
supplements: no major interactions at recommended doses

name: Selenium (Selenomethionine)
summary: Highly bioavailable organic selenium for thyroid and antioxidant function. Narrow optimal range requires careful dosing.
evidence_notes: Well-established for thyroid and antioxidant
mechanism_of_action: Essential for T4 to T3 conversion

dosage.special_populations: Thyroid patients

citations.title: Selenium and thyroid function
citations.finding: Essential for thyroid hormone metabolism

citations.title: Selenium and immune function
citations.finding: Deficiency impairs immune function; supplementation beneficial in deficient individuals

name: Selenium (as Sodium Selenite)
summary: Selenium (as sodium selenite) is an inorganic form of selenium with moderate bioavailability, essential for glutathione peroxidase and thyroid hormone metabolism. Selenomethionine (organic form) is better absorbed.
mechanism_of_action: component of selenoproteins (glutathione peroxidase, thioredoxin reductase)
evidence_notes: Selenium has good evidence for thyroid and antioxidant support; inorganic selenite form has lower bioavailability than organic selenomethionine.

dosage.timing: can be taken anytime; consistent daily intake recommended
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe long-term; avoid exceeding UL of 400 µg daily to prevent selenosis
dosage.special_populations: Brazil nuts naturally very high in selenium; adjust supplemental intake accordingly

name: Semax
summary: Russian peptide nootropic with neuroprotective and cognitive benefits. Requires nasal administration.
evidence_notes: Well-studied in Russia

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive enhancement

name: Senna
summary: Stimulant laxative for occasional constipation, works by stimulating intestinal contractions.
evidence_notes: Well-established stimulant laxative

dosage.typical_range: 15-30mg sennosides or 1 cup tea at bedtime
dosage.special_populations: Occasional constipation

medications: May reduce absorption of some drugs

name: Sensoril® Ashwagandha (Withania somnifera) Extract
summary: Sensoril® is a standardized ashwagandha extract derived from root and leaf that contains bioactive withanolides, compounds studied for stress reduction and cognitive support. Multiple randomized controlled trials support its efficacy for reducing cortisol and anxiety symptoms.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence from multiple RCTs demonstrating efficacy for stress, anxiety, and cortisol reduction. Sensoril® specifically has published clinical trials. Grade B rather than A due to variability in study quality and effect sizes, though human evidence is substantial.

dosage.timing: With meals; often divided into 2-3 doses
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent use for 4-8 weeks typically needed for optimal effects; benefits may continue with long-term use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; may interact with sedating medications

contraindications: Pregnancy and lactation (traditionally used in some cultures but consult provider)
Thyroid disorders (may have mild thyroid effects)

name: Serrapeptase
summary: Proteolytic enzyme originally isolated from silkworm gut bacteria. Used as an anti-inflammatory and mucolytic; most evidence is in ENT and post-surgical settings.
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence in ENT (sinusitis, post-tonsillectomy edema) and respiratory clearance. Broader anti-inflammatory claims rest on small older studies; recent systematic reviews are mixed. Must be enteric-coated to survive stomach acid.
mechanism_of_action: Hydrolyzes bradykinin and other inflammatory mediators
Breaks down non-vital (dead or fibrotic) tissue and fibrin deposits
Reduces viscosity of bronchial and nasal secretions

forms.notes: Standard — non-enteric forms are degraded in stomach acid

dosage.typical_range: 10,000-60,000 SPU per day, divided into 1-3 doses
dosage.timing: On an empty stomach — at least 30 minutes before food or 2 hours after
dosage.with_food: Strictly without food — food reduces absorption of systemic enzymes
dosage.duration_notes: Short courses (2-4 weeks) most studied; no clear safety signal at longer durations but evidence is limited
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in pregnancy, active bleeding, or surgery within 2 weeks

serious_warnings: Possible mild fibrinolytic activity — increased bleeding risk with anticoagulants
Rare reports of pneumonitis at high doses
contraindications: Concurrent anticoagulant therapy without clinician oversight
safety.liver_kidney_notes: No significant concerns at typical doses

medications: Warfarin, heparin, DOACs — theoretical additive bleeding risk
supplements: High-dose fish oil, garlic, ginkgo — additive bleeding risk
food_alcohol: Take on empty stomach — food blunts absorption

avoid_combining_with: Other systemic fibrinolytics without clinician supervision

practical_notes: Enteric coating is non-negotiable — uncoated product is digested before it can be absorbed
Take well away from meals for any systemic effect

name: SBI (Serum-Derived Bovine Immunoglobulins)
summary: Concentrated immunoglobulins that bind pathogens and toxins in the gut, supporting barrier function.
evidence_notes: Growing clinical evidence for gut conditions

dosage.special_populations: IBS-D, enteropathy, HIV enteropathy, difficult GI cases

contraindications: Severe milk allergy (though different from milk proteins)

name: Sesanum indicum Extract (Sesame Seed) - 70% Sesamins
summary: Sesame seed extract standardized to 70% sesamins is a lignan-rich antioxidant derived from sesame seeds with preliminary evidence for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Human clinical research remains limited, with most supportive data from in vitro and animal studies.
mechanism_of_action: Lignan-based antioxidant activity via free radical scavenging
evidence_notes: Limited human RCT evidence; most studies are in vitro or animal models. A few small human studies show modest benefits for antioxidant status and lipid profiles, but robust clinical efficacy data is lacking. Standardization to sesamins is appropriate, but clinical outcomes require additional investigation.

dosage.duration_notes: No established minimum duration; effects may take 4-8 weeks to manifest
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; limited safety data in pediatric populations

contraindications: Concurrent use with immunosuppressants (potential sesame immunogenicity)

medications: CYP3A4/CYP2C9 substrates (sesamins may alter drug metabolism)
Antihypertensive agents (additive blood pressure effects possible)
supplements: Other antioxidants (potential synergy but not well-studied)

name: Sesamum indicum Extract (Sesame Seed Extract, 70% Sesamins)
summary: Sesame seed extract standardized to 70% sesamins is a plant lignan concentrate with preliminary evidence for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Most human evidence remains limited, with potential benefits primarily demonstrated in animal models and small clinical studies.
mechanism_of_action: Potent antioxidant activity via free radical scavenging
Potential enhancement of antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD, catalase)
evidence_notes: Sesamins have substantial in vitro and animal model evidence for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Human clinical trials are limited in number and sample size; most available studies involve small cohorts or focus on sesame oil rather than standardized lignan extracts. Grade C reflects preliminary but promising evidence lacking robust large-scale RCT confirmation.

dosage.duration_notes: Typically used continuously; effects may require 4-8 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; limited safety data in these populations

common_side_effects: Rare: allergic reactions in sesame-sensitive individuals
contraindications: Use with caution in patients with bleeding disorders (theoretical risk based on animal data)

medications: Potential interaction with anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents (theoretical; limited human data)
supplements: Additive effects possible with other antioxidant supplements
Potential synergy with vitamin E or other lipophilic antioxidants

name: Sulforaphane Glucosinolate (SGS)
summary: Stable sulforaphane precursor from broccoli seeds. Powerful Nrf2 activator for detoxification and cellular protection.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for Nrf2 activation and detox support

dosage.special_populations: Detox support

serious_warnings: May affect thyroid at very high doses

name: Shark Cartilage
summary: Shark cartilage is a source of glycosaminoglycans (GAGs) and collagen promoted for joint and connective tissue support, though clinical evidence for efficacy remains limited and inconsistent.
mechanism_of_action: Provides glycosaminoglycans (chondroitin sulfate, keratan sulfate) and collagen peptides
evidence_notes: Limited clinical trials with mixed results; most evidence is preliminary or from animal/in vitro studies. Lacks the robust RCT support of established joint ingredients like glucosamine or collagen peptides.

dosage.timing: Any time of day; consistency recommended
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically assessed after 4–8 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider; individuals with shellfish allergies should exercise caution

contraindications: Bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy (theoretical concern)

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelet drugs (potential additive effect; use with caution)
supplements: Other joint-support ingredients (e.g., glucosamine, collagen) may be combined but additive benefit unproven

name: Shatavari
summary: Ayurvedic adaptogen for women's health throughout life stages, hormonal balance, and vitality.
evidence_notes: Limited Western evidence, traditional Ayurvedic use

dosage.special_populations: Women seeking hormonal support, nursing mothers

name: Shiitake Mushroom
summary: Shiitake mushroom extract is a fungal ingredient containing bioactive compounds including polysaccharides (beta-glucans) and lentinan, traditionally used to support immune function. Limited clinical evidence supports immune-modulating properties, primarily from observational and preliminary studies.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for immune modulation and beta-glucan benefits

dosage.special_populations: Immune support seekers

name: Shilajit
summary: Himalayan mineral resin rich in fulvic acid. Traditional longevity and energy tonic.
evidence_notes: Traditional tonic, emerging research

dosage.special_populations: Energy, longevity, mineral support

upper_limit.label: Risk of heavy metals if unpurified

name: Shoden® Ashwagandha Extract (Withania somnifera)
summary: Shoden® is a proprietary ashwagandha extract standardized to 35% withanolides, the active compounds traditionally used in Ayurvedic medicine for stress management and cognitive support. Multiple clinical trials support its use for reducing cortisol, anxiety, and improving stress resilience.
mechanism_of_action: Withanolides modulate GABA receptors and may reduce cortisol levels
Adaptogenic activity supporting HPA axis regulation
evidence_notes: Ashwagandha has substantial RCT evidence for stress reduction and cortisol modulation. Shoden® specifically has published clinical research supporting efficacy. Grade B reflects good clinical evidence, though individual studies vary in methodology and effect size.

dosage.typical_range: 300-600 mg daily (of standardized extract at 35% withanolides)
dosage.timing: With meals; can be divided into morning and evening doses
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to enhance absorption and reduce potential GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent use for 4-8 weeks typically recommended to observe full benefits; safe for long-term use
dosage.special_populations: Contraindicated in pregnancy/nursing; use caution with auto-immune conditions

supplements: Other sedating adaptogens or herbal extracts (additive effects)

name: SIBO Treatment Stack
summary: Herbal antimicrobial protocol: Berberine, Oregano Oil, and Neem/Allicin for small intestinal overgrowth.
evidence_notes: Herbal alternative to antibiotics for SIBO

dosage.typical_range: Berberine 500mg 3x + Oregano oil 200mg 2x + Neem 900mg or Allicin 450mg 2x daily
dosage.special_populations: Diagnosed SIBO

supplements: Don't combine with probiotics during treatment

name: Silica (Silicon)
summary: Trace mineral for connective tissue, bone, hair, skin, and nails. Supports collagen structure.
evidence_notes: Trace mineral for structure

dosage.special_populations: Hair/skin/nails, bone health

name: Silymarin (Milk Thistle Extract)
summary: Silymarin is a standardized extract from milk thistle seeds containing flavonolignans with hepatoprotective and antioxidant properties. Well-established traditional use supported by clinical research for liver health and function.
primary_uses: Hepatic function and liver enzyme regulation
evidence_notes: Substantial clinical evidence for liver support and toxin defense; multiple RCTs support safety and efficacy. Gold standard for herbal hepatoprotection, though effect sizes in early-stage fatty liver disease are moderate. Traditional use combined with modern research justifies B-grade.

dosage.typical_range: 140–300 mg silymarin daily (often 70–150 mg per dose, 2–3 times daily)
dosage.timing: With or without food; often taken before meals or with meals
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; absorption slightly improved with fat
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically accumulate over 8–12 weeks; safe for long-term use (months to years)
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy and lactation at recommended doses (consult provider). Safe for children at appropriate doses. Renally impaired may benefit from lower doses.

common_side_effects: Allergic reactions (rare; caution in ragweed/thistle allergies)
contraindications: Ragweed, daisy, or thistle family allergies
Concurrent use with certain immunosuppressive medications

medications: CYP3A4 substrates (silymarin may inhibit; potential reduction in drug levels)
Statin drugs (may have complementary lipid effects; monitor)

name: Magnesium + L-Theanine + Glycine Stack
summary: Gentle sleep support without next-day grogginess. Non-habit forming.
evidence_notes: Gentle sleep support stack

dosage.special_populations: Sleep difficulty, non-drug approach

name: Slippery Elm
summary: Demulcent herb that coats and soothes the digestive tract, from esophagus to colon.
evidence_notes: Traditional use, limited clinical research

dosage.typical_range: 400-1000mg 3x daily, or 1-2 tsp powder in water
dosage.with_food: Often away from food for coating effect
dosage.special_populations: GERD, IBD, digestive inflammation

medications: May slow absorption - take 2 hours apart
supplements: May slow absorption of other supplements

practical_notes: Powder makes gel when mixed with water

name: Slippery Elm Bark Extract
summary: Slippery elm bark contains mucilage (a viscous polysaccharide) that may coat and soothe the gastrointestinal tract. Traditional use supports digestive comfort, though human clinical evidence remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Mucilage coating of GI tract lining
evidence_notes: Slippery elm has a long history of traditional use and in vitro studies support mucilage composition, but clinical trials in humans are sparse and small. Evidence is primarily observational or from animal models.

dosage.typical_range: 1.5-8 grams daily, often 1-2 grams per dose
dosage.timing: Can be taken with meals or between meals; often mixed with water or warm liquid
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with food may reduce potential GI irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Generally safe for long-term use; typically used for weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant and nursing women should consult a healthcare provider; may affect medication absorption if taken simultaneously

contraindications: Pregnancy (limited safety data; traditional use suggests caution)

medications: May reduce absorption of oral medications if taken simultaneously (mucilage coating effect); separate dosing by 1-2 hours
Diabetes medications (monitor blood sugar; theoretical blood sugar effects)
supplements: Other oral supplements may have reduced absorption if taken concurrently

name: Sodium
summary: An essential electrolyte critical for fluid balance, nerve function, and muscle contraction. Sodium is lost through sweat and requires dietary replenishment, especially during exercise or in hot climates.
mechanism_of_action: Maintains osmotic pressure and fluid balance across cell membranes
Required for action potential generation in nerves and muscles
Regulates blood pressure through fluid retention
evidence_notes: Sodium is a well-established essential mineral with extensive clinical evidence supporting its physiological roles. Strong RCT data on hydration, performance, and electrolyte balance.

dosage.typical_range: 500-700 mg daily (adequate intake); higher during exercise
dosage.timing: With meals and fluids; especially important during/after exercise
dosage.with_food: Typically consumed with food and water for optimal absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Continuous daily intake required; losses vary by activity level and climate
dosage.special_populations: Athletes and those in hot climates may require 500-1000 mg additional sodium during/after exercise; restricted in certain hypertension protocols

common_side_effects: Excessive intake may cause thirst, fluid retention, or elevated blood pressure in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Sodium-restricted diets (certain hypertension, heart failure, kidney disease)

medications: ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics (monitor intake)

name: Sodium Beta-Hydroxybutyrate (Sodium BHB)
summary: Sodium beta-hydroxybutyrate is an exogenous ketone supplement that provides ketone bodies to elevate blood ketone levels independent of diet. Research shows modest benefits for endurance performance and ketogenic diet adherence, though evidence for cognitive benefits in healthy individuals remains preliminary.
primary_uses: Ketosis support and ketogenic diet supplementation
Energy provision during fasting or low-carb diets
mechanism_of_action: Provides exogenous beta-hydroxybutyrate, a ketone body that can be metabolized for energy
Elevates blood ketone levels (beta-hydroxybutyrate) to mimic ketotic state
Offers alternative fuel substrate independent of glucose metabolism
evidence_notes: Some controlled studies show modest benefits for endurance performance and ketogenic diet support, but evidence is limited and mixed. Cognitive benefits in healthy individuals lack robust RCT evidence. Sodium BHB as a standalone ingredient (distinct from full ketone ester products) has fewer high-quality studies.

dosage.typical_range: 5–15 g daily, often 10 g in a single dose
dosage.timing: Pre-workout or mid-morning for performance benefits; can be taken any time on ketogenic diets
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; some prefer on empty stomach for faster absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used regularly on ketogenic diets or acutely before exercise; no established maximum duration
dosage.special_populations: Those with kidney disease should consult a physician; diabetics using insulin or medications should monitor blood glucose

common_side_effects: Mild electrolyte imbalance (due to sodium content)
contraindications: Type 1 diabetes (risk of ketoacidosis without medical supervision)

medications: Insulin and oral hypoglycemic agents (may require dose adjustment)

name: Sodium Bicarbonate
summary: Baking soda as a buffer for high-intensity exercise. Can delay fatigue but often causes GI distress.
evidence_notes: Effective but GI issues common

dosage.with_food: With small meal may help GI
dosage.special_populations: Sprint/high-intensity athletes

name: Sodium Citrate
summary: Sodium citrate is a mineral compound that provides bioavailable sodium and may support hydration status and electrolyte balance, particularly during or after exercise. It is commonly used as a buffering agent in sports supplements to enhance performance and reduce exercise-induced acidosis.
mechanism_of_action: Provides sodium to maintain osmotic balance and fluid retention
Buffers lactate and hydrogen ions during high-intensity exercise
evidence_notes: Sodium is an essential electrolyte with strong evidence for its role in hydration and performance. Sodium citrate specifically has good evidence as a buffering agent in endurance sports; multiple RCTs support modest performance benefits in high-intensity exercise lasting 1-7 minutes, though effects are modest and variable by individual.

dosage.typical_range: 500 mg to 5 g daily, depending on activity level and sodium needs
dosage.timing: During or around workouts for performance applications; with meals for general supplementation
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; with food may reduce GI distress if taken in higher doses
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use as part of balanced mineral intake; total daily sodium from all sources should not exceed recommended dietary limits (2,300 mg/day for most adults)
dosage.special_populations: Athletes and active individuals may benefit from higher intake; people with hypertension or sodium-sensitive conditions should limit intake; older adults should monitor total sodium consumption

common_side_effects: Excessive intake may cause bloating or water retention
High doses may cause mild GI upset
contraindications: Hypertension or sodium-sensitive conditions (consult healthcare provider)
Kidney disease or impaired renal function

supplements: Other sodium-containing supplements (monitor total sodium intake)

name: Sodium Copper Chlorophyllin
summary: Sodium copper chlorophyllin is a water-soluble derivative of chlorophyll that has been studied for potential detoxification, deodorizing, and antioxidant properties. Evidence for health benefits remains limited and primarily comes from in vitro and animal studies.
mechanism_of_action: Potential binding to toxins and heavy metals
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials; most evidence is preliminary, in vitro, or from older small studies. Some support for odor reduction and wound healing in animal/limited human data, but insufficient robust RCT evidence for antioxidant or detoxification claims in humans.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with food may reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used as a daily supplement; long-term safety data limited
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy/lactation not well established; use with caution

contraindications: Caution with medications affected by GI absorption

medications: May theoretically bind to certain medications; separate dosing by 2+ hours if concerned
supplements: No major known interactions with common supplements

name: Sodium (Electrolyte)
summary: Essential electrolyte often under-consumed by athletes and keto dieters. Critical for hydration and performance.
evidence_notes: Essential electrolyte

dosage.special_populations: Hypertension consult doctor

avoid_combining_with: Not needed if diet high Na

practical_notes: Athletes and keto often need more

citations.title: Sodium during exercise
citations.finding: Sodium helps maintain plasma volume during exercise

citations.title: Electrolyte balance
citations.finding: Sodium key electrolyte for hydration status

name: Sophorа Japonica Extract (Bud) – Quercetin 95% Anhydrous
summary: Quercetin is a widely-studied polyphenolic flavonoid found in plants like Sophora japonica; it functions primarily as an antioxidant and has preliminary evidence for anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular support effects.
mechanism_of_action: Potent free radical scavenging via polyphenolic structure
Mast cell stabilization and histamine modulation
NF-κB pathway inhibition and pro-inflammatory cytokine reduction
evidence_notes: Quercetin has robust in vitro and animal model evidence plus multiple human clinical trials supporting antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and mild cardiovascular benefits; however, effect sizes in humans are often modest and some trials show mixed results. Grade B reflects good cumulative evidence with some limitations in translation to clinical outcomes.

dosage.timing: With meals for better absorption and reduced GI irritation
dosage.with_food: Recommended; food enhances bioavailability, particularly with fat
dosage.duration_notes: Suitable for long-term use; benefits may take 4–8 weeks to manifest
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider; may interact with certain medications affecting drug metabolism

common_side_effects: Nausea or GI upset at high doses
Tingling in extremities (very rare, high doses)
contraindications: Bleeding disorders or concurrent anticoagulant/antiplatelet therapy (may have mild platelet effects)
Kidney disease (metabolized hepatically; use with caution at high doses)

medications: CYP3A4 and CYP2C8 substrates (potential inhibition at high doses)
Immunosuppressants (may reduce efficacy via anti-inflammatory action)

name: Sorghum bicolor Extract (Leaf) with Polyphenols
summary: ReDaxin™ is a standardized sorghum leaf extract rich in polyphenols and bioflavonoids traditionally used in Asian cuisines for potential antioxidant and metabolic support. Limited clinical research exists on this specific extract in humans, though sorghum is recognized as a polyphenol-rich grain with antioxidant properties.
mechanism_of_action: Potential modulation of antioxidant enzyme systems
evidence_notes: While sorghum is recognized as a polyphenol-rich food source with in vitro antioxidant capacity, clinical human trials specifically evaluating ReDaxin™ or sorghum leaf extract at standardized doses are limited. Evidence relies primarily on in vitro studies, traditional use, and general polyphenol biology rather than robust RCT data.

dosage.with_food: Recommended; polyphenols absorb better with dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Typical use as a daily antioxidant supplement; long-term safety data is limited
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; safety in children not established

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated; mild gastrointestinal effects possible at high doses
contraindications: Known sorghum allergy or grass pollen sensitivity
Use with caution in individuals with polyphenol-sensitive conditions

medications: Potential interaction with blood-thinning medications (polyphenols have weak antiplatelet activity)
May interact with diabetes medications if combined with other glucose-regulating agents
supplements: Other high-dose antioxidant supplements may have additive effects

name: Soy Isoflavones (Menopause)
summary: Plant estrogens from soy that may provide mild menopause symptom relief.
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence for mild hot flash relief

dosage.special_populations: Menopausal women seeking mild symptom relief

serious_warnings: Phytoestrogens - discuss with oncologist if cancer history

food_alcohol: Either way, soy away from thyroid meds

practical_notes: Discuss with oncologist if cancer history

name: Soy Protein Isolate
summary: Soy protein isolate is a plant-based complete protein containing all nine essential amino acids, derived from defatted soybean solids. Well-established evidence supports its efficacy for muscle protein synthesis and strength gains comparable to animal proteins.
mechanism_of_action: Provides all nine essential amino acids for protein synthesis
Leucine and other branched-chain amino acids stimulate mTOR pathway
Bioactive isoflavones may support bone metabolism and hormonal balance
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence demonstrates soy protein isolate produces muscle protein synthesis and strength gains equivalent to whey and other complete proteins. Large body of safety and efficacy literature supports use for athletic performance and general protein supplementation.

dosage.timing: Post-workout timing optimal; can be distributed throughout day for total protein intake
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; combining with carbohydrates post-workout enhances protein synthesis
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used long-term as a staple protein source
dosage.special_populations: Safe for most; individuals with soy allergy should avoid; those with estrogen-sensitive conditions may want to discuss with healthcare provider due to isoflavone content

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal bloating or gas in sensitive individuals
Potential allergic reactions in those with soy allergy
contraindications: Soy allergy or severe soy sensitivity
Potential caution in estrogen-dependent breast cancer (discuss with oncologist)

medications: May interact with thyroid medications—take 2-4 hours apart (theoretical concern; clinical significance unclear)

name: Spermidine
summary: Polyamine that induces autophagy - cellular cleanup. Emerging longevity compound.
evidence_notes: Emerging autophagy inducer

dosage.special_populations: Anti-aging, autophagy focus

practical_notes: Found in aged cheese, wheat germ

name: Spearmint
summary: Spearmint is a gentle herb with antioxidant properties, traditionally used for digestive support and shown to have mild anti-androgen effects.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for digestive support and anti-androgen effects in PCOS

dosage.special_populations: PCOS patients may use higher doses

practical_notes: Can be used as tea or extract

name: Spermidine
summary: Polyamine that induces autophagy, the cellular recycling process. Found in wheat germ and aged cheese, associated with longevity in population studies.
evidence_notes: Population studies link to longevity, autophagy induction confirmed

dosage.special_populations: Longevity-focused, autophagy support

food_alcohol: Wheat germ, aged cheese, mushrooms, legumes

practical_notes: Population studies link to longer lifespan
Wheat germ is rich food source

name: Spirulina
summary: Nutrient-dense blue-green algae rich in protein, vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants. Supports energy, detox, and immune function.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for nutritional support, antioxidant, and immune effects

dosage.special_populations: General nutrition

name: Split Gill Polypore
summary: A common wood-colonizing fungus with a distinctive split-gill morphology, traditionally used in Asian cuisines and folk medicine for immune modulation. Clinical evidence in humans is minimal and mostly theoretical.
mechanism_of_action: Proposed immunomodulating compounds in laboratory studies
evidence_notes: Laboratory and animal studies show potential bioactive compounds, but human clinical trials are absent or extremely limited. Mycelial biomass on rice substrate form lacks specific human efficacy data.

dosage.typical_range: 500-1500 mg daily (as part of mushroom blends)
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety profile not well characterized
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in immunocompromised individuals without medical supervision; pregnancy/lactation data unavailable

contraindications: Possible allergic reaction in mold-sensitive individuals

name: Spore-Based Probiotics
summary: Bacillus spore probiotics that survive stomach acid and shelf storage. Don't require refrigeration.
evidence_notes: Excellent survivability

dosage.special_populations: Those wanting shelf-stable option

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Likely safe at normal doses - consult provider

name: St. John's Wort
summary: St. John's Wort is a traditional herbal supplement used primarily for mood support, with moderate clinical evidence supporting its efficacy for mild-to-moderate depressive symptoms comparable to some conventional antidepressants. However, it carries significant drug-herb interaction risks and variable standardization across commercial products.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for mild-moderate depression

dosage.special_populations: Mild depression, SAD

serious_warnings: Can cause organ rejection in transplant patients
contraindications: Patients on SSRIs, SNRIs, or MAOIs

drug_interactions.effect: Reduces cyclosporine levels 40-60%, causing organ rejection
Reduces tacrolimus levels, causing organ rejection
Reduces sirolimus levels, transplant rejection risk
Reduces digoxin levels 25-40%, cardiac arrhythmia risk
Reduces indinavir levels 57%, HIV treatment failure
Reduces active metabolite levels 50%, treatment failure
Reduces imatinib levels 30-45%, cancer treatment failure
Complex interaction - may increase active metabolite

safety.interaction_summary: St. John's Wort is a POTENT inducer of CYP3A4, CYP2C9, CYP1A2, and P-glycoprotein. It reduces blood levels of 50%+ of prescription medications. ALWAYS check for interactions before use. This list is not exhaustive - consult a pharmacist.
safety.interaction_warning: DO NOT combine with: transplant medications, HIV drugs, chemotherapy, SSRIs/SNRIs, MAOIs, oral contraceptives, or warfarin.
safety.washout_period: Effects persist 2 weeks after stopping St. John's Wort due to enzyme induction

practical_notes: MAJOR drug interactions - check all meds
Effective for menopause mood with Black Cohosh

name: Stevia Extract (Stevia rebaudiana Leaf)
summary: Stevia is a non-caloric natural sweetener extracted from Stevia rebaudiana leaves, containing steviol glycosides that provide sweetness without affecting blood glucose levels. It is widely used as a sugar substitute and has been recognized as safe by major regulatory agencies.
primary_uses: Sugar substitute for blood sugar management
mechanism_of_action: Steviol glycosides bind to sweet taste receptors without caloric content or metabolic impact
Non-fermentable by oral bacteria, reducing dental caries risk
May modulate glucose homeostasis through minor pharmacological effects at high doses
evidence_notes: Stevia has extensive safety data and regulatory approval from FDA, EFSA, and others. Good evidence supports its non-caloric sweetening properties and glucose neutrality in typical use. Limited clinical evidence for specific health benefits beyond sweetening; most health claims remain preliminary.

dosage.typical_range: 250-450 mg daily (stevia extract); as a sweetener, quantities vary by product potency and intended sweetness level
dosage.timing: Use as needed; no specific timing requirement
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; regulatory approval based on extensive safety studies
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe in pregnancy and lactation at typical dietary levels; individuals with rare genetic disorder phenylketonuria (PKU) should verify no additives in specific products

common_side_effects: Rare: mild gastrointestinal effects at very high doses
Some individuals report aftertaste or metallic taste
contraindications: No absolute contraindications at approved dietary levels
Theoretical concern: very high doses may have modest effects on insulin or glucose metabolism (clinically insignificant in typical use)
Individuals with ragweed allergy may have cross-reactivity (rare)

medications: Theoretical minor interaction with antidiabetic medications at very high doses, though clinical significance is minimal at normal use levels

name: Stevia Leaf Powder (Stevia rebaudiana)
summary: Stevia is a natural zero-calorie sweetener derived from the leaf of Stevia rebaudiana, approximately 200–300 times sweeter than sucrose. It has been used for centuries in South America and is approved as a food additive in many countries, though whole leaf preparations differ from purified steviol glycoside extracts.
mechanism_of_action: Steviol glycosides bind to sweet taste receptors without metabolic caloric impact
May enhance insulin secretion and improve glucose handling (mechanism not fully established)
evidence_notes: Stevia has long historical use and regulatory approval; safety profile well-established in food applications. Modest evidence supports potential modest benefits for glycemic control in some populations. Whole leaf powder is less standardized than purified extracts; evidence grade reflects established safety rather than strong efficacy claims.

dosage.typical_range: 1–3 grams (approximately 1/4 to 3/4 teaspoon) per serving as a sweetener; total daily intake typically 1–10 grams depending on sweetness preference
dosage.with_food: Not required; can be mixed into beverages or foods
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; no established duration limits
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe in pregnancy and lactation by major regulatory bodies; suitable for diabetics and those managing blood sugar

contraindications: Individuals with allergies to plants in the Asteraceae family (ragweed, chrysanthemum)
Those taking medications for blood pressure or blood sugar regulation (potential additive effects; medical supervision advised)

medications: Antihypertensive medications (theoretical potential for additive effects)
supplements: Other blood sugar-supporting supplements (monitor combined effect)

name: Stinging Nettle
summary: Stinging nettle leaf is a traditional herbal remedy used to support joint health, urinary function, and general wellness. Evidence for specific health claims is moderate and primarily from smaller clinical studies and traditional use.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for prostate and SHBG effects

dosage.special_populations: Men with prostate concerns, those seeking free T support

name: Stress Resilience Stack
summary: Comprehensive stress support: Ashwagandha + Rhodiola + Holy Basil + Magnesium.
evidence_notes: Synergistic adaptogens

dosage.timing: Rhodiola AM, Ashwagandha PM, Tulsi anytime
dosage.special_populations: High stress

name: Strontium
summary: Bone mineral that may support bone density. Take separately from calcium.
evidence_notes: Some evidence for bone density

dosage.special_populations: Osteoporosis risk, postmenopausal

serious_warnings: Interferes with bone density scans (artifactually high readings)

name: Succinic Acid
summary: A four-carbon dicarboxylic acid and key intermediate in the citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle). Used as an energy metabolism support ingredient in wellness formulas.
evidence_notes: Minimal human clinical evidence; primarily mechanistic interest based on biochemical role. Very limited evidence for supplementation benefits in humans.

dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to minimize GI irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Limited guidance; used as part of formulas
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe (GRAS); limited specific data

common_side_effects: Possible mild GI upset at high doses

name: Sugar Alcohols
summary: Sugar alcohols are carbohydrate derivatives used as low-calorie sweeteners in supplements and foods, with minimal impact on blood glucose levels. They contain fewer calories than regular sugar but may cause digestive side effects in some individuals.
evidence_notes: Well-established food additive with extensive safety data; minimal impact on blood glucose is well-documented across multiple studies

dosage.special_populations: IBS or sensitive GI patients may experience bloating or laxative effects

name: Sulbutiamine
summary: Synthetic thiamine derivative crossing BBB. Reduces mental fatigue and may support mood.
evidence_notes: Fat-soluble thiamine with CNS effects

dosage.special_populations: Mental fatigue, asthenia

name: Sulforaphane
summary: Potent Nrf2 activator from broccoli sprouts. Induces phase II detoxification and has anticancer properties.
evidence_notes: Powerful Nrf2 activator

dosage.special_populations: Detox, cancer prevention

moderate_dose.label: 10-30 mg/day
moderate_dose.notes: Stable form preferred (e.g. broccoli sprout extract)

upper_limit.label: No established UL
upper_limit.notes: Pair with myrosinase or use stable forms

name: Sunifiram
summary: Ampakine nootropic many times more potent than piracetam. Very limited research.
evidence_notes: Very limited human data

dosage.special_populations: Experimental use

name: Supercritical CO2 Boswellia sacra Extract
summary: Boswellia sacra is a tree resin traditionally used in Ayurvedic and traditional medicine, with modern research suggesting potential anti-inflammatory and joint-support properties via boswellic acid compounds. Supercritical CO2 extraction concentrates these active constituents while avoiding solvent residues.
mechanism_of_action: Boswellic acids inhibit NF-κB pathway and reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines
evidence_notes: Boswellia sacra and frankincense extracts have multiple RCTs showing modest benefits for joint comfort and function, with consistent mechanistic support.
