Supercritical CO2 extraction is a premium form but lacks direct comparative RCT data versus standard extracts; evidence applies to boswellia genus generally.

dosage.typical_range: 300–600 mg daily (extract; equivalent to 1.5–3 g crude resin)
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food or fat to enhance bioavailability
dosage.duration_notes: Typical studies use 8–12 weeks; benefits may accumulate over time
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider before use

contraindications: Allergy to Boswellia or related resins

medications: May have mild anticoagulant properties; monitor with blood thinners
Potential interaction with NSAIDs (additive anti-inflammatory effect)
supplements: Other anti-inflammatory herbs (curcumin, ginger) may have additive effects

name: Supercritical CO2 Coriander Extract
summary: Supercritical CO2 extraction of coriander fruit concentrates volatile oils, particularly linalool, which may support cognitive function and stress response. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most data comes from traditional use and in vitro studies.
mechanism_of_action: Linalool and other volatile compounds may modulate GABAergic neurotransmission
Traditional use suggests anxiolytic and mild sedative properties
evidence_notes: Coriander has traditional use for cognitive and digestive health, and linalool shows promise in preclinical studies for anxiolytic effects. However, human clinical trials specifically on supercritical CO2 coriander extract are minimal. The 15% linalool standardization is a quality marker but does not elevate evidence grade without clinical validation.

dosage.timing: Once or twice daily; morning or early afternoon preferred if stimulating effects desired
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; some prefer with food to minimize GI sensitivity
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used continuously for cognitive or stress support; benefits may emerge after 2-4 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider; safety in children not well established

common_side_effects: Rare: mild GI upset, allergic reaction (in sensitive individuals)
contraindications: Allergy to Apiaceae family plants (carrot, celery, parsnip, parsley, fennel)

medications: Potential additive sedative effects with CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, sedating antihistamines)
supplements: Potential additive anxiolytic/sedative effects with other calming herbs (valerian, passionflower, ashwagandha)

name: Supercritical CO2 Myrrh Extract (Commiphora myrrha)
summary: Myrrh is a traditional resin used in Ayurvedic and traditional medicine with emerging evidence for anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Supercritical CO2 extraction concentrates bioactive compounds including furanodiene, though human clinical evidence remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Furanodiene and other sesquiterpenes may inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines
Antimicrobial activity against oral and digestive pathogens
evidence_notes: Myrrh has a long traditional use history and in vitro/animal studies support anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. However, human RCT evidence is limited and sparse. The 4% furanodiene standardization is a quality marker but does not change the overall evidence base. Grade C reflects preliminary/moderate evidence rather than strong clinical trials.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; taking with food may reduce gastrointestinal sensitivity
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests 4-12 weeks for noticeable effects; no established maximum duration
dosage.special_populations: Avoid during pregnancy/breastfeeding; may interact with anticoagulants

common_side_effects: Allergic skin reactions in sensitive individuals (very rare)
contraindications: Bleeding disorders or concurrent anticoagulant therapy

medications: Warfarin and anticoagulants (theoretical risk of potentiation)
supplements: Other anticoagulant herbs (e.g., ginkgo, garlic) — use with caution

name: Superfood Antioxidant Blend
summary: A proprietary blend of plant-based ingredients marketed for antioxidant support, typically containing fruit and vegetable extracts. Specific composition and potency depend on the exact formula, which varies by product.
mechanism_of_action: Scavenging free radicals via polyphenols and phytonutrients
evidence_notes: Proprietary blends lack transparency regarding active components and their concentrations. Individual ingredients may have antioxidant properties, but the blend's efficacy as formulated is not well-established in peer-reviewed literature. Grade reflects preliminary/mixed evidence typical of multi-ingredient formulations.

dosage.with_food: Recommended for absorption and GI tolerance
dosage.special_populations: Generally suitable for adults 50+; pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider

common_side_effects: Rare; generally well-tolerated in multivitamin context
contraindications: Individuals with allergies to fruits, vegetables, or plant extracts should review full ingredient list

medications: Possible interactions with anticoagulants if blend contains high-dose vitamin K (unlikely in small amounts)

name: Surgery Recovery Stack
summary: Pre/post-surgery support: zinc, vitamin C, arginine, glutamine, and bromelain.
evidence_notes: Surgical healing support

dosage.typical_range: 30-50mg zinc + 1000-2000mg vitamin C + 5-10g arginine + 5-15g glutamine + 500mg bromelain
dosage.timing: Start before surgery if possible, continue after
dosage.special_populations: Surgical patients, wound healing

name: Synephrine (Bitter Orange)
summary: Citrus extract for thermogenesis. Similar to ephedrine but supposedly safer.
evidence_notes: Modest evidence, safety concerns debated

dosage.special_populations: Fat loss with stimulant tolerance

name: Tart Cherry Extract
summary: Tart cherry extract is a concentrated source of anthocyanins and other polyphenols that has been studied for supporting sleep quality, exercise recovery, and inflammatory markers. Evidence suggests potential benefits for sleep duration and post-exercise muscle recovery, though effects are generally modest.
evidence_notes: Reduces exercise-induced muscle damage

dosage.special_populations: Athletes, sleep issues

name: Tart Cherry (Prunus cerasus)
summary: A fruit-derived extract rich in anthocyanins and melatonin with evidence supporting improvements in sleep quality, duration, and athletic recovery. The Montmorency variety is most commonly studied.
mechanism_of_action: Endogenous melatonin content (0.135 mcg per ounce of juice)
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs show modest but consistent improvements in sleep duration (26-84 min increase) and sleep efficiency; anthocyanin and melatonin content well-characterized.

dosage.typical_range: 8-12 oz juice equivalent per day, or 480-1200 mg standardized extract
dosage.timing: 1-2 hours before bed (to align with melatonin content)
dosage.with_food: Can take with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically observed after 7-14 days of consistent use; optimal with 2+ weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; diabetics should monitor carbohydrate content of juice forms

common_side_effects: Juice forms may cause mild GI effects in sensitive individuals

medications: Warfarin (high polyphenol content theoretically possible, monitor INR)
supplements: Minimal interactions; may complement other sleep supports

name: Taurine
summary: Conditionally essential amino acid abundant in heart, brain, and eyes. Supports cardiovascular health and calm energy.
evidence_notes: Cardiovascular and neurological support

dosage.timing: Any time; before exercise for performance
dosage.special_populations: Vegans (not in plants), heart health

practical_notes: Not stimulating despite being in energy drinks

name: Taurine (Longevity Dose)
summary: Conditionally essential amino acid with emerging longevity research. 2023 Science paper linked declining taurine to aging across species.
evidence_notes: Strong 2023 Science paper on longevity, established cardioprotective effects

dosage.typical_range: 500-3000mg daily; up to 6g in some longevity protocols
dosage.special_populations: Longevity, heart health, vegans (no dietary source)

name: Taurine (for Relaxation)
summary: Calming amino acid supporting GABA. High concentrations in brain and heart.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for calming

dosage.special_populations: Relaxation, cardiovascular

name: Taurine (Sulfur Amino Acid)
summary: Sulfur-containing amino acid supporting heart, brain, and bile acid function.
evidence_notes: Conditional amino acid

dosage.special_populations: Heart health, vegetarians, energy drinks alternative

name: TeaCrine (Theacrine)
summary: Long-lasting caffeine-like compound without tolerance. Provides sustained energy and focus.
evidence_notes: Long-lasting caffeine alternative

dosage.special_populations: Sustained energy needs

name: Tetrahydromagnolol
summary: A semi-synthetic derivative or bioactive from Magnolia officinalis bark that exhibits anxiolytic and neuroprotective properties. Often studied as part of magnolia bark extract formulations for relaxation and emotional support.
mechanism_of_action: Reduction of cortisol and stress markers
evidence_notes: Limited clinical trials specifically on tetrahydromagnolol; most evidence derives from magnolia bark extract and honokiol/magnolol studies. Animal and in vitro data support mechanism, but robust human RCTs are lacking for this isolated derivative.

dosage.typical_range: 100–300 mg daily (as formulated; exact dosage varies by product)
dosage.timing: Evening or as needed for relaxation; may take 2–4 weeks for adaptation
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Regular use typically recommended; no established upper limit, but long-term safety data is limited
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy/nursing: avoid; pediatrics: insufficient data

common_side_effects: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses
contraindications: Driving or operating machinery if sedation occurs
Concurrent use with strong sedatives without medical guidance

medications: CNS depressants (benzodiazepines, opioids) — may have additive sedative effect
Antidepressants — potential interaction via monoamine pathways
supplements: Other magnolia extracts — redundant dosing risk

name: Theanine
summary: Amino acid from tea promoting calm focus without drowsiness. Alpha brain waves.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for stress and anxiety

dosage.special_populations: Caffeine users, daytime anxiety

name: Theobromine
summary: Theobromine is a naturally occurring alkaloid found in cacao and tea with stimulant and mild thermogenic properties. It has a slower onset and longer duration than caffeine, with modest evidence supporting its use for energy and fat oxidation in pre-workout contexts.
mechanism_of_action: phosphodiesterase inhibition leading to increased cAMP
evidence_notes: Theobromine has moderate human evidence from controlled trials showing modest improvements in energy, cognition, and fat oxidation. It is less potent than caffeine but has a distinct pharmacological profile. Well-established safety profile supports B grade; not A due to smaller evidence base compared to caffeine.

dosage.timing: 30-60 minutes before activity or as part of pre-workout formula
dosage.with_food: can be taken with or without food; fat content may slightly slow absorption
dosage.duration_notes: longer half-life than caffeine (5-10 hours); effects build over repeated doses
dosage.special_populations: Sensitive individuals may prefer lower doses (100-150 mg); pregnant/nursing women should minimize use

common_side_effects: insomnia or sleep disruption if taken late in day

medications: may increase effect of stimulant medications
potential interaction with some cardiac medications
supplements: additive stimulant effects with caffeine, guarana, or other xanthines
may amplify effects of sympathomimetic compounds

name: Thiamin (Vitamin B1)
summary: Thiamin is an essential B vitamin required for carbohydrate metabolism, nerve function, and energy production. It plays a critical role in converting glucose into usable cellular energy.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor in pyruvate dehydrogenase complex for energy metabolism
Essential for transketolase in pentose phosphate pathway
Required for myelin formation in nervous system
evidence_notes: Thiamin is an essential micronutrient with extensive clinical evidence supporting its role in metabolism and neurological function. Deficiency prevention and treatment are well-established.

dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; absorption enhanced with food
dosage.duration_notes: Daily supplementation for chronic support; therapeutic doses may vary
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/lactating women: 1.4 mg daily; higher doses used therapeutically for Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome

common_side_effects: Very rare; occasional skin reactions at high doses
contraindications: None at nutritional doses; use cautiously in those with known thiamin allergy

medications: Diuretics may increase urinary loss; alcohol impairs absorption
supplements: Generally compatible with other B vitamins

name: Thiamine (Vitamin B1)
summary: Water-soluble B vitamin essential for energy metabolism and nervous system function. Acts as a coenzyme in carbohydrate metabolism and neurotransmitter synthesis.
mechanism_of_action: Serves as coenzyme (thiamine pyrophosphate) in carbohydrate metabolism
Essential for pyruvate dehydrogenase complex function
Required for transketolase in pentose phosphate pathway
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for deficiency correction and energy metabolism support. Well-established essential nutrient with clear clinical evidence for neurological function.

dosage.typical_range: 1.1 mg daily RDA (women 50+: 1.1 mg); supplements typically 25-100 mg
dosage.with_food: Absorption enhanced with food; may cause GI upset on empty stomach
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use. Water-soluble excess is excreted in urine.
dosage.special_populations: Women 50+ have same RDA as younger women. Older adults with poor nutrition, chronic diarrhea, or alcohol use disorder at increased risk of deficiency.

common_side_effects: Very high doses may cause nausea, headache, or irritability

medications: Chemotherapy agents (5-FU, cisplatin) - impair thiamine metabolism
supplements: Other B vitamins - work synergistically in B-complex supplements

name: Thyroid Support Stack
summary: Comprehensive thyroid support: Selenium, Zinc, Iodine (carefully dosed), Tyrosine, Ashwagandha.
evidence_notes: Evidence-based comprehensive thyroid nutrient support

dosage.typical_range: Selenium 200mcg + Zinc 15-30mg + Iodine 150-200mcg (if deficient) + Tyrosine 500-1000mg + Ashwagandha 300-600mg
dosage.timing: Morning, separate iodine from thyroid meds
dosage.with_food: Zinc, selenium with food; Tyrosine away from protein
dosage.special_populations: Subclinical hypothyroid, nutrient-based thyroid support

medications: Thyroid medications (may reduce need, must monitor)

name: TMG (Trimethylglycine/Betaine)
summary: Methyl donor supporting homocysteine metabolism and methylation. Important cofactor when taking NAD+ precursors that consume methyl groups.
evidence_notes: Well-established for homocysteine and methylation support

dosage.special_populations: NAD+ precursor users, MTHFR variants, high homocysteine

name: Tongkat Ali (Longjack)
summary: A Southeast Asian herb traditionally used to support testosterone levels and male vitality. Recent clinical evidence suggests it may modestly improve testosterone, muscle strength, and sexual function in men.
evidence_notes: Multiple studies showing testosterone and fertility benefits

dosage.special_populations: Men with low testosterone, stress

name: Tongkat Ali (Longjack) Extract
summary: Tongkat Ali is a traditional Southeast Asian herb studied for potential effects on testosterone levels, sexual function, and exercise performance. Evidence suggests modest benefits in specific populations, particularly men with low testosterone, but results are mixed and often limited to branded extracts.
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence from multiple RCTs showing benefits for testosterone in hypogonadal men and sexual function in some populations. Benefits for athletic performance less clear. Quality varies significantly by extract standardization; most positive studies use specific branded extracts (LJ100). Evidence is weaker in normotestosterolemic men.

dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble compounds benefit from food
dosage.duration_notes: Studies typically show effects after 4-12 weeks of consistent use; some effects may require 8+ weeks
dosage.special_populations: Most evidence in men; limited safety data in women and individuals under 18

common_side_effects: Insomnia or sleep disturbances (especially if taken late in day)
contraindications: Use with caution in individuals with sleep disorders

name: Total Omega-3 Fatty Acids
summary: The collective measurement of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, including EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid), DHA (docosahexaenoic acid), and ALA (alpha-linolenic acid), which are essential fatty acids critical for cardiovascular, neurological, and inflammatory health. Supplements typically list total omega-3 content alongside individual EPA/DHA amounts.
mechanism_of_action: substrate for anti-inflammatory mediator production (resolvins, protectins)
incorporation into phospholipids in cell membranes
modulation of gene expression related to lipid metabolism
evidence_notes: Robust RCT evidence from major cardiovascular trials; consistent meta-analyses demonstrate benefits for triglycerides and cardiovascular outcomes. EPA+DHA efficacy best established at 2-4g daily; ALA evidence more modest.

dosage.typical_range: 500-4000 mg daily total omega-3 (composition varies: typically 300-2000 mg EPA+DHA combined)
dosage.timing: with largest meal of the day
dosage.with_food: absorption substantially improved when taken with meals containing dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: minimum 4-12 weeks for measurable cardiovascular/triglyceride effects; long-term use (6-24 months) typically required for cognitive or anti-inflammatory outcomes
dosage.special_populations: pregnant/nursing women: 200-300 mg DHA daily recommended; older adults: may derive greater cardiovascular benefit; individuals with familial hypertriglyceridemia: higher doses (2-4g daily) used under medical supervision

contraindications: active hemorrhage or severe bleeding disorder
scheduled surgery within 1 week (may increase bleeding risk)
allergy to fish or marine organisms (fish oil source)

medications: anticoagulants (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran) - monitor INR/bleeding
antiplatelet agents (aspirin, clopidogrel, prasugrel) - additive effect at high doses
NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) - increased bleeding risk at high omega-3 doses
blood pressure lowering medications (potential additive hypotensive effect)
supplements: vitamin E at high doses (>400 IU daily) - may increase bleeding risk in combination
ginger, garlic, turmeric, curcumin - potential additive antiplatelet/anticoagulant effects

name: Total Omega Fatty Acids
summary: A collective measure of essential polyunsaturated fatty acids (primarily omega-3 and omega-6) derived from marine or plant sources. Total omega content reflects the combined concentration of these metabolically important lipids.
mechanism_of_action: reduction of triglycerides and LDL cholesterol
evidence_notes: Strong evidence supports omega-3 fatty acids for cardiovascular health; total omega content varies by source and processing, making standardized evidence more complex than individual components

dosage.with_food: Take with food to reduce gastrointestinal upset and improve absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent daily intake recommended; benefits often apparent after 4–12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy: consult healthcare provider regarding mercury and vitamin A content; anticoagulant users should monitor intake

name: Total Tocotrienols (Delta, Gamma-tocotrienols) (DeltaGold®)
summary: A blend of palm-derived tocotrienols (a form of vitamin E) enriched in delta and gamma isomers with distinct antioxidant and neuroprotective properties. DeltaGold® is a branded, characterized tocotrienol complex.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for antioxidant and neuroprotective benefits, particularly delta and gamma isomers. DeltaGold® has proprietary research supporting efficacy.

dosage.timing: With a meal containing fat for optimal absorption
dosage.special_populations: Those on blood thinners should consult healthcare provider at doses >400 mg daily

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated; rare mild GI upset
contraindications: Caution with anticoagulants at high doses

medications: May have additive effects with antiplatelet/anticoagulant agents at high doses
supplements: Compatible with other fat-soluble vitamins; separate from iron by 2+ hours

name: Trace Mineral Drops
summary: Concentrated liquid minerals from sea or salt lake sources. Provides a spectrum of trace minerals that may be lacking in modern diets.
evidence_notes: Provides spectrum of trace minerals
mechanism_of_action: Replaces minerals lost from soil depletion

dosage.typical_range: 1/4 to 1/2 tsp daily or as directed
dosage.special_populations: Those on mineral-poor diets

name: Trametes versicolor Extract (Fruiting Body)
summary: Trametes versicolor is a medicinal mushroom rich in beta-glucans and bioactive polysaccharides, traditionally used in Asian medicine to support immune function and gut health. Clinical research, primarily from Asia, suggests potential benefits for immune support and as an adjunctive therapy, though evidence in Western populations remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Beta-glucan polysaccharides activate immune cells (macrophages, NK cells, T cells)
Prebiotic effects supporting beneficial gut bacteria
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity from phenolic compounds
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence from Asian clinical trials (particularly PSK/PSP extracts) showing immune and gut health benefits, but limited high-quality RCT data in Western populations. Many studies are small or from single research centers. Stronger evidence exists for specific standardized extracts (PSK, PSP) than for general fruiting body extracts.

dosage.typical_range: 1,000-3,000 mg daily (fruiting body extract); 1,000-2,000 mg daily (standardized PSK/PSP)
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; with meals may improve absorption
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food to improve tolerability and polysaccharide absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Benefits typically appear after 4+ weeks of consistent use; research protocols often use 3-12 months
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for most adults; limited safety data in children or during pregnancy/lactation

common_side_effects: Rare allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Avoid if allergic to mushrooms or molds
Use with caution in autoimmune conditions (may overstimulate immune response)

medications: Anticoagulants/antiplatelet agents — theoretical additive effects, use cautiously
supplements: Other immune-stimulating mushrooms or supplements — additive effects

name: Trans-Resveratrol (from Polygonum cuspidatum root extract)
summary: A polyphenolic compound extracted from Polygonum cuspidatum (Japanese knotweed) root with potent antioxidant and sirtuin-activating properties. Trans-resveratrol is the bioactive isomer found in high concentrations in knotweed root.
evidence_notes: Good evidence in animal and cell models for sirtuin activation and cardiovascular support; moderate human evidence. Polygonum cuspidatum is a rich, well-characterized source of trans-resveratrol.

dosage.timing: With meals for improved absorption and bioavailability
dosage.with_food: With food; bioavailability significantly enhanced with fat
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; effects may improve with consistent dosing over weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Those on blood thinners or with hormone-sensitive cancers should consult healthcare provider before high-dose supplementation

common_side_effects: Generally well-tolerated; rare mild GI upset or headache
contraindications: Caution with anticoagulants at very high doses; theoretical concern with hormone-sensitive cancers (human evidence limited)

medications: May have additive antiplatelet effects with blood thinners
Potential CYP3A4/CYP1A2 interactions at high doses (limited human data)
supplements: Synergistic with other sirtuin activators; enhances NAD+ pathways

name: Tremella fuciformis Extract
summary: Tremella fuciformis is a medicinal mushroom traditionally used in Asian medicine, with emerging research suggesting potential cognitive and immune-supporting properties through its polysaccharide content. Limited clinical evidence exists in Western research, though traditional use spans centuries in Chinese medicine.
mechanism_of_action: Beta-glucan signaling through pattern recognition receptors
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials in Western research. Most evidence is traditional use and preclinical studies examining polysaccharide fractions. No large RCTs specifically on cognitive or immune endpoints in humans.

dosage.typical_range: 500-2000 mg daily (extract), or 1-3 g dried fruit body
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; often divided into 2 doses
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; may improve absorption with meals
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests ongoing supplementation; minimum 4-8 weeks for assessment of effects
dosage.special_populations: Safe profile in pregnancy/lactation traditionally, but limited modern safety data

common_side_effects: Possible mild GI effects at high doses
contraindications: Severe immunosuppression (theoretical concern with immune-modulatory polysaccharides)

supplements: No significant known interactions with other supplements

name: Tremella Mushroom Extract
summary: Tremella fuciformis is a gelatinous mushroom traditionally used in Asian cuisine and herbal medicine, primarily studied for potential skin hydration and immune support properties. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most research is in vitro or animal models.
mechanism_of_action: Polysaccharide content may support skin moisture and hydration
Beta-glucans may modulate immune cell activity
Antioxidant compounds may reduce oxidative stress
evidence_notes: Primarily in vitro and animal studies support bioactive polysaccharides and beta-glucans; human clinical trials are limited and mostly unpublished or non-peer-reviewed. Traditional use in Asian medicine is well-documented but does not constitute high-level evidence.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time of day; often consumed with meals
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; food may aid absorption of fat-soluble compounds if extracted with lipids
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests long-term consumption; optimal duration for benefits unclear from clinical literature
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and pediatric populations not well established; individuals with mushroom allergies should avoid

common_side_effects: Rare: gastrointestinal upset, nausea, or allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Immunocompromised individuals should consult a healthcare provider before use
Pregnancy and lactation: insufficient safety data

medications: Potential interaction with immunosuppressant medications (theoretical, due to immune-modulating polysaccharides)
supplements: No major documented interactions with common supplements

name: Triacetylrudine
summary: Triacetylrudine is a acetylated form of uridine that crosses the blood-brain barrier more efficiently than uridine alone, potentially supporting nucleotide synthesis and cognitive function. Limited human research exists, with most evidence derived from cell-based and animal models.
mechanism_of_action: Acetylation enhances blood-brain barrier penetration relative to uridine
Provides substrate for nucleotide and RNA synthesis in neurons
Potential neuroprotective effects through enhanced pyrimidine metabolism
evidence_notes: Triacetylrudine has theoretical neurochemical advantages over uridine, but human clinical trials are sparse and preliminary. Most support comes from in vitro and animal research. The prodrug form is more bioavailable than parent uridine, but translatable cognitive benefits in humans remain inadequately demonstrated.

dosage.timing: Morning to early afternoon preferred; may have mild stimulant-like effects
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; fat intake may enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Optimal duration of use not established; typical cognitive support protocols suggest 4-12 weeks minimum to assess effects
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and children not established; caution in gout or uric acid metabolism disorders

common_side_effects: Sleep disruption at high doses or late timing (theoretical)
contraindications: History of gout or elevated uric acid (uridine metabolized to uric acid)

medications: Potential interaction with uric acid-lowering medications (allopurinol, febuxostat)
May affect medications dependent on purine/pyrimidine metabolism
supplements: Choline, pantothenic acid, and other nucleotide precursors may have complementary effects
Avoid combining with other uridine sources without medical guidance

name: Triacetylruridine
summary: Triacetylruridine is a prodrug form of uridine designed for enhanced bioavailability and membrane permeability to support cellular energy metabolism and cognitive function. Preliminary evidence suggests potential benefits for cognitive performance and neuroprotection, though human clinical data remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Acetylated uridine prodrug improving blood-brain barrier penetration
Increases intracellular uridine availability for nucleotide synthesis
Supports ATP and phospholipid production in neural tissue
May enhance membrane fluidity and synaptic function
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials; mostly in vitro and animal model data supporting neurological mechanisms. Some observational use in nootropic communities but lacks robust RCT evidence for cognitive endpoints in healthy humans.

dosage.timing: Morning or early afternoon for cognitive support
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; food may slightly enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Often used in 4-12 week cycles; long-term safety profile not extensively studied
dosage.special_populations: Pregnancy and lactation: safety not established; use with caution in those with uridine metabolism disorders

contraindications: History of gout or elevated uric acid (uridine metabolizes to uric acid)
Concurrent use with high-dose cytotoxic chemotherapy

medications: Potential interaction with antiretroviral nucleoside analogues (theoretical)
supplements: Other uridine or uracil-based supplements may have additive effects

name: Tribulus Terrestris
summary: Traditional herb for libido. Less evidence for testosterone but may help libido.
evidence_notes: Better evidence for libido than testosterone

dosage.special_populations: Libido support

name: Tribulus Terrestris
summary: Tribulus terrestris is a traditional plant extract standardized to protodioscin, a steroidal saponin claimed to support sexual function and athletic performance. Evidence for these effects remains limited and inconsistent, with most studies showing modest benefits at best.
mechanism_of_action: Does NOT significantly raise testosterone despite marketing
evidence_notes: Better evidence for libido than testosterone increase

dosage.special_populations: Those seeking libido support

supplements: Stack with actual T-boosters if seeking that

name: Trigonella foenum-graecum Extract (Seed)
summary: A seed extract containing trigonelline and protodioscin with traditional use for hormonal health, lactation support, and blood sugar management. Moderate evidence supports some metabolic applications.
mechanism_of_action: Protodioscin content provides hormonal support (limited evidence)
evidence_notes: Good evidence for blood sugar and lactation support; moderate clinical trial data. Sexual function claims have less robust support. Well-studied for glycemic effects.

dosage.typical_range: 500-1000 mg daily of standardized extract (or 1-5g whole seed)
dosage.timing: With meals for blood sugar; flexible for other uses
dosage.with_food: Recommended for GI tolerance and absorption
dosage.duration_notes: 4-8 weeks for glycemic effects; lactation benefits within days
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy for lactation support (consult provider); monitor glucose if diabetic

name: Trimethylglycine (Betaine Anhydrous)
summary: Trimethylglycine is a naturally occurring compound that serves as a methyl donor in cellular metabolism and may support cardiovascular health and athletic performance. Evidence supports its role in homocysteine metabolism and some ergogenic benefits, though overall research is moderate.
mechanism_of_action: Methyl group donor in one-carbon metabolism
Reduces plasma homocysteine via conversion to dimethylglycine
Enhances cellular hydration and osmolyte balance
evidence_notes: Good evidence for homocysteine-lowering effects and some athletic performance benefits (particularly anaerobic power); well-conducted RCTs exist but sample sizes are modest. Strong mechanistic rationale supported by clinical data, though long-term cardiovascular outcomes remain less established.

dosage.timing: Typically divided into 500–1,500 mg doses; timing relative to exercise may enhance ergogenic effects
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; may be taken with meals to minimize any gastrointestinal effects
dosage.duration_notes: Effects on homocysteine typically evident after 2–4 weeks of consistent use; ergogenic benefits may develop over similar timeframe
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in healthy adults; limited safety data in pregnant/nursing women or those with genetic methylation disorders

contraindications: Caution in individuals with homocystinuria or genetic methylation disorders
May require monitoring if combined with other homocysteine-lowering agents

medications: B vitamins (folate, B6, B12): synergistic for homocysteine management
supplements: Creatine: may have additive ergogenic effects
Folate, B6, B12: enhanced homocysteine reduction

name: Triphala
summary: Triphala is a traditional Ayurvedic formula combining three dried fruits known for digestive and mild laxative properties. Limited clinical evidence supports use for digestive health, though traditional use is extensive.
evidence_notes: Traditional formula with modern research support for GI benefits

dosage.with_food: Usually without, before meals or bedtime
dosage.special_populations: Digestive support, gentle cleansing

medications: May affect drug absorption - separate by 2 hours

practical_notes: May take few days to regulate

name: TUDCA (Tauroursodeoxycholic Acid)
summary: Water-soluble bile acid that protects liver cells, supports bile flow, and has neuroprotective properties.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for liver and bile support
mechanism_of_action: Protects hepatocytes from toxic bile acids

dosage.duration_notes: Can use ongoing for liver support
dosage.special_populations: Those with liver concerns, bile flow issues, steroid users

medications: May affect absorption of some drugs

practical_notes: Popular with steroid users for liver protection

name: Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor)
summary: Most-researched medicinal mushroom for immune support, particularly studied as adjunct in cancer care.
evidence_notes: Strong research especially for immune modulation
mechanism_of_action: PSK and PSP are potent immunomodulators
Enhances T-cell and NK cell activity
Used as cancer adjunct in Japan

dosage.special_populations: Those seeking immune support, cancer adjunct therapy (with oncologist)

serious_warnings: Use as cancer adjunct only with oncologist approval

medications: Chemotherapy (discuss with oncologist - may be synergistic)

name: Turkey Tail (Trametes versicolor) Mycelium
summary: Turkey Tail is a medicinal fungus containing bioactive polysaccharides (beta-glucans) and other compounds studied for immune support. Evidence for immune function is moderate, primarily from in vitro and animal studies, with limited human clinical data.
mechanism_of_action: Beta-glucan activation of immune cells (macrophages, natural killer cells)
Prebiotic effects supporting beneficial gut bacteria
evidence_notes: Moderate evidence from in vitro and animal studies; some human studies exist but are limited in sample size and methodological rigor. Well-established traditional use in Asian medicine. Fermented mycelium on brown rice is a common commercial form but bioavailability and active compound content vary by production method.

dosage.typical_range: 1,000–2,000 mg daily (standardized extract or mycelium biomass)
dosage.with_food: Recommended; may enhance bioavailability and reduce gastrointestinal upset
dosage.duration_notes: Typically taken continuously for 4–12 weeks; long-term use is considered safe based on traditional use
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult a healthcare provider; immunocompromised individuals should discuss with their physician

common_side_effects: Allergic reactions (very rare, in susceptible individuals)
contraindications: Known allergy to mushrooms or fungi
Concurrent immunosuppressive medication (consult physician—may enhance immune activity)
Uncontrolled autoimmune conditions (theoretical concern; medical consultation advised)

medications: Immunosuppressants (cyclosporine, tacrolimus): potential counteraction; monitor with physician
Anticoagulants/antiplatelet drugs: theoretical interaction; seek medical advice
supplements: Other immune-supporting mushrooms (reishi, shiitake, maitake): additive effects possible; monitor response

name: Turkey Tails Mycelium
summary: Turkey Tails mycelium is rich in polysaccharides (particularly PSK and PSP) with established traditional use in Asia and growing clinical research for immune and gastrointestinal health. Several human studies support benefits for immune function.
mechanism_of_action: PSK and PSP polysaccharides activate immune cells
evidence_notes: Strong evidence from multiple human studies, particularly from Asia, for immune-modulating effects. PSK (polysaccharide-K) is studied as adjunctive in oncology. Evidence quality is good but Western RCTs could be more robust.

dosage.typical_range: 1,500-3,000 mg daily (mycelium form); 3-9 grams daily if PSK/PSP standardized
dosage.duration_notes: Designed for consistent long-term use; benefits may accumulate over weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe; immunocompromised patients should consult healthcare provider

name: Turmeric Extract (Curcuma longa)
summary: Turmeric extract is a standardized source of curcumin, a polyphenol compound with demonstrated anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties supported by substantial clinical research. Multiple RCTs show efficacy for joint comfort, inflammatory markers, and general wellness when combined with bioavailability enhancers like black pepper extract (piperine).
mechanism_of_action: Suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8)
Free radical scavenging and antioxidant activity
Modulation of COX-2 and other inflammatory enzymes
evidence_notes: Good clinical evidence for anti-inflammatory and joint health benefits in multiple RCTs, though bioavailability remains a limitation without absorption enhancers. Extensive mechanistic research supports safety and efficacy. Grade reflects established but somewhat modest effect sizes in some populations.

dosage.with_food: Take with fat-containing food and black pepper extract (piperine, 5–10 mg) to enhance bioavailability by up to 2,000%
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use (6+ months in clinical trials); effects may accumulate over 4–8 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy at food levels; avoid therapeutic doses if pregnant. Caution in bleeding disorders or prior to surgery.

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal upset (nausea, diarrhea) at higher doses
contraindications: Active bleeding or bleeding disorders (may have mild antiplatelet effects)
High-dose use in pregnancy (food amounts are safe)

medications: Anticoagulants (warfarin, dabigatran): potential additive antiplatelet/anticoagulant effect; monitor
NSAIDs: potential additive gastrointestinal irritation risk
Diabetes medications: may enhance glucose-lowering effect; monitor blood sugar
Medications metabolized by CYP3A4 or CYP2C9: potential minor interaction via enzyme inhibition
supplements: Ginger: may have additive anti-inflammatory effects (generally favorable)
Fish oil/omega-3: potential mild additive antiplatelet effect

name: L-Tyrosine (Performance)
summary: Dopamine precursor for focus and stress resilience during exercise.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for stress and cognitive performance

dosage.special_populations: Demanding training, sleep deprivation

name: Tyrosol
summary: Tyrosol is a simple polyphenol found in Rhodiola rosea, red wine, and olives that acts as a free-radical scavenger. Human evidence for isolated tyrosol is extremely limited; benefits are largely inferred from Rhodiola extract research.
evidence_notes: Tyrosol has strong in vitro antioxidant properties but virtually no rigorous human clinical trials as an isolated compound. Data primarily comes from cell studies and animal models.

dosage.typical_range: Unknown (typically dosed as component of Rhodiola extracts; estimated 10-50 mg daily)
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: No established optimal duration for isolated tyrosol
dosage.special_populations: Insufficient safety data in vulnerable populations

common_side_effects: No reported adverse effects in human studies

name: UC-II (Undenatured Type II Collagen)
summary: Specialized collagen that works through immune modulation rather than building blocks. Low dose, high effect.
evidence_notes: Unique mechanism, good evidence

dosage.special_populations: OA, joint support

citations.title: UC-II collagen for joint health
citations.finding: 40mg UC-II improves joint comfort and flexibility

citations.title: Undenatured type II collagen
citations.finding: UC-II more effective than glucosamine/chondroitin combination

name: Undecylenic Acid
summary: Natural antifungal fatty acid derived from castor oil. Used to support healthy gut flora and combat candida overgrowth.
evidence_notes: Traditional use with limited clinical trials

dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for adults

practical_notes: Take with meals to reduce GI upset

name: Unifiram
summary: Sister compound to sunifiram. Even more potent AMPA modulator with minimal research.
evidence_notes: Minimal human research

dosage.special_populations: Experimental only

name: Unsaturated Fat
summary: Unsaturated fats are dietary lipids with one or more double bonds in their carbon chain, including monounsaturated (MUFA) and polyunsaturated (PUFA) varieties. Strong evidence supports their role in cardiovascular health, inflammation management, and cognitive function when consumed as part of a balanced diet.
mechanism_of_action: Improves endothelial function and arterial flexibility
Reduces systemic inflammation through omega-3 and omega-6 metabolism
Enhances absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) and lipophilic compounds like CoQ10
Supports neuronal membrane integrity and synaptic function
evidence_notes: Unsaturated fats have extensive RCT and epidemiological evidence supporting cardiovascular and cognitive benefits. As a carrier/solubilizer in CoQsol-CF, unsaturated fats significantly enhance CoQ10 bioavailability (established through multiple bioavailability studies).

dosage.typical_range: 20-35% of total daily calories (~40-75 g daily for 2000 kcal diet)
dosage.timing: With meals containing fat-soluble compounds (e.g., CoQ10)
dosage.with_food: Recommended with food; enhances absorption of fat-soluble vitamins and lipophilic supplements
dosage.duration_notes: Chronic consumption recommended for cardiovascular and cognitive benefits; benefits accumulate over weeks to months
dosage.special_populations: Higher intake may be beneficial for those with low HDL or elevated inflammatory markers; moderation advised for calorie-restricted diets

contraindications: No absolute contraindications; individuals with fat malabsorption disorders may have reduced tolerance

medications: May enhance absorption of fat-soluble medications (statins, warfarin, beta-blockers may have altered kinetics)
supplements: Synergistic with fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)
May improve absorption of other lipophilic compounds

name: Uridine Monophosphate
summary: Nucleotide supporting dopamine receptor density and synaptic plasticity. Key component of brain health stacks.
evidence_notes: Supports dopamine receptor expression

dosage.special_populations: Dopamine support, stimulant recovery

practical_notes: Best combined with DHA and choline

name: Uridine Monophosphate Disodium Salt
summary: Uridine monophosphate disodium is a nucleotide precursor form of uridine designed for enhanced bioavailability and absorption. It serves as a substrate for RNA synthesis and may support cognitive function through phosphatidylcholine production.
mechanism_of_action: Substrate for phosphatidylcholine synthesis via the Kennedy pathway
Potential support for neuronal membrane integrity and synaptic function
evidence_notes: Uridine has preliminary evidence for cognitive support and brain health, primarily from animal and in vitro studies. Human clinical evidence remains limited. The disodium salt form is a bioavailability enhancement strategy but lacks dedicated human efficacy trials.

dosage.timing: Can be taken anytime; often with meals for optimal absorption
dosage.with_food: May enhance absorption when taken with food containing fats
dosage.duration_notes: Typical supplementation is ongoing; no standard duration limits established
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy, lactation, and pediatric populations not well characterized

contraindications: Individuals with gout or elevated uric acid should consult a healthcare provider, as nucleotides may increase purine metabolism

medications: Antiviral medications (e.g., antiretrovirals): theoretical interaction due to nucleotide metabolism; consult healthcare provider
supplements: May be used synergistically with other cognitive support supplements (e.g., choline, omega-3 fatty acids)

name: Uridine + DHA + Choline Stack
summary: Mr. Happy Stack for dopamine receptor support and brain health. Long-term brain building.
evidence_notes: Dopamine and membrane support

dosage.special_populations: Mood, dopamine support

name: Urolithin A
summary: Gut-derived metabolite that activates mitophagy, clearing damaged mitochondria. Most people don't produce it efficiently, making supplementation valuable.
evidence_notes: Strong clinical evidence for mitochondrial and muscle benefits

dosage.duration_notes: Safe long-term, benefits build over weeks
dosage.special_populations: Age-related muscle decline, mitochondrial support

practical_notes: Most people don't efficiently convert precursors

name: Valerian
summary: Valerian root is a traditional herbal remedy with moderate clinical evidence for improving sleep quality and reducing time to sleep onset. It contains multiple bioactive compounds including valerenic acid and appears to work through GABAergic and serotonergic pathways.
evidence_notes: Traditional sleep herb - variable response

dosage.duration_notes: May take 2-4 weeks for full effect
dosage.special_populations: Sleep difficulty, mild anxiety

name: Vanadium
summary: Vanadium is a trace mineral that may support insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism, though human evidence remains preliminary. It is often included in multivitamins at very low doses with limited clinical validation in humans.
mechanism_of_action: May enhance insulin-stimulated glucose uptake in muscle and adipose tissue
Potential tyrosine kinase inhibition affecting insulin signaling pathways
evidence_notes: Most human evidence comes from small, short-term studies with mixed results. Animal studies show promise for glucose control, but clinical efficacy in humans at typical supplemental doses is not well-established. Lacks large-scale RCT support in humans.

dosage.typical_range: 10-30 mcg daily (multivitamin form); research doses 25-150 mg daily
dosage.with_food: Recommended—may enhance absorption and reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term safety and efficacy beyond 8-12 weeks not well-established
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should avoid; diabetics should consult physician before supplementing due to potential additive insulin effects

contraindications: Diabetes (risk of hypoglycemia if combined with medications)
Thyroid disorders (vanadium may affect thyroid function)

medications: Insulin and antidiabetic drugs (potential additive glucose-lowering effect)
Thyroid medications (may reduce absorption or interfere)
supplements: Other glucose-metabolism support supplements may have additive effects

name: VasoDrive-AP / VASO6
summary: Peptides derived from casein that inhibit ACE. Enhances nitric oxide and blood flow.
evidence_notes: Emerging evidence for NO and blood pressure

dosage.special_populations: Pump, blood pressure support

practical_notes: Don't use with ACE inhibitor drugs

name: Vigna radiata Extract (Seed Coat)
summary: A seed coat extract rich in flavonoids (vitexin and bovitexin) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Clinical evidence is very limited.
mechanism_of_action: Vitexin and bovitexin are C-glycoside flavonoids with ROS scavenging activity
evidence_notes: Very limited human clinical data; primarily cell culture and animal studies. Traditional food use is long-established, but supplement efficacy in humans is unproven.

dosage.typical_range: 200-500 mg daily (estimated based on flavonoid content)
dosage.duration_notes: Optimal duration unknown; assume minimum 4-8 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe (GRAS); insufficient safety data for pregnancy/nursing

medications: Anticoagulants (theoretical risk from flavonoid content; monitor)

name: Vinpocetine
summary: Semi-synthetic compound from periwinkle enhancing cerebral blood flow and neuroprotection.
evidence_notes: Enhances brain circulation

dosage.special_populations: Cognitive decline, cerebrovascular

name: Vitamin A
summary: A fat-soluble vitamin essential for vision, immune function, and skin health that the body converts from beta-carotene as needed. Beta-carotene is a plant-derived precursor that acts as an antioxidant and is safer at high doses than preformed retinol.
evidence_notes: Essential vitamin with well-established functions

dosage.special_populations: Caution in pregnancy

name: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)
summary: Thiamine is an essential B-vitamin that functions as a coenzyme in carbohydrate and amino acid metabolism. Thiamine HCl is a stable, highly bioavailable form.
primary_uses: Energy metabolism (carbohydrate and branched-chain amino acid oxidation)
Nervous system function and myelin formation
evidence_notes: Essential B vitamin

dosage.special_populations: Alcoholics at high risk of deficiency

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Likely safe at normal doses - consult provider

name: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)
summary: An essential water-soluble vitamin required for red blood cell formation, neurological function, and DNA synthesis. Cyanocobalamin is a stable synthetic form commonly used in supplements.
mechanism_of_action: Essential for myelin synthesis and nerve function
evidence_notes: Well-established essential nutrient with strong evidence for deficiency treatment

forms.notes: Active form, best for general use

dosage.special_populations: Vegans, elderly, those on metformin or PPIs need more

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Likely safe at normal doses - consult provider

practical_notes: Sublingual form bypasses digestive absorption issues
Test levels if fatigued or over 50

name: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)
summary: Essential B vitamin involved in energy production, antioxidant function, and metabolism of other B vitamins. The active form (R5P) is preferred.
evidence_notes: Well-established essential nutrient, good evidence for migraine prevention
mechanism_of_action: Precursor to FAD and FMN coenzymes
Required for folate and B6 activation

dosage.special_populations: Migraine sufferers may need higher doses

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Likely safe at normal doses - consult provider

practical_notes: Yellow urine is normal and harmless

name: Vitamin B3 (Niacin)
summary: Niacinamide is an essential B vitamin and form of vitamin B3 that supports energy metabolism, DNA repair, and cellular function. It is well-established as an effective supplement for preventing deficiency and has demonstrated benefits for skin health and niacin flush-free supplementation.
mechanism_of_action: Precursor for NAD+ and NADP+ cofactors essential for oxidative metabolism
Modulates lipid synthesis and cholesterol metabolism at pharmacological doses
evidence_notes: Vitamin B3 is one of the most well-researched vitamins with extensive RCT evidence for deficiency prevention, energy support, and cardiovascular effects. Strong evidence exists for pharmacological niacin use in lipid management; supplemental doses for general health have robust mechanistic and observational support.

dosage.typical_range: 14-16 mg daily (RDA); 500-2000 mg daily for supplementation
dosage.timing: With meals to reduce GI side effects and flushing
dosage.with_food: Strongly recommended, especially for niacin form, to minimize flushing and nausea
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use at RDA levels; extended use above 35 mg daily requires medical supervision
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/lactating women: 18 mg daily; children: 2-16 mg daily (age-dependent); hepatic impairment: use with caution

upper_limit.label: 35 mg niacin
upper_limit.notes: UL for the flushing form; nicotinamide is safer at higher doses

common_side_effects: Niacin flush (flushed skin, tingling, heat sensation) — transient and harmless with niacin but not niacinamide
contraindications: Gout or hyperuricemia (niacin can elevate uric acid)

medications: Statins: additive lipid-lowering effects; monitor combined use
Diabetes medications: niacin may affect glucose control
Aspirin: may reduce niacin flush if taken beforehand
Blood pressure medications: potential additive vasodilatory effects
supplements: Other B vitamins: synergistic support for energy metabolism

name: Vitamin B6
summary: An essential B-vitamin cofactor for neurotransmitter synthesis, homocysteine metabolism, and sleep-wake regulation. Pyridoxal-5-phosphate is the active, bioavailable form that directly participates in enzymatic reactions.
evidence_notes: Essential vitamin with well-established functions

dosage.special_populations: Higher needs in pregnancy

upper_limit.label: 100 mg
upper_limit.notes: Above this risks reversible peripheral neuropathy

serious_warnings: Neuropathy at very high doses (>200mg long-term)

practical_notes: Combine with ginger for better effect

name: Vitamin C
summary: Vitamin C is an essential water-soluble antioxidant required for collagen synthesis, immune function, and iron absorption. Calcium ascorbate is a buffered form that reduces gastric irritation.
evidence_notes: Essential vitamin with extensive research

dosage.special_populations: Higher needs when sick

moderate_dose.label: 200-500 mg
moderate_dose.notes: Saturates plasma at ~200 mg per dose

upper_limit.label: 2,000 mg
upper_limit.notes: GI upset above this; liposomal tolerates more

serious_warnings: Kidney stones risk at very high doses
safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Likely safe at normal doses - consult provider

practical_notes: Higher doses needed for cortisol effects
Must have for collagen to work
Liposomal for high doses without GI issues

name: Vitamin D
summary: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble hormone and micronutrient essential for calcium absorption, bone health, immune function, and cellular regulation. Most people have insufficient levels and may benefit from supplementation, particularly those with limited sun exposure.
mechanism_of_action: Acts as a hormone regulating calcium and phosphate absorption
Activates vitamin D receptors throughout the body
Modulates immune cell differentiation and function
evidence_notes: Strong RCT evidence supports vitamin D for bone health, immune function, and reducing deficiency. Extensive observational data supports broader health benefits. Recommended by major health organizations.

dosage.typical_range: 400-4,000 IU daily for maintenance; 1,000-4,000 IU common in supplements
dosage.timing: Best absorbed with fat-containing meal; timing otherwise flexible
dosage.with_food: Recommend with dietary fat for optimal absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; fat-soluble so accumulates in body—monitor levels if taking high doses
dosage.special_populations: Older adults (50+) may need higher amounts (600-800 IU minimum); dark-skinned individuals in northern climates need more; pregnant/nursing women should discuss dosing with healthcare provider

common_side_effects: Excessive intake (>10,000 IU chronically) may cause hypercalcemia, nausea, weakness
contraindications: Hypercalcemia or conditions causing elevated calcium
Sarcoidosis and certain granulomatous diseases (vitamin D increases calcium absorption)
Concurrent high-dose calcium supplementation (risk of toxicity)

medications: Anticonvulsants like phenytoin (increased vitamin D metabolism)
supplements: K2 (complementary for bone and cardiovascular health)

practical_notes: Consider lichen-derived D3 as vegan alternative
Produces natural D plus other compounds

name: Vitamin E
summary: A fat-soluble antioxidant vitamin that protects cells from free radical damage and supports immune and cardiovascular health. The dl-form (synthetic) is bioequivalent to natural alpha-tocopherol.
evidence_notes: Essential antioxidant vitamin

dosage.special_populations: Avoid high doses before surgery

serious_warnings: May increase bleeding risk at high doses

practical_notes: Natural d-form twice as potent as synthetic

name: Vitamin K
summary: A fat-soluble vitamin essential for blood clotting, bone metabolism, and cardiovascular health. Phytonadione (K1) is the plant-derived form found in leafy greens and the primary supplemental form.
mechanism_of_action: Essential cofactor for clotting factors II, VII, IX, X
Converts to MK-4 in some tissues
evidence_notes: Essential for coagulation

dosage.special_populations: Those on warfarin need consistent intake

serious_warnings: Warfarin users must consult physician before use
Keep vitamin K intake consistent if on blood thinners

drug_interactions.effect: Vitamin K REVERSES warfarin effect - can cause clotting
drug_interactions.mechanism: Vitamin K is the antidote to warfarin; restores clotting factors
Same as warfarin - vitamin K antagonism

drug_interactions.effect: Minimal interaction - does not depend on vitamin K
drug_interactions.mechanism: Direct factor Xa inhibitor - not vitamin K dependent

drug_interactions.effect: Antibiotics kill gut bacteria that produce vitamin K2; may need more K
Can cause vitamin K deficiency; bleeding risk

drug_interactions.mechanism: Blocks fat absorption including fat-soluble vitamins

drug_interactions.effect: High vitamin E may interfere with vitamin K function
drug_interactions.mechanism: Competitive inhibition of vitamin K-dependent clotting

drug_interactions.effect: May interfere with vitamin K absorption

safety.interaction_summary: Vitamin K is CRITICAL for warfarin users. Vitamin K REVERSES warfarin's anticoagulant effect - this can cause dangerous clotting. Consistent vitamin K intake is key for warfarin users (don't suddenly increase or decrease).
safety.interaction_warning: WARFARIN USERS: Do NOT take vitamin K supplements without physician guidance. Sudden changes in vitamin K intake can destabilize INR.
safety.warfarin_note: If on warfarin: Keep dietary vitamin K intake CONSISTENT (don't avoid, but don't vary wildly). Supplements require INR monitoring.

name: Vitamin K2
summary: Vitamin K2 (MK-7) is a long-chain menaquinone that activates bone and vascular proteins involved in calcium regulation, supporting bone mineralization and cardiovascular health. MK-7 from plant sources like chickpea offers improved bioavailability and longer tissue half-life compared to K1.
mechanism_of_action: Directs calcium to bone, away from soft tissues
evidence_notes: Strong RCT and observational evidence, particularly for bone density and fracture risk reduction; good evidence for vascular calcification prevention. MK-7 form shows superior bioavailability and half-life (2.5 days) compared to MK-4 or vitamin K1. EPIC-Norfolk and other large cohort studies support cardiovascular benefits.

dosage.with_food: Essential—fat-soluble vitamin; absorption enhanced with dietary lipids
dosage.duration_notes: Bone mineralization benefits may require 6–12 months of consistent use; vascular benefits similarly require long-term intake
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant and breastfeeding women should maintain adequate K2 intake (safe at typical supplemental doses). Caution in those on warfarin or other vitamin K antagonists—consult physician before supplementing.

contraindications: Warfarin and other vitamin K antagonists—high-dose supplementation may reduce drug efficacy; consistent intake is preferred over variable intake

medications: Warfarin and other coumarin anticoagulants—may reduce anticoagulant effect; consistency is key
Some antibiotics (broad-spectrum) may reduce K2 production by gut bacteria
supplements: Synergistic with vitamin D and calcium for bone health
May complement magnesium and boron for optimal bone metabolism

name: Vitex (Chasteberry)
summary: Well-researched herb for women's hormonal balance, PMS, and cycle regulation.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for PMS and cycle regulation
mechanism_of_action: Acts on pituitary to support progesterone

dosage.typical_range: 20-40mg standardized extract or 160-240mg dried berry equivalent
dosage.special_populations: Women with PMS, irregular cycles, luteal phase issues

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Avoid in pregnancy, traditional galactagogue but use cautiously

practical_notes: May worsen symptoms initially before improving

name: Vitex (Agnus Castus) Extract
summary: Vitex is a traditional herbal extract used to support hormonal balance, particularly for menstrual cycle regulation and premenstrual symptoms in women. Evidence for efficacy is moderate, with some clinical studies supporting symptom relief but limited mechanistic understanding.
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs demonstrate efficacy for PMS symptoms; however, mechanism of action remains incompletely understood and some studies are small or have methodological limitations.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: May require 2-3 months of consistent use to observe effects on menstrual cycle
dosage.special_populations: Not recommended during pregnancy; use with caution while breastfeeding

name: Vitex (PMS)
summary: Well-researched herb for PMS symptoms, supporting progesterone and reducing prolactin.
evidence_notes: Strong evidence for PMS symptom reduction

dosage.special_populations: PMS, luteal phase symptoms, breast tenderness

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Avoid in pregnancy, galactagogue for breastfeeding

name: Vitexin + Isovitexin Isoflavones
summary: Vitexin and isovitexin are flavonoid compounds (specifically C-glycosyl flavones) with antioxidant properties found in various plants. In vitro and animal evidence suggests potential neuroprotective and anti-inflammatory effects, though human clinical data is limited.
evidence_notes: Primary evidence is from in vitro and animal models; human clinical trials are scarce or absent. Bioavailability and physiological relevance in humans unclear.

dosage.typical_range: Typically 50-500 mg daily (as part of combination products)
dosage.duration_notes: No established optimal duration; generally safe for prolonged use
dosage.special_populations: Insufficient data in pregnancy/lactation; use cautiously

name: Wasabi Extract
summary: Extract from Japanese wasabi root containing isothiocyanates with antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and gut microbiome modulating properties.
evidence_notes: Emerging research on gut microbiome effects

dosage.special_populations: Those with digestive sensitivities start low

common_side_effects: Mild GI upset at high doses
serious_warnings: May irritate stomach at very high doses

medications: May enhance effects of blood thinners

practical_notes: Often combined with other prebiotic compounds

name: Watercress Extract (Nasturtium officinale)
summary: Watercress is a nutrient-dense cruciferous vegetable containing glucosinolates, vitamins, and minerals with preliminary evidence for antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Most human studies involve whole watercress consumption rather than isolated extracts, limiting conclusions about extract efficacy.
mechanism_of_action: Glucosinolate metabolism to isothiocyanates with potential phase II enzyme induction
Potential inhibition of oxidative stress pathways
evidence_notes: Limited clinical evidence specific to watercress extract. Most research uses whole watercress consumption showing modest benefits for oxidative stress and inflammation. Extract form lacks robust RCT data. Traditional use is well-established but modern extract research is preliminary.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; taking with meals may improve absorption of fat-soluble compounds
dosage.duration_notes: No established optimal duration; typically used long-term for nutritional support
dosage.special_populations: Generally well-tolerated; avoid very high intakes in pregnancy due to limited safety data

common_side_effects: Possible mild gastrointestinal upset at high doses
Potential skin irritation with topical contact in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Individuals taking warfarin or other vitamin K–dependent anticoagulants (watercress is high in vitamin K)
Known allergy to Brassicaceae family plants
Pregnant or nursing individuals should use cautiously due to limited safety data

medications: Anticoagulants (warfarin, other vitamin K antagonists) — watercress is high in vitamin K and may reduce efficacy
supplements: Other high-vitamin K sources may have additive effects with anticoagulants

name: Watermelon Juice Powder
summary: Watermelon juice powder is a concentrated form of watermelon fruit containing naturally occurring citrulline, lycopene, and other phytonutrients. Limited clinical evidence supports its use for cardiovascular and exercise performance support, primarily via citrulline content.
mechanism_of_action: Citrulline conversion to arginine and nitric oxide production
evidence_notes: While watermelon contains citrulline (which has B-grade evidence), whole watermelon juice powder studies are limited and results are mixed. Most evidence comes from concentrated citrulline isolates rather than whole powder form.

dosage.typical_range: 1-2 tablespoons (5-10 g) daily mixed with liquid
dosage.timing: Pre-workout (1-2 hours before exercise) or daily
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; mixing with liquid recommended
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent daily use recommended for best results; effects may take 2-4 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for most adults; individuals with FODMAP sensitivities should note watermelon is low-FODMAP

common_side_effects: Minimal; well-tolerated as a whole food
Possible mild gastrointestinal effects in sensitive individuals

medications: Potential additive effects with blood pressure medications (theoretical, based on nitric oxide activity)
supplements: May have additive effects with other citrulline or arginine-containing supplements

name: Watermelon Powder
summary: Watermelon is a natural source of L-citrulline, supporting nitric oxide production, blood flow, and exercise performance.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for citrulline content and cardiovascular benefits

dosage.special_populations: Athletes, cardiovascular support

medications: May enhance blood pressure medication effects

name: Wheatgrass Powder
summary: Wheatgrass is a nutrient-dense plant powder containing chlorophyll, vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants, though human clinical evidence for specific health benefits remains limited. Traditional use focuses on general wellness and detoxification, but most claims lack robust scientific support.
mechanism_of_action: Chlorophyll and phytochemical content may provide antioxidant effects
Contains trace minerals and B vitamins that support metabolism
Potential anti-inflammatory compounds from plant constituents
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trials; most evidence is in vitro or anecdotal. A few small studies suggest antioxidant potential, but no strong RCT evidence for specific health claims in humans. Safety profile is generally favorable.

dosage.typical_range: 1-2 teaspoons (3-6 grams) daily, or as directed on label
dosage.timing: Any time of day; often mixed into water, juice, or smoothies
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; some prefer with meals to minimize potential nausea
dosage.duration_notes: Can be used daily as a nutritional supplement; no established duration limits
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing individuals should consult a healthcare provider; those with celiac disease should verify gluten-free certification

contraindications: Wheat allergy or celiac disease (unless certified gluten-free; young shoots contain minimal gluten but cross-contamination possible)
Pregnancy and nursing (limited safety data; consult healthcare provider)

medications: May theoretically interfere with anticoagulants (warfarin) due to vitamin K content; monitor if on blood thinners
supplements: No major known interactions with common supplements

name: Whey Protein
summary: Whey protein concentrate is a dairy-derived protein containing 70-80% protein by weight, along with lactose and fat, and is extensively researched for supporting muscle protein synthesis and recovery. Numerous high-quality RCTs demonstrate efficacy for increasing lean muscle mass when combined with resistance training.
evidence_notes: Extensive research supporting muscle building

dosage.typical_range: 20-40g per serving, as needed for protein goals
dosage.special_populations: Athletes, those needing more protein

name: Whey Protein Isolate
summary: Whey protein isolate is a highly refined form of whey protein containing 90%+ protein by weight with minimal lactose and fat, making it an effective source of complete amino acids for muscle protein synthesis. Extensive research supports its efficacy for muscle growth and recovery when combined with resistance training.
mechanism_of_action: Provides all essential amino acids, particularly leucine, to stimulate mTOR pathway and muscle protein synthesis
Rapid absorption delivers amino acids to muscle tissue
Supports nitrogen balance and lean body mass maintenance
evidence_notes: Whey protein isolate has strong RCT evidence demonstrating efficacy for muscle hypertrophy and recovery when combined with resistance training. Hundreds of well-designed studies confirm its bioavailability and amino acid composition.

dosage.timing: Ideally within 1-2 hours post-workout; can be taken any time to increase daily protein intake
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; absorption is not significantly affected
dosage.special_populations: Individuals with lactose intolerance generally tolerate whey isolate well due to minimal residual lactose; those with whey allergy should avoid

common_side_effects: Minor bloating or digestive discomfort in sensitive individuals (rare with isolate)

supplements: No contraindicated interactions; commonly stacked with creatine and carbohydrates for enhanced recovery

name: White Jelly Fungus
summary: White Jelly Fungus (Tremella fuciformis) is a gelatinous mushroom used in traditional Asian cuisine and medicine for skin hydration and immune support. Limited but promising preliminary research suggests polysaccharide benefits.
evidence_notes: Several in vitro and animal studies suggest immunomodulatory and skin-health benefits; human clinical trials are limited but emerging. Some evidence for skin hydration support.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Skin benefits may require 8-12 weeks of consistent use
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in pregnancy (traditional food use); caution in immunocompromised individuals

common_side_effects: Mild GI upset in sensitive individuals

name: White Kidney Bean Extract (Phaseolus vulgaris)
summary: White kidney bean extract contains alpha-amylase inhibitors that may reduce carbohydrate digestion and absorption. Research shows modest effects on postprandial blood glucose and weight management, though clinical significance remains limited.
mechanism_of_action: Delays carbohydrate absorption in small intestine
evidence_notes: Multiple RCTs show modest benefits for glucose control and modest weight loss (~2-3 lbs over 8-12 weeks), but effect sizes are small and heterogeneous. Some studies funded by ingredient suppliers. Not suitable for A-B grade due to modest clinical magnitude and variable study quality.

dosage.with_food: Must be taken with food containing carbohydrates to be effective
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used daily for 8–12 weeks in clinical trials; can be used long-term
dosage.special_populations: Generally safe in adults; limited data in pregnancy/lactation and pediatric populations

medications: May reduce absorption of some oral medications taken simultaneously
Potential interaction with diabetes medications (additive glucose-lowering effects)
supplements: Other alpha-amylase inhibitors may have additive effects

name: White Mulberry Extract (Morus alba)
summary: White mulberry leaf extract contains polyphenols and alkaloids that may help moderate blood sugar levels and support cardiometabolic health. Most evidence comes from in vitro and animal studies, with limited human clinical data.
evidence_notes: Several human studies show modest effects on postprandial glucose, but sample sizes are generally small and study quality is mixed. Stronger evidence exists for related compounds like mulberry leaf tea in Asian populations. More rigorous RCTs needed.

dosage.timing: With meals, particularly before carbohydrate-heavy meals
dosage.with_food: Recommended; may be taken with food to enhance effect on glucose
dosage.duration_notes: Effects on glucose are typically assessed within 2–4 weeks; long-term safety data limited beyond 12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; individuals on diabetes medications should monitor glucose closely

contraindications: Concurrent use with diabetes medications without medical supervision (hypoglycemia risk)

medications: Diabetes medications (metformin, sulfonylureas, insulin) — potential additive hypoglycemic effect
ACE inhibitors and antihypertensives — possible additive effect on blood pressure
supplements: Other glucose-lowering herbs (cinnamon, fenugreek) — potential cumulative effect

name: White Willow Bark
summary: White willow bark contains salicin, a naturally occurring compound structurally similar to aspirin, traditionally used for pain and inflammation management. Moderate evidence supports its use for mild-to-moderate joint and musculoskeletal pain, though effects are generally modest.
evidence_notes: Natural aspirin precursor

dosage.special_populations: Joint/muscle pain, natural preference

practical_notes: Gentler than aspirin but similar cautions

name: Wild Blueberry Complex
aliases: Wild Blueberry Complex (fruit, leaf, and stem)
summary: Concentrated extract from wild blueberries (Vaccinium angustifolium) rich in anthocyanins and polyphenols. Supports cognitive function, cardiovascular health, and provides potent antioxidant protection.
evidence_notes: Good evidence for anthocyanin benefits, cognitive and cardiovascular support

dosage.special_populations: Generally safe for all

practical_notes: Wild blueberries have 2x anthocyanins vs cultivated
Whole fruit/leaf/stem extracts capture full spectrum
Wild varieties more potent than cultivated

name: Wild Blueberry Fruit Juice Powder
summary: Wild blueberry juice powder is concentrated from wild blueberry (Vaccinium angustifolium) fruit and contains high levels of anthocyanins and other polyphenols with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. It is commonly used to support cognitive function, cardiovascular health, and antioxidant status.
evidence_notes: Wild blueberry has good evidence in human trials for antioxidant status, cognitive outcomes, and vascular function. The juice powder form concentrates anthocyanins, though processing can affect phytochemical profile.

dosage.with_food: May enhance absorption of fat-soluble polyphenols
dosage.special_populations: Diabetes: monitor if sweetened formulation used; pregnancy/nursing: likely safe in food amounts

common_side_effects: Mild digestive upset in sensitive individuals

medications: Potential additive effects with anticoagulants (warfarin) at very high doses—monitor if using therapeutic amounts

name: Wild Cod Liver Oil
summary: A nutrient-dense oil from wild Atlantic cod (Gadus morhua) livers, naturally rich in EPA, DHA, vitamin A, and vitamin D3. Traditionally used for joint health and immune support.
mechanism_of_action: provides preformed EPA/DHA reducing need for conversion
reduces systemic inflammation through omega-3 content
evidence_notes: Cod liver oil has good clinical evidence for omega-3 and vitamin benefits, though newer purified fish oils have more extensive RCT data. Traditional use and composition well-documented.

dosage.typical_range: 1-3 grams daily of cod liver oil (providing 180-540 mg EPA and 120-360 mg DHA approximately)
dosage.timing: with meals containing additional fat for optimal absorption
dosage.with_food: recommended to reduce GI upset and improve absorption
dosage.duration_notes: safe for long-term continuous use; fat-soluble vitamins accumulate over time
dosage.special_populations: pregnant women should verify vitamin A content does not exceed safe limits; individuals on anticoagulants should monitor intake

medications: warfarin/anticoagulants (additive antiplatelet effect at high doses)
supplements: high-dose vitamin A from other sources (additive toxicity risk)

name: Wild Jujube Extract
summary: Wild jujube (Ziziphus jujuba) fruit extract is a traditional Chinese medicine ingredient studied for potential sleep and anxiety-related effects, though robust human clinical evidence remains limited.
