The fruit contains polysaccharides, flavonoids, and other phytochemicals that may support relaxation and sleep quality.
mechanism_of_action: Contains polysaccharides and flavonoids that may enhance GABA signaling
Potential anxiolytic activity via saponins and alkaloid-like compounds
Traditional use suggests sedative properties in CNS function
evidence_notes: While jujube has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries and some preliminary in vitro and animal studies suggest sleep-supporting mechanisms, human clinical trials are limited and often small. Most evidence is observational or from traditional use rather than rigorous RCTs.

dosage.typical_range: 1–3 g daily (extract powder), or 5–15 g dried fruit equivalent
dosage.timing: Evening or 30–60 minutes before bedtime for sleep support
dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests ongoing use; optimal duration for benefits unclear
dosage.special_populations: Limited safety data in pregnancy/lactation; use under practitioner guidance

common_side_effects: Rare: mild GI upset, drowsiness (intended effect)
contraindications: May interact with sedating medications or supplements

medications: Benzodiazepines and other CNS depressants (potential additive sedation)
supplements: Other sleep-support herbs (valerian, passionflower, magnesium) — potential additive effects

name: Wild Yam
summary: Wild yam root extract is traditionally used in herbal medicine for hormonal balance and menopausal symptom support, though clinical evidence remains limited. The extract contains diosgenin and other steroidal saponins, but lacks robust RCT evidence confirming efficacy in humans.
mechanism_of_action: Body CANNOT convert diosgenin to progesterone (contrary to marketing)
evidence_notes: Limited human clinical trial data. Most evidence is traditional use, in vitro studies, or animal models. Diosgenin research is preliminary; human bioavailability and efficacy for hormone support lacks rigorous RCT validation.

dosage.special_populations: Those understanding its limitations

serious_warnings: Does NOT convert to progesterone in body

name: Withania somnifera Extract (Whole Plant)
summary: Withania somnifera (ashwagandha) is a traditional Ayurvedic herb standardized to withanolides, the active compounds responsible for its adaptogenic effects. Multiple clinical trials support its use for stress reduction, cortisol modulation, and anxiety management.
mechanism_of_action: Withanolides modulate GABA and serotonin signaling
evidence_notes: Multiple randomized controlled trials demonstrate efficacy for anxiety, stress, and cortisol reduction. Well-established traditional use with consistent clinical evidence. 9% withanolide standardization is a clinically relevant dose.

dosage.timing: Once or twice daily with meals
dosage.with_food: With food may improve absorption and reduce gastrointestinal upset
dosage.duration_notes: Consistent use for 4-8 weeks recommended for full adaptogenic benefits; long-term use is generally safe
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; those on sedatives may experience additive effects

common_side_effects: Stomach upset if taken on empty stomach
contraindications: Nightshade sensitivity (Withania is in Solanaceae family)
Thyroid disorders (may have minor thyroid effects)

medications: Benzodiazepines and CNS depressants (additive sedative effects)
supplements: Sedating herbs like valerian or passionflower (additive effects)

name: Wolfberry Extract (Lycium barbarum)
summary: Wolfberry (goji berry) is a traditional Chinese fruit rich in polysaccharides, carotenoids, and antioxidants with preliminary evidence for immune support and eye health. Most human studies are small or conducted in Asian populations, limiting generalizability of findings.
mechanism_of_action: Lutein and zeaxanthin content for ocular protection
evidence_notes: Moderate preliminary evidence from small human trials and in vitro studies. Polysaccharide and carotenoid content is well-documented, but clinical efficacy in Western populations remains limited. Most robust data from traditional use and animal models; human RCTs are sparse and often published in regional journals.

dosage.typical_range: 1–15 g daily of whole fruit or dried equivalent; 300–500 mg of concentrated extract
dosage.timing: With meals to optimize absorption of fat-soluble carotenoids
dosage.with_food: Recommended; fat-soluble nutrient content benefits from dietary fat
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests ongoing supplementation; typical studies range 4–12 weeks
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe in food form; pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider before supplemental use

common_side_effects: Mild digestive upset in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Potential interaction with blood-thinning medications (theoretical; use with caution)
May cause diarrhea or stomach upset at very high doses
Caution in individuals with nightshade sensitivity

medications: Warfarin and anticoagulants (theoretical concern; monitor if used concurrently)
Diabetes medications (may potentiate glucose-lowering effects—monitor blood sugar)
supplements: Other antioxidant-rich herbs (additive effect; generally safe but may require dose adjustment)

name: Wolfiporia extensa Extract (Sclerotium)
summary: Wolfiporia extensa (formerly Poria cocos) is a fungal sclerotium used in traditional Chinese medicine with preliminary evidence for anxiolytic and cognitive support properties. Limited human clinical trials exist; most evidence derives from in vitro and animal studies.
evidence_notes: Traditional use and preliminary in vitro/animal data support cognitive and anxiolytic claims, but human RCT evidence is limited and inconsistent. Grade C reflects moderate/preliminary evidence status without robust clinical validation.

dosage.timing: With meals; divided doses commonly used
dosage.with_food: May be taken with or without food; with food may reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use supports long-term consumption; optimal duration for cognitive effects unclear
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/nursing women should consult healthcare provider; safety in children not established

name: Wolfporia extensa Extract (Sclerotium)
summary: Wolfporia extensa (formerly classified as Poria cocos) is a medicinal fungus used in traditional Chinese medicine primarily for cognitive and sleep support. Limited human clinical evidence exists; most research is preclinical or conducted in Asian populations.
mechanism_of_action: Polysaccharide and beta-glucan bioactive compounds may modulate immune function
Potential GABAergic activity may support relaxation and sleep
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects from triterpenes
evidence_notes: Traditional use in Chinese medicine is well-documented, but human clinical trials are limited and mostly conducted in Asia. Preclinical studies show promise for bioactive compounds, but evidence in Western populations remains preliminary. No strong RCT evidence in primary literature.

dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; often used in evening for sleep support
dosage.with_food: No specific requirement; can be taken with or without food
dosage.duration_notes: Traditional use suggests extended use (weeks to months) for best results
dosage.special_populations: Safety in pregnancy/lactation not established; use with caution or avoid

common_side_effects: Rare: allergic reactions in sensitive individuals
contraindications: Immunocompromised individuals should consult healthcare provider

medications: Potential interaction with sedative medications (additive effect)
supplements: Caution with other sleep-supporting herbs or sedative adaptogens

name: Women's Hormone Balance Stack
summary: Natural support for hormonal balance: Vitex, Maca, Magnesium, B6, DIM.
evidence_notes: Evidence-based natural hormone support combination

dosage.timing: Vitex AM empty stomach, others with food
dosage.special_populations: Women with PMS, irregular cycles, perimenopause

safety.pregnancy_breastfeeding: Avoid during pregnancy, some components ok nursing

name: XOS (Xylooligosaccharides)
summary: Emerging prebiotic with benefits at very low doses. Well-tolerated with minimal GI effects.
evidence_notes: Effective at low doses

dosage.special_populations: Those sensitive to other prebiotics

practical_notes: Works at lower doses than other prebiotics

name: Yeast Extract (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) Cell Wall with Beta-Glucan
summary: Yeast extract cell wall is a source of beta-glucans, polysaccharides that have demonstrated immune-modulating properties in research. The standardized 50% beta-glucan content provides a consistent dose of compounds studied for supporting immune function and inflammatory response.
mechanism_of_action: Beta-glucan binding to pattern recognition receptors (Dectin-1) on immune cells
Support of barrier function in gastrointestinal tract
evidence_notes: Beta-glucans from yeast have moderate-to-good clinical evidence for immune support, particularly for upper respiratory infection reduction and immune marker modulation. However, evidence is less robust than for other well-established immune ingredients. Standardization to 50% beta-glucan provides stronger evidence basis than non-standardized yeast extract.

dosage.timing: Can be taken with or between meals
dosage.with_food: No requirement; food may enhance absorption of some polysaccharides
dosage.duration_notes: Typically used on an ongoing basis; benefits may take 2-4 weeks to manifest
dosage.special_populations: Generally recognized as safe; may be limited in individuals with yeast sensitivities or active yeast infections, though cell wall extracts are generally well-tolerated

common_side_effects: Mild gastrointestinal discomfort in sensitive individuals
Rare allergic reactions in yeast-sensitive persons
contraindications: Immunocompromised individuals should consult healthcare provider before use

medications: May interact with immunosuppressive medications (consult provider)
supplements: Generally compatible with other immune support ingredients
May have additive effects with other beta-glucan sources

name: Yohimbine
summary: Alpha-2 antagonist for stubborn fat. Effective but significant side effects.
evidence_notes: Good evidence but narrow use case

dosage.special_populations: Advanced, stubborn fat areas

name: Zeaxanthin
summary: Partner carotenoid to lutein. Together they provide complete macular protection.
evidence_notes: Essential macular carotenoid

dosage.special_populations: AMD risk, screen users

citations.finding: Zeaxanthin with lutein protects macula

citations.finding: Lutein/zeaxanthin effective for AMD

name: Zeaxanthin from Marigold Flower Extract
summary: Zeaxanthin is a carotenoid antioxidant naturally found in marigold flowers, primarily studied for its potential role in supporting eye health and macular pigment density. Evidence supports its use alongside lutein for maintaining vision, particularly in protecting the macula from age-related damage.
mechanism_of_action: Accumulates in the macula and protects against blue light and oxidative stress
Acts as a carotenoid antioxidant, neutralizing free radicals
Supports macular pigment optical density for visual filtering
evidence_notes: Good evidence exists for zeaxanthin's role in eye health, particularly from the AREDS2 trial and observational studies showing association between dietary zeaxanthin intake and reduced AMD risk. Evidence is solid but less extensive than for lutein. Most benefits are observed when combined with lutein and other nutrients.

dosage.with_food: Recommended—fat-soluble carotenoid requiring dietary fat for absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term use (weeks to months) may be needed to build macular pigment
dosage.special_populations: Often combined with lutein in 1:2 to 1:1 ratios for synergistic eye protection

common_side_effects: Carotenemia (harmless yellowing of skin) at very high doses
contraindications: Caution in smokers—some data suggest high carotenoid intake may increase lung cancer risk in this population

medications: Orlistat and other fat-malabsorption drugs may reduce zeaxanthin absorption
supplements: Synergistic with lutein for eye health
Complementary with vitamin C, vitamin E, zinc, and omega-3 for comprehensive antioxidant support

name: Zeolite (Clinoptilolite)

dosage.special_populations: Those with heavy metal concerns

avoid_combining_with: Medications and supplements at same time

summary: Essential mineral critical for immune cell development and function. Can reduce duration of common cold when taken at onset.

name: Zinc (as Zinc Bisglycinate)
summary: Zinc is an essential mineral involved in immune function, protein synthesis, and wound healing. Bisglycinate chelation enhances bioavailability and reduces gastrointestinal irritation compared to zinc oxide or sulfate forms.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor for immune cell development and function
evidence_notes: Extensive RCT evidence supports zinc's role in immune function, wound healing, and overall health. Bisglycinate chelation shows superior absorption and tolerability in clinical comparisons.

dosage.with_food: Take with food to reduce nausea and enhance absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Ongoing supplementation for optimal immune and general health support
dosage.special_populations: RDA: 11 mg (adult males), 8 mg (adult females), 11-12 mg (pregnant/lactating), 2-11 mg (children). Upper limit: 40 mg/day to avoid copper antagonism.

common_side_effects: Nausea (reduced with chelated form and food)
Copper deficiency with excessive long-term use

name: Zinc (as Zinc Bisglycinate Chelate)
summary: Zinc is an essential trace mineral critical for immune function, protein synthesis, and DNA replication. Zinc bisglycinate chelate is a highly absorbable form bound to the amino acid glycine.
mechanism_of_action: component of immune cells and antibodies
evidence_notes: Zinc has strong RCT evidence for immune support and wound healing. Bisglycinate chelate form shows superior absorption compared to inorganic salts like oxide or sulfate.

dosage.timing: can be taken with or without food, though food may reduce absorption
dosage.with_food: absorption better on empty stomach, but food can reduce GI upset
dosage.duration_notes: ongoing for maintenance; higher doses for acute immune support should be limited to 2-3 weeks
dosage.special_populations: pregnant/nursing women: 11-13 mg daily; vegans/vegetarians may need higher intakes

contraindications: do not exceed 40 mg daily long-term without medical supervision

medications: copper depletion possible with long-term use above 25 mg daily
may interfere with certain antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, tetracyclines)
supplements: copper and iron (compete for absorption; timing important)

name: Zinc Gluconate
summary: Zinc gluconate is an organic salt form of zinc commonly used in supplements for immune support and general nutritional repletion. As a mineral cofactor essential for immune function, wound healing, and protein synthesis, zinc gluconate delivers bioavailable zinc with generally good tolerability.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor for 300+ metalloenzymes including superoxide dismutase and RNA polymerase
Essential for T-cell development and immune response
Required for collagen synthesis and epithelial integrity
evidence_notes: Zinc is an essential mineral with extensive RCT evidence for immune function, wound healing, and deficiency correction. Gluconate is a well-characterized, bioavailable form with established safety profile.

dosage.timing: Can be taken anytime; with or without food
dosage.with_food: Food may slightly reduce absorption but also reduces GI upset; consider individual tolerance
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use at recommended doses; RDI is 8-11 mg daily for adults
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/lactating women: 11-13 mg daily; older adults should not exceed 40 mg daily to avoid copper antagonism

contraindications: Do not exceed 40 mg/day long-term (risk of copper deficiency and neurological effects)
Use caution in patients with copper deficiency
Avoid within 2 hours of certain medications (quinolones, tetracyclines)

medications: ACE inhibitors may increase zinc excretion
supplements: High-dose copper (competitive absorption; zinc can impair copper status)

name: Zinc Orotate
summary: Zinc orotate is a chelated form of zinc bound to orotic acid, intended to enhance absorption and bioavailability compared to standard zinc salts. While zinc itself has strong evidence for immune function and wound healing, evidence specific to the orotate form is limited.
mechanism_of_action: Cofactor for 300+ enzymes including DNA polymerase and superoxide dismutase
Enhances skin barrier and wound repair
The orotate carrier may improve intestinal absorption and cellular uptake
evidence_notes: Zinc itself has strong evidence (Grade A) for immune support and deficiency correction. The orotate chelate form lacks direct RCT evidence compared to other zinc forms, but the underlying mineral has robust clinical support. Grade lowered to B due to limited form-specific data.

dosage.timing: Any time of day; consistent timing recommended
dosage.with_food: With food to minimize gastrointestinal upset; avoid with high-fiber or high-phytate meals which may reduce absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term use; monitor intake from all sources to avoid exceeding 40 mg/day upper limit
dosage.special_populations: Adult RDA: 8 mg (women), 11 mg (men). Pregnant/lactating women require 11–12 mg daily. Avoid excessive supplementation in older adults without medical supervision.

common_side_effects: Copper deficiency with chronic high-dose use (>50 mg/day)
contraindications: Do not exceed 40 mg/day long-term without medical monitoring
Caution in patients with copper deficiency history
Use cautiously with certain antibiotics or bisphosphonates

medications: Quinolone and tetracycline antibiotics (reduced absorption if taken together)
supplements: High-dose calcium, iron, or copper may compete for absorption
Phytates and tannins reduce zinc bioavailability

name: Zinc (as Zinc Oxide)
summary: Zinc oxide is an inorganic zinc form used in supplements for immune function and wound healing, though it has poor oral bioavailability and is primarily used topically. Oral zinc oxide is not an ideal form for systemic supplementation.
mechanism_of_action: essential for immune cell development and function
structural component of zinc fingers (transcription factors)
evidence_notes: Zinc's immune and wound-healing roles are well-established; however, oxide form has very poor oral bioavailability (~5-10%), limiting effectiveness as oral supplement.

dosage.typical_range: 8-11 mg daily (adult RDA); oxide form requires higher doses for equivalent absorption
dosage.timing: take 1-2 hours before or after meals for better absorption
dosage.with_food: avoid taking with food, phytates, or calcium which reduce absorption
dosage.duration_notes: long-term supplementation acceptable at RDA levels; acute high-dose therapy for specific conditions should be time-limited
dosage.special_populations: pregnant/nursing women: 11-13 mg daily; children: 5-11 mg daily (age-dependent); elderly at higher risk of deficiency

medications: tetracycline antibiotics (reduced antibiotic absorption, separate by 2+ hours)
fluoroquinolones (reduced antibiotic absorption, separate by 2+ hours)

dosage.special_populations: Athletes, vegetarians, elderly

name: Zinc (as Zinc Sulfate)
summary: Zinc sulfate is an inorganic zinc salt with moderate bioavailability, used in supplements for immune function, wound healing, and protein synthesis. Absorption is better on an empty stomach but may cause nausea.
evidence_notes: Zinc has good evidence for immune support and wound healing; sulfate form has moderate bioavailability compared to glycinate or picolinate.

dosage.timing: best absorbed on empty stomach, but may take with food if GI upset occurs
dosage.with_food: Food reduces absorption; take 1–2 hours apart from calcium, iron, magnesium
dosage.duration_notes: Long-term use >25 mg daily may impair copper absorption; avoid exceeding UL of 40 mg daily
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant/lactating women: 11–13 mg daily; vegetarians may need higher intake

contraindications: copper deficiency (zinc excess impairs copper absorption)

name: Zinc (as Zinc Sulphate)
summary: Zinc is an essential trace mineral critical for immune function, protein synthesis, and wound healing. Zinc sulphate is an inorganic salt form with moderate bioavailability.
mechanism_of_action: Component of immune cell development and function
Cofactor for zinc finger proteins and DNA transcription
Required for collagen synthesis and wound healing
Involved in taste and smell perception
evidence_notes: Zinc is well-established as essential with strong RCT evidence for immune support and cold duration reduction. Zinc sulphate has moderate bioavailability; chelated or amino acid forms may be better absorbed.

dosage.typical_range: 8-11 mg daily (adult women RDA: 8 mg; adult men RDA: 11 mg)
dosage.timing: Can be taken any time; consistent daily timing recommended
dosage.with_food: Take with food to improve absorption and reduce GI irritation
dosage.duration_notes: Safe for long-term daily use at RDA levels; extended high doses (>40 mg/day) require medical supervision
dosage.special_populations: Pregnant women: 11 mg daily; Lactating women: 12 mg daily; Plant-based dieters may need higher intake due to lower bioavailability

contraindications: Chronic high-dose use without copper supplementation

medications: ACE inhibitors: may increase zinc retention
supplements: Copper: high zinc doses inhibit copper absorption (monitor copper status)

name: ZumXR Delayed Release Caffeine
summary: ZumXR Delayed Release is a proprietary delayed-release caffeine formulation engineered to begin absorption after a set period, providing timed mental stimulation without an immediate jolt. It is useful for customizing caffeine timing throughout the day.
mechanism_of_action: Delayed-release matrix technology (likely enteric-coated or pH-sensitive)
Caffeine absorption begins after gastric passage
Adenosine receptor antagonism at specified time
evidence_notes: Delayed-release caffeine formulations are established; however, ZumXR-specific clinical data is limited. General delayed-release technology is well-documented for nutrient delivery.

dosage.timing: Morning (takes effect 1-2 hours post-ingestion) or early afternoon for afternoon boost
dosage.with_food: Typically taken with food for reliable delayed-release activation
dosage.duration_notes: Effects begin 1-2 hours after ingestion; sustained for 5-7 hours
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in evening; limit total daily caffeine to <400 mg

common_side_effects: Sleep disruption if taken late in day
contraindications: Severe GI conditions affecting absorption timing

supplements: Other delayed or extended-release caffeine products

name: ZumXR Extended Release Caffeine
summary: ZumXR Extended Release is a proprietary extended-release caffeine formulation designed to provide sustained energy and mental alertness over several hours. It mitigates the energy crash typical of immediate-release caffeine.
mechanism_of_action: Controlled-release caffeine absorption via proprietary matrix
Adenosine receptor antagonism over extended period
evidence_notes: Extended-release caffeine formulations have demonstrated efficacy in maintaining alertness without energy crashes; however, ZumXR-specific data is limited. General extended-release technology is well-established.

dosage.with_food: Can be taken with or without food; consistent timing optimizes absorption
dosage.duration_notes: Effects sustained for 6-8+ hours; reduces mid-afternoon energy crash
dosage.special_populations: Avoid in evening; limit total daily caffeine to <400 mg

supplements: Other extended or immediate-release caffeine products
